Latvia became the first NATO member to deploy acoustic detection along its entire eastern border, completing 87% coverage by September 2025 with €50 million allocated for UAS capabilities in 2026, a 150% budget increase from the prior year. The Eirshield multilayered defense system combines radars, cameras, RF detectors, and acoustic sensors with AI-automated decision-making, developed by DefSecIntel (Estonia) and Origin Robotics (Latvia).36
Germany leads the broader NATO "Drone Wall" initiative covering nearly 3,000 kilometers across six partner nations: Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Finland, Poland, and Norway. The upgraded ASUL system from Hensoldt integrates passive and active radar, electro-optical sensors, and countermeasures with AI-supported algorithms, incorporating combat experience from Ukraine. German Defense Ministry estimates full implementation requires 3-4 years.37
The European Union Drone Defence Initiative, separate from but coordinated with NATO efforts, aims for a continent-spanning detection network by 2027. EU planning acknowledges a 20-30% Ukrainian interception failure rate and prioritizes acoustic sensors for low-altitude detection, recognizing that radar alone cannot address threats flying at 310 mph at 330-660 feet altitude profiles.38
U.S. military interest in acoustic detection intensified following the surge in base incursions. NORTHCOM now serves as DoD's lead Counter-sUAS synchronizer for continental operations, testing acoustic systems at Falcon Peak exercises where Squarehead Discovair demonstrated the ability to "listen to everything at once, in all directions."7
The Defense Innovation Unit's Low-Cost Sensing Challenge (May 2025) specifically targeted acoustic among other modalities, seeking 50-80% total cost of ownership reduction versus current DoD systems. From 115 submissions, MatrixSpace Inc. won the $500,000 prize in December 2025, with acoustic sensor provider Squarehead among the 10 finalists. Follow-on Other Transaction agreements will expedite operational transition.39
The DoD has established an integrated distributed sensing network along the U.S.-Mexico border combining passive acoustic sensors with active radar for counter-UAS operations against cartel drones. This domestic deployment marks the transition of acoustic technology from overseas military theaters to homeland security applications, with a dedicated Army task force signaling institutional recognition that cartel drone capabilities have matured beyond the nuisance level.40
The U.S. Naval Postgraduate School demonstrated a passive AI-fusion C-UAS system integrating acoustic, electro-optical/infrared, RF, and low-probability-of-intercept radar specifically for maritime craft operating in signature-constrained environments. Acoustic sensors enable counter-drone detection without the electromagnetic emissions that would reveal vessel position in contested waters.5
December 2025's Bold Machina deployment integrated the system with Nvidia Jetson AI engines for edge processing. The emphasis on passivity reflects operational realities in special operations: active radar may provide better data, but the cost of revealing position can be mission-ending. Acoustic detection offers detection capability at zero emission cost, a trade-off increasingly favored for covert operations.
No single detection modality provides complete airspace awareness. The operational consensus is that robust C-UAS requires fusion of heterogeneous sensors where each compensates for others' weaknesses. Acoustic detection achieves its greatest value as a component of layered architectures, not as a standalone solution.
Sensor fusion can occur at multiple levels, each with different trade-offs. Data-level fusion (early fusion) combines raw data from all sensors into a massive input vector for central processing. This preserves maximum information but demands massive bandwidth and precise time synchronization between sensors operating on different physical principles.41
Feature-level fusion has each sensor extract features, such as acoustic embedding vectors or radar tracks, which are then combined. This reduces bandwidth requirements while preserving more information than higher-level approaches. Multi-modal Transformers that process combined feature streams represent the current research frontier.41
Decision-level fusion (late fusion) has each sensor make independent classifications, with a "manager" algorithm voting on results or applying confidence-weighted combination rules. This is the most common industry implementation due to modularity: sensors from different vendors can be integrated without sharing proprietary signal processing. The trade-off is potential loss of correlated information that would be visible at lower fusion levels.41
The pairing of acoustic sensors with optical cameras is particularly effective. Acoustic arrays provide omnidirectional coverage, detecting threats anywhere in the soundscape. However, they provide only direction (and sometimes rough range) without visual confirmation. Optical cameras provide identification but through a narrow field of view, the "soda straw" problem that makes scanning large airspace impractical.42
The standard operational model is slew-to-cue: acoustic detection calculates a bearing, then automatically slews a pan-tilt-zoom camera to that coordinate for visual confirmation and tracking. This solves both problems: acoustics provides the search capability, optics provides the identification. Research demonstrates this fusion maintains system accuracy above 96% even when individual sensors experience degraded performance.42
Dynamic weighting enables graceful degradation. In low-light conditions where optical confidence drops, the fusion engine weights acoustic input higher. In high-wind conditions where acoustic confidence drops, optical input receives greater weight. This adaptive approach maintains system performance across environmental variations that would defeat single-modality systems.
Radar and acoustics are physically orthogonal: radar detects dielectric mass and motion; acoustics detects aerodynamic disturbance. This orthogonality makes them complementary rather than redundant.3
Radar excels at detection and tracking but struggles with classification. The radar return from a quadcopter may be indistinguishable from a large bird. Acoustics excels at classification: propeller harmonics produce signatures distinct from wing beats. Fusing radar track data with acoustic classification drastically reduces false alarm rates from biological targets.3
Acoustics also fills specific radar gaps. Hovering drones may be filtered out by radar Moving Target Indicator (MTI) processing as static clutter. Low radar cross-section plastic drones may fall below detection thresholds. Acoustics provides an independent phenomenology that detects these targets through their lift-generation noise regardless of their radar characteristics.
Sensor Fusion: Role of Each Modality
| Modality | Primary Role in Fusion | Key Limitation Addressed by Others |
|---|---|---|
| Acoustic | Initial detection/cueing; RF-silent drone detection; classification | Limited range; no visual confirmation |
| Radar | Precise range/altitude/velocity; weather-resistant tracking | Bird confusion; urban clutter; active emissions |
| RF Detection | Protocol identification; operator location | Blind to autonomous drones |
| EO/IR | Visual identification; engagement confirmation | Narrow FOV; weather/lighting dependent |
Integration across different vendor systems requires standardized interfaces. The SAPIENT protocol (Sensing for Asset Protection with Integrated Electronic Networked Technology), adopted by UK MoD in 2019 and undergoing NATO ratification, provides this framework.43
SAPIENT defines an open architecture enabling autonomous sensors to make local decisions using edge AI while supporting network-wide correlation, association, and tracking. Testing with over 70 C-UAS systems at NATO TIE23 exercises validated interoperability across multinational systems. The protocol specifies message formats, data models, and functional interfaces that allow acoustic sensors from one nation to integrate with radar from another and command systems from a third.43
Standardization addresses a practical deployment problem: major airports currently deploy up to 17 different sensor types from multiple manufacturers, creating integration gaps that complicate threat assessment. The Terma Group's acquisition of OSL specifically targeted sensor fusion capability to address this fragmentation in European critical infrastructure protection.44
Acoustic detection operates under fundamental physics constraints that define its role in layered defense. Understanding these limitations is essential for realistic capability assessment and appropriate system employment.
Maximum effective detection ranges of 300-500 meters for typical commercial quadcopters represent a fundamental limitation compared to radar systems achieving 5-30 km. This disparity reflects physics: sound attenuates rapidly with distance while radar can be arbitrarily amplified with power.14
However, range claims require careful interpretation. Specialized systems demonstrate extended capabilities against larger, noisier targets. Microflown AVISA's SkySentry reportedly detects fixed-wing 2kg drones at 1 km and manned helicopters at 10 km. Ukraine's networks achieve multi-kilometer ranges against Shahed-type loitering munitions with their loud piston engines. Detection range scales with target acoustic output: quiet quadcopters at 200-300 meters, loud fixed-wing drones at several kilometers.6
Range specifications should always indicate the target platform and environmental conditions. A "5 km detection range" claim without these qualifications is marketing, not engineering.
Wind represents the most severe environmental challenge. Wind noise is broadband, high-energy, and chaotic. It mechanically saturates microphone diaphragms and masks target signals across wide frequency bands. Research benchmarks show that wind speeds exceeding 5 m/s create severe distortion, with upwind measurements dropping by 20 dB or more. High-quality windshields can lower the noise floor by 2-3 dB and extend detection range by over 30% in gusty conditions, but cannot eliminate wind effects entirely.16
Urban ambient noise creates different challenges. Traffic, HVAC systems, construction, and crowd noise produce background levels of 50-80 dB that can mask drone signatures entirely at ranges beyond 200-300 meters. Urban environments also create acoustic multipath from building reflections, complicating localization. These factors make urban deployment substantially more challenging than rural or semi-rural scenarios.14
Algorithmic mitigation is evolving. "Wind suppression" algorithms using Generative Adversarial Networks can learn the statistical texture of wind noise and subtract it, effectively denoising audio in real-time. Adaptive beamforming can null out stationary noise sources like nearby transformers. These techniques improve performance but cannot overcome fundamental physics: when wind noise exceeds drone signal by sufficient margin, detection becomes impossible regardless of algorithm sophistication.22
Environmental Impact on Detection
- Wind >5 m/s: Creates shadow zones; upwind detection drops >20 dB; fundamental limitation
- Urban Noise (50-80 dB): Masks signatures; reduces typical range below 300m
- Temperature Inversion: Actually enhances propagation by ducting sound; +3-4 dB possible
- Normal Temperature Gradient: Refracts sound upward; reduces range
- Precipitation: Increases ambient masking; wet surfaces amplify noise 6-9 dB
- Terrain Masking: Hills, buildings block line-of-sight; elevated sensors required
Classification accuracy depends critically on training data. Systems trained only on known drone signatures may fail completely against novel types. The rate of drone innovation, with new commercial models released monthly and military variants deliberately modified, creates a perpetual update challenge.27
CNN-based systems achieve reported false positive rates of 1.28% in controlled conditions, with Squarehead claiming "near-zero" through advanced noise suppression. However, operational false positive rates typically exceed test conditions due to environmental complexity. Primary false positive sources include birds (the dominant challenge for all detection modalities), helicopters, ground vehicles, and industrial machinery.26
Squarehead claims its propeller-sound-focused training recognizes "any drone, both current and future," suggesting generalization beyond specific signatures. Ukraine's experience validates rapid retraining capability: 97% accuracy maintained despite deliberate enemy signature modifications. However, this adaptability requires operational infrastructure for continuous model updates, not just initial deployment.17
Time from detection to actionable alert varies by architecture. Edge processing achieves millisecond latency for classification. Network-based processing adds transmission delays. Ukraine's Zvook achieves 12-second integration time to the Delta situational awareness system, fast by historical standards but potentially insufficient against fastest threats.6
The SAPIENT protocol emphasizes edge processing specifically to reduce latency by performing ML classification locally and transmitting only detections rather than raw data. This approach also reduces bandwidth requirements and communication signatures. For engagement decisions requiring sub-second response, cloud-based processing is generally inadequate.
Limitations as Design Constraints
Acoustic detection limitations are not failures to be overcome but physics constraints to be accommodated. System architecture must account for wind-driven detection gaps, urban range reduction, and signature library incompleteness. Realistic operational concepts deploy acoustic sensors as one layer among many, not as standalone solutions. Where acoustics fails, radar or EO/IR should be positioned to compensate. Where environmental conditions exceed acoustic capabilities, operators should understand that degradation is expected. The appropriate question is not "why doesn't acoustic detection work in all conditions?" but "what conditions maximize acoustic contribution to the overall system?"
As acoustic detection proliferates, adversaries are innovating to reduce signatures. This "stealth arms race" poses existential questions for acoustic C-UAS investment. Understanding emerging quiet drone technologies is essential for future-proofing detection architectures.
Israel's Aerosol G2 represents a paradigm shift in acoustic stealth. Independent testing by Galit Environmental Quality Company using professional NTI-Audio measurement systems verified an extraordinary 14.9 dB noise level at one kilometer. This is approximately 10-15 dB quieter than competing platforms like DJI Matrice 350 (25 dB at 1 km) across all distances.45
At 14.9 dB, the G2 operates below rural nighttime ambient noise levels (approximately 35 dB), making acoustic-only detection essentially impossible at operational ranges. The platform achieves this through optimized propeller geometry, tuned motor RPM ranges that avoid audible harmonic peaks, careful airframe damping, and mid-frequency optimization targeting the 250-1,250 Hz band where human hearing and detection algorithms are most sensitive.45
The G2's modest specifications (80-minute endurance, 11 km range, 1 kg payload, 12 m/s maximum speed) reflect inevitable trade-offs between noise reduction and performance. However, for ISR missions over contested areas, acoustic stealth may be worth these limitations. The platform demonstrates that properly designed aircraft can defeat acoustic detection entirely, forcing defenders toward more expensive radar-based alternatives.45
Quiet Drone Acoustic Signatures
| Platform | Noise at 1m | Noise at 1km | Design Philosophy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Israeli Aerosol G2 | 74.9 dB | 14.9 dB | Acoustic stealth optimized |
| DJI Matrice 350 | 85.1 dB | ~25 dB | Commercial, no stealth optimization |
| Thai KB-5E | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Electric for signature reduction |
| Shahed-136 | High (piston engine) | 5+ km detection | Range over stealth |
Thailand's KB-5E electric kamikaze drone, unveiled in December 2024 as Thailand's first indigenous loitering munition, demonstrates military adoption of acoustic stealth principles. The battery-electric design sacrifices range (150 km versus 500+ km for petrol variants) for dramatically reduced acoustic and thermal signatures compared to Shahed-type platforms using two-stroke piston engines.46
Electric propulsion provides inherent advantages over internal combustion. Brushless motors eliminate brush friction noise entirely. Energy is concentrated in the 0.8-1.6 kHz band, less intrusive than the concentrated harmonics of combustion engines. RPM can be tuned to avoid strong harmonic peaks. Reduced mechanical vibration decreases airframe noise transmission. The Royal Thai Air Force explicitly emphasizes "low acoustic and thermal signatures, enabling operations in contested environments where detection avoidance is critical."46
Commercial motor developments like ePropelled's Sparrow series specifically target defense applications with high-grade neodymium magnets, dynamically balanced rotors, and ceramic bearings that minimize mechanical noise. The commercial availability of these components democratizes quiet drone capability, enabling state and non-state actors to develop acoustically stealthy platforms without indigenous motor development programs.47
Propeller innovation compounds electric propulsion advantages. MIT's toroidal propeller design, which received the R&D 100 Award in 2022, eliminates tip vortices by closing the loop at blade tips. Tests show axial noise reductions of nearly 20 dB compared to conventional propellers. Critically, toroidal designs shift noise from discrete tonal peaks to broadband "whoosh," defeating detection algorithms trained on harmonic spike identification.48
"Break50Tip90" blade designs that unload blade tips achieve 74.6% reduction in perceived sound with 5.2% efficiency improvements. Serrated trailing edges inspired by owl feathers demonstrate up to 5.5 dB reduction, while serration-finlet combinations reach 20 dB reduction in computational simulations. These designs are transitioning from research to commercial availability.12
The implication for acoustic C-UAS is stark: detection algorithms looking for sharp harmonic spikes will fail against toroidal-equipped drones. Systems must evolve from "line detection" to "texture detection" that identifies broadband turbulent signatures. Current detection ranges are likely overstated against next-generation quiet drones, and red teams should begin testing against these platforms now.
Beyond platform design, tactical approaches degrade acoustic detection effectiveness. Terrain masking using mountains, valleys, and buildings blocks acoustic line-of-sight. Higher altitudes reduce perceived ground noise by approximately 2.8 dB per 10-meter altitude increase, potentially placing drones above detection thresholds. Urban environments with 65-75 dB ambient noise can mask drone signatures entirely.14
Swarm tactics present a different challenge: multiple simultaneous signatures can overwhelm classification algorithms designed for single-target detection. Coordinated timing can create acoustic confusion, and distributed attacks from multiple azimuths stress the tracking capabilities of systems designed for sequential threat engagement.
These tactical considerations reinforce the fusion imperative. Acoustic sensors may be defeated by quiet platforms, terrain masking, or environmental noise. Radar provides backup detection independent of acoustic phenomenology. The layered architecture ensures that defeating one modality does not provide free passage.
The Detection-Stealth Arms Race
The emergence of 14.9 dB platforms operating below ambient noise poses existential questions for acoustic detection. As quiet drone technology proliferates, detection systems must achieve sub-30 dB sensitivity for next-generation threats, requiring advanced filtering, multi-array triangulation, and real-time spectral analysis beyond current capabilities. Simultaneously, detection improvements drive further stealth innovation in a classic arms race dynamic. The strategic question is whether acoustic detection can evolve faster than signature reduction technology. If not, acoustic systems will transition from primary detection to supplementary verification roles within 3-5 years. Investment decisions should account for this trajectory.
Acoustics is not only a detection modality but an emerging effector domain. Novel approaches exploit the physical vulnerability of drone electronics to calibrated sound energy.
Fractal Antenna Systems unveiled the JERECHO acoustic array platform in November 2025, employing Acoustic Resonance Mitigation (ARM) technology patented in 2015. The system emits sonic, ultrasonic, and subsonic waves that induce vibrations and Prandtl layer instability causing flight control failure.49
The physical mechanism targets drone MEMS gyroscopes, which are vulnerable to acoustic interference. Propeller blades become "driven oscillators" transferring vibrations to inertial measurement units (IMUs) that cannot be practically isolated, particularly in smaller drones where mass constraints preclude damping systems. Demonstrated effects include smartphone compass interference and camera image blur/shake.49
JERECHO leverages fractal-based array architecture to shape and direct sound waves, extending operational range with wideband sonification. The company claims "pennies per use" operational costs, a dramatic contrast to kinetic effectors costing thousands of dollars per engagement. The system also avoids legal restrictions on RF jamming that constrain most electronic warfare approaches.50
Acoustic Soft-Kill: JERECHO Characteristics
- Mechanism: Acoustic Resonance Mitigation (ARM) targeting MEMS gyroscopes
- Effects: IMU vibration, compass interference, camera degradation, flight control failure
- Cost: "Pennies per use" versus thousands for kinetic effectors
- Legal Status: Avoids RF jamming restrictions; acoustic energy not regulated
- Limitations: Effectiveness against military-grade hardened systems unproven; range constraints
Acoustic soft-kill offers advantages in specific scenarios. Urban environments where kinetic intercept risks collateral damage from falling debris or missed rounds benefit from non-kinetic approaches. Sensitive facilities where explosive engagements are unacceptable (chemical plants, nuclear sites) require non-kinetic options. High-volume swarm attacks may exhaust kinetic magazines, making low-cost acoustic defeat economically attractive for attrition.50
The system provides a potential intermediate layer between detection and kinetic response. Rather than immediately launching interceptors or firing guns, operators could attempt acoustic defeat, reserving kinetic options for confirmed failures. This layered approach could improve cost-exchange ratios against mass drone attacks.
Acoustic soft-kill effectiveness against military-grade hardened systems remains unproven. Current demonstrations target commercial electronics designed without acoustic interference considerations. Military drones may incorporate vibration isolation, hardened IMUs, and redundant navigation that resist acoustic attack. Without testing against representative military platforms, effectiveness claims should be viewed with appropriate skepticism.49
Range constraints are significant. Sound attenuates rapidly, and directing sufficient energy at moving targets at useful engagement distances (hundreds of meters) presents substantial technical challenges. Line-of-sight requirements limit utility against low-flying or terrain-masked threats. Weather effects on acoustic propagation affect weapon delivery as much as detection.
The technology remains in early-stage development. Operational deployments will reveal whether acoustic soft-kill is a genuine capability or a niche tool for specific, constrained scenarios. Investment should balance the potential of low-cost defeat against uncertainty in effectiveness and applicability.
The acoustic C-UAS market is experiencing rapid growth driven by battlefield validation and institutional procurement decisions. Understanding the commercial landscape helps contextualize vendor claims and identify procurement opportunities.
Drone detection technology broadly is projected to grow from $659 million in 2024 to $2.32 billion by 2029, representing a 28.7% compound annual growth rate. The broader counter-UAS market is expected to reach $20.31 billion by 2030. Europe's anti-drone segment alone projects growth from $0.91 billion in 2025 to $4.63 billion by 2033. Acoustic sensors represent a growing component within this radar-dominated (40% market share) segmentation.51
The $500 million FEMA C-UAS Grant Program announced in December 2025 will drive U.S. domestic deployments, with FIFA World Cup 2026 protection a specific priority. This injection of federal funding into state and local procurement will accelerate adoption of integrated detection systems including acoustic components.52
Squarehead Technology (Norway) exemplifies the high-end, array-based approach with the Discovair G2+ system. Massive microphone arrays achieve high-precision direction finding with near-zero false positive rates. The system is ruggedized to MIL-STD-810H standards for extreme environments and integrates into broad C-UAS command systems like DroneShield's DroneSentry. Norwegian Armed Forces contract under Project P5007 validates military-grade procurement.17
Sorhea (France) commercializes the DAS concept with the LUMOR system, marketed for perimeter protection. The approach transforms kilometers of fiber-optic cable into continuous acoustic sensors, emphasizing long-range coverage with minimal maintenance requirements. The system targets border security, pipeline protection, and critical infrastructure applications where discrete sensor deployment is impractical.20
Dedrone (Germany/USA) operates as a comprehensive integrator. DedroneSens hardware includes acoustic sensors as part of multi-sensor suites, leveraging cloud-based machine learning for rapid signature updates. The company's subscription model enables continuous capability improvement through over-the-air model updates, addressing the signature library challenge through ongoing service rather than one-time deployment.53
DroneShield (Australia) offers the DroneSentry platform with UK National Protective Security Authority certification. The AI-enabled DroneSentry-C2 command-and-control engine provides modular sensor-effector integration, with SaaS-based architecture enabling scalability from single-site to networked defense architectures.54
Key Acoustic Detection Vendors
| Vendor | System | Approach | Key Differentiator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Squarehead (Norway) | Discovair G2+ | Dense microphone arrays | Military-grade; MIL-STD-810H; NATO validation |
| Sorhea (France) | LUMOR | Fiber-optic DAS | Perimeter coverage; minimal maintenance |
| Dedrone (Germany/USA) | DedroneSens | Multi-sensor integration | Cloud ML; subscription updates |
| DroneShield (Australia) | DroneSentry | Modular sensor-effector | SaaS C2; UK NPSA certified |
The lack of performance standards has complicated procurement, as vendor claims of detection range are meaningless without specifying drone type and environmental conditions. The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) is developing standards to standardize how acoustic C-UAS performance is measured and reported, adapting existing acoustic measurement standards (IEC 60318 series) for this application.55
NATO's Industrial Advisory Group (NIAG) is running innovation challenges to standardize interfaces for sensor fusion, ensuring that acoustic sensors from one nation can integrate with command systems from another. This interoperability is essential for coalition defense operations where multinational systems must cooperate seamlessly.2
Thales has announced plans for an acoustic network presentation in 2026. The EU Eastern Flank Watch aims for initial operational capability by late 2026 with full functionality by 2028. Lithuania will deploy Ukraine's Sky Fortress system in 2026, marking the first direct transfer of combat-proven Ukrainian technology to a NATO member. DIU integration of validated sensing technologies into the Replicator-2 mass deployment program will accelerate U.S. adoption.56
AI enhancement remains the primary capability improvement vector. Fraunhofer IDMT's German system demonstrates detection "outside line of sight" and "around corners" with 360° coverage updating every second. Data augmentation research shows significant ML classifier accuracy improvements through pitch shifting, time delays, harmonic distortion, and ambient noise integration during training.57
The fundamental challenge remains the quiet drone revolution. As platforms approach 50-55 dB at 100 meters, approaching library quiet levels, acoustic-only detection becomes unreliable across most operational environments. Detection systems must achieve sub-30 dB sensitivity against next-generation threats, requiring advances in filtering, multi-array triangulation, and spectral analysis beyond current capabilities.45
Acoustic soft-kill weapons represent an emerging effector category. Beyond Fractal's JERECHO, research continues into focused ultrasonic energy for IMU interference. If effective against hardened military systems, acoustic defeat could transform engagement economics with "pennies per use" costs against threats costing thousands of dollars.49
Maritime acoustic applications will expand. Research indicates heavy-lift drones create "evanescent pressure waves" detectable by near-surface hydrophones, potentially allowing submarines to detect aerial threats without surfacing. Hybrid buoys with both microphones and hydrophones address the air-water interface for coastal defense and naval force protection.58
The following developments will signal the trajectory of acoustic detection effectiveness:
Future Trajectory Indicators
- Quiet drone proliferation: Adoption rates of toroidal propellers and acoustic-optimized platforms
- Detection sensitivity advances: Commercial availability of sub-30 dB detection systems
- Soft-kill effectiveness: Test results against military-grade hardened platforms
- NATO standardization: SAPIENT protocol ratification timeline and adoption
- Ukrainian export programs: Sky Fortress and Zvook adoption by additional NATO members
- AI update cycles: Time required to adapt classifiers to novel signatures
- DAS infrastructure conversion: Telecommunications fiber repurposing for acoustic sensing
- Counter-acoustic countermeasures: Emergence of acoustic jamming or spoofing techniques
Assessment
Acoustic drone detection has proven its operational value from Ukrainian battlefields to NATO borders, delivering wide-area coverage at costs orders of magnitude below radar alternatives. Ukraine's entire national network cost less than $5 million yet tracks 20% of all airspace targets and helped achieve 95% interception rates in documented engagements. The technology fills specific critical gaps: detection of RF-silent autonomous drones, operation in GPS-denied environments, and passive surveillance without revealing defensive positions. These capabilities are not incremental improvements but categorically different from what radar and RF analysis provide.
However, acoustic detection operates under fundamental physics constraints that cannot be engineered away. Maximum ranges of 300-500 meters for small quadcopters, severe degradation in wind above 5 m/s, and vulnerability to the emerging generation of quiet drones like the 14.9 dB Aerosol G2 define clear boundaries. The technology is not a standalone solution but a critical layer in multi-sensor fusion architectures, cueing radar and optical systems to threats they would otherwise miss. Procurement decisions should account for integration requirements, environmental limitations, and the ongoing detection-stealth arms race that will define effectiveness trajectories over the next 3-5 years.
In the silent competition for airspace control, the ability to listen effectively determines who detects threats first. Acoustic sensors will not replace radar, but they will increasingly determine whether radar looks in the right direction. Ukraine has demonstrated what mass deployment of low-cost acoustic systems can achieve under fire. The strategic question for NATO members is not whether to deploy acoustic detection, but how quickly they can absorb the lessons already validated in combat.

results.title: A holistic review of acoustic detection for unmanned aerial vehicles | AIP ...
results.snippet: Dec 2, 2025 ... A parallel event occurred in 2021 when the Iraqi prime minister's residence fell victim to a UAV-based assault. UAVs are highly susceptible to ...
results.full_text:
The application of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) has gained considerable momentum in recent years, with a marked reduction in costs, attracting growing interest from diverse fields. Nevertheless, as their prevalence continues to rise, amateur UAVs operating recklessly at low altitudes have become a serious threat to public safety. As a result, an economically efficient preventive solution is urgently required to identify potential UAV-related threats at an early stage. Acoustic-based detection offers a cost-effective and easily deployable solution on a large scale while also addressing certain limitations of other technologies (such as radar, radio frequency, and visual systems), thus holding considerable research significance. In this work, we review the research developments in acoustic-based UAV detection, highlighting the involved key technologies of UAV acoustic signals, such as signal acquisition, blind source separation, feature extraction and recognition, and sound source localization. A wide range of technical methods and application strategies employed in existing research are thoroughly covered, with an organized analysis of the relevant technological framework, detailing its core principles and representative methods, and comparing their merits and limitations in addressing UAV detection tasks. In addition, the challenges and problems faced by acoustic-based UAV detection are examined, with a discussion of the development paths required to fulfill its anticipated future potential.
I. INTRODUCTION
Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), colloquially referred to as drones, possess the capability of remote manipulation within predetermined boundaries,1 with their genesis rooted in military applications.2 The progression of multiple advanced technologies in recent years has facilitated the evolution of UAVs into more operationally simplified and economically viable systems, resulting in their extensive utilization and growing interest across civilian and commercial sectors. Presently, the UAV sector is witnessing unprecedented growth, with its utilization extending to various fields: agricultural management (crop spraying, farmland surveillance, and automated seeding),3 logistics and transportation (last-mile delivery and traffic monitoring),4 emergency services (disaster relief, fire suppression, and law enforcement),5 media production (news coverage, live broadcasting, and cinematic photography),6 and critical infrastructure maintenance (power grid inspection, pipeline integrity assessment, and water resource monitoring).7 Despite their remarkable contributions to industrial productivity enhancement, UAVs have concurrently generated critical public safety risks, presenting formidable obstacles to effective oversight and preventive measures.
The exponential growth of UAVs in airspace over recent years has resulted in a surge of illegal drone activities, generating substantial apprehension.8 For example, the year 2019 witnessed substantial operational disturbances at airports across the United States, the United Kingdom, Ireland, and the United Arab Emirates due to unauthorized drone intrusions.9 A parallel event occurred in 2021 when the Iraqi prime minister’s residence fell victim to a UAV-based assault. UAVs are highly susceptible to exploitation for various criminal activities, primarily including unauthorized entry into restricted airspace, privacy violations, contraband smuggling, terrorist attacks, and espionage operations.10 These developments constitute a critical hazard to community safety, carrying profound impacts. Despite the implementation of comprehensive regulatory protocols by multiple countries to limit illegal UAV utilization, these initiatives still fall short of effectively countering intentional criminal conduct and terrorist operations. Consequently, regulatory authorities urgently require technologies capable of effectively detecting and tracking unauthorized UAVs,11 which has significantly propelled the advancement of drone detection systems.
Current UAV detection methodologies primarily encompass radar tracking surveillance,12 radio frequency (RF) monitoring,13 optical identification,14 and acoustic detection.15 Figure 1 provides a comprehensive summary of these technologies, each demonstrating distinct advantages and limitations. Although radar-based detection is commonly acknowledged as the most effective approach for remote, precise monitoring,16 its deployment entails significant costs and frequently lacks the capability to discriminate between amateur drones and ornithological entities. RF-based detection enables simultaneous localization of both UAVs and their operators;17 nevertheless, it is susceptible to interference from ground clutter in low-altitude environments and incapable of detecting autonomous drones.18 Optical-based detection renders visual recognition an exceptionally attractive methodology, offering capabilities in identification, tracking, and even payload inspection;19 however, it is highly susceptible to adverse weather conditions (e.g., dust, fog, and low light), obstructions, and UAV flight velocity.20 In contrast, acoustic-based detection is characterized as a cost-effective, all-weather technology with relatively high precision. Relative to the aforementioned methodologies, acoustic technology demonstrates resilience against challenging conditions such as speed fluctuations, adverse weather patterns, and electromagnetic noise, successfully mitigating the deficiencies of other detection technologies. In particular, within integrated detection systems employing multiple technologies, acoustic-based detection effectively addresses the coverage gaps present in alternative detection methods. Significantly, acoustic-based detection technology, operating as a passive sensing method, maintains complete non-interference with concurrent detection mechanisms and proximate communication infrastructures. Consequently, investigating and applying acoustic detection methodologies constitute an indispensable technical component in UAV regulation.
The acoustic-based UAV detection technology processes the acoustic signals captured by sensor arrays, distinguishing the distinctive features of UAV acoustic patterns from ambient noise,21 thereby enabling effective identification and spatial localization of UAVs. Generally, the methodology fundamentally manipulates audio signals and isolates discriminative features to optimize detection capabilities. Pioneering studies in this detection technology are predominantly centered around classical machine learning (ML) techniques, notably support vector machines (SVMs),22 K-nearest neighbors (KNNs),23 multilayer perceptrons (MLPs),24 and hidden Markov models (HMMs),25 establishing fundamental frameworks for recognizing UAVs. Yet, such techniques exhibit substantial performance limitations when confronted with sophisticated noise conditions and the demands of large-scale dataset training. With the rise of deep learning (DL) techniques over recent years, substantial advancements have been achieved in UAV acoustic detection, where deep learning architectures, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs),26 recurrent neural networks (RNNs),27 and convolutional recurrent neural networks (CRNNs),28 have exhibited exceptional complex pattern learning abilities, significantly boosting the system’s noise immunity. In addition, research efforts have concentrated on blind source separation (BSS)29 methodologies to address multi-UAV detection, effectively distinguishing between different drone signatures within mixed acoustic environments, which facilitates simultaneous target identification. Notably, incorporating sound source localization methodologies enables retrieving comprehensive spatial data30 regarding UAVs, leveraging time-of-arrival differences, phase disparities, and angle-of-arrival information to trace UAV trajectories in both two- and three-dimensional space.
Capitalizing on the technological merits of acoustic-based detection, relevant methodologies have emerged as a progressively vital component in drone detection systems, exhibiting broad potential for practical implementation. Extensive research has confirmed that acoustic-based detection approaches, through cross-domain integration with other technologies, offer enhanced UAV monitoring solutions. Employing multi-intelligent algorithms in UAV acoustic monitoring extends beyond mere presence identification, allowing for the recognition of specific drone types, flight trajectories, and behavioral assessment in controlled airspace. Deployment of lightweight, power-efficient acoustic array technologies facilitates large-scale implementation of acoustic-based UAV monitoring systems. Yet, as of now, in-depth summaries and investigations of acoustic-based UAV detection techniques remain scarce. The accelerated advancement of acoustic detection technology, coupled with the lack of systematic review and consolidation of numerous emerging theoretical approaches, has motivated us to initiate this detailed research endeavor. The key contributions of this work are outlined as follows:
A comprehensive review of the acoustic-based UAV detection technology framework is conducted, encompassing theoretical foundations, conceptual definitions, methodological approaches, and algorithmic equations.
Methodical categorization and comparison of diverse techniques in acoustic-based UAV detection are performed, spanning from signal acquisition and processing to feature extraction, deep learning architectures, blind source separation algorithms, and acoustic localization methods, covering both traditional approaches and emerging technologies.
An analysis of acoustic-based UAV detection applications is examined, accompanied by an exploration of prevailing challenges and promising future directions.
The overall layout of this work is depicted in Fig. 2. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Sec. II introduces the characteristics, acquisition methods, and datasets related to UAV acoustic signals. Section III consolidates UAV’s acoustic signal processing methods and feature extraction technologies. Section IV provides a detailed discussion of existing UAV acoustic recognition and detection technologies from the perspective of signal feature learning. Section V delves into the theoretical foundation of sound source localization techniques and their utilization in acoustic-based UAV detection. Section VI presents future directions and their challenges for acoustic-based UAV detection. This paper concludes with Sec. VII, offering a comprehensive summary of the research.
II. UAV’S ACOUSTIC SIGNAL ACQUISITION
A. Acoustic characteristics of UAVs
UAV acoustic properties mainly consist of low-frequency and high-frequency signal components, exhibiting sound pressure levels between 60 and 90 dB, characterized by broadband and narrowband spectral features, periodic modulation patterns, directional properties, and sensitivity to environmental conditions and aircraft configurations. These attributes are essential for drone detection, recognition, and system design. The dominant noise components in UAVs are generated by their rotating blades and electric motors.31 This is attributed to the fact that during aerial operation, the high-speed rotation of UAV propellers creates low-frequency aerodynamic noise, whereas motor functioning emits high-frequency electromagnetic noise. The superposition of noise components at different frequencies results in UAV acoustic signals exhibiting distinct harmonic characteristics.32 The noise spectrum consists of line spectra and their interconnecting components, where harmonic frequencies appear as distinct spectral lines. These acoustic signatures often display broadband features with substantial spectral information,33 a characteristic that enhances time-domain cross correlation localization techniques. Through appropriate signal processing approaches, mainly the Fourier transform and Mel-frequency cepstral coefficient analysis, UAV acoustic signatures are generally capable of being clearly differentiated from interference signals and ambient noise. Consequently, the unique characteristics of acoustic signals from UAVs make it possible to detect a range of multi-rotor and fixed-wing drones using acoustic signals and their corresponding processing methods. Furthermore, the UAV type, aircraft status, environmental conditions, and design materials are essential factors influencing its acoustic properties. These aspects offer a crucial foundation for detecting, localizing, and tracking multiple UAVs.
B. Acoustic sensors
Acoustic sensors, serving as crucial instruments for acquiring UAV acoustic signals, significantly influence algorithm selection, resource allocation,34 and detection performance in monitoring tasks. The selection of appropriate sensors adheres to a series of criteria, principally involving sensitivity, frequency response (FR), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), dynamic range, directivity, environmental durability, compatibility, size, shape, cost, and power consumption. In contemporary drone acoustic monitoring applications, sensors are typically classified according to their working mechanisms as capacitive,35 piezoelectric,36 fiber-optic,37 and micro-electro-mechanical system (MEMS)38 based acoustic detectors. Capacitive sensors operate by utilizing sound-induced vibrations on the eardrum, resulting in a change in the distance between the capacitor electrodes. This variation in polarization voltage is then used to convert the sound signal into an electrical signal. These sensors are characterized by high sensitivity, a broad frequency range, and low noise, with electret capacitive sensors being especially popular for various acoustic detection tasks due to their low cost. Piezoelectric acoustic sensors function by generating electrical signals from mechanical deformation. They are inexpensive, small in size, and require no external power, making them well-suited for long-term, unattended monitoring. However, their lower sensitivity and restricted high-frequency response (with significant signal attenuation above 10 kHz) limit their overall detection precision. Fiber-optic acoustic sensors rely on optical waveguide effects and acoustically induced light modulation for non-electrical sensing. Their primary benefits include long-range detection, high sensitivity, and strong resistance to interference, particularly in extreme electromagnetic environments such as those near substations or radar stations, making them highly suitable for drone surveillance. Although the cost of optical fibers is relatively low, the widespread civilian deployment of this technology is constrained by the expensive demodulation equipment required. MEMS is a micro-sized acoustic sensor produced through semiconductor fabrication techniques. Its cost-effectiveness in mass production and ease of integration make it particularly well-suited for embedded mobile monitoring systems or integrated monitoring networks, holding a crucial and irreplaceable role in drone detection.
Table I provides a comparative analysis of acoustic sensors with different operational principles in UAV detection scenarios, evaluating their performance across five key parameters: sensitivity, frequency range (FR), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), cost, and size. As can be observed, capacitive-based sensors possess excellent sensitivity characteristics, although with significant sensitivity to environmental noise, thus being ideal for UAV detection in low-noise scenarios. Conversely, piezoelectric-based sensors demonstrate superior performance for UAV detection in high-noise scenarios. Optical fiber-based sensors are capable of achieving high-accuracy remote monitoring for UAVs, enabling consistent data collection for prolonged durations. MEMS-based sensors exhibit distinctive miniaturization properties, rendering them especially appropriate for portable detection equipment and onboard drone surveillance applications.

C. Acoustic signal datasets of UAVs
UAV acoustic signal datasets typically encompass sound signatures generated during various flight states (e.g., hovering, ascending, descending, and level flight), collected through high-sensitivity microphone arrays, covering multiple drone models, flight altitudes, velocities, and environmental conditions (including indoor, outdoor, and scenarios with or without background noise). Commonly accessible, relevant datasets are primarily derived from publicly available databases developed through dedicated research efforts. As presented in Table II, these datasets, although limited in quantity, contain substantial UAV acoustic recordings accompanied by detailed labeling information (including drone types, flight states, timestamps, etc.), facilitating research and algorithm development for UAV detection, classification, and trajectory monitoring. For instance, according to references,39 a comprehensive publicly available dataset has been developed, allowing for the classification of various UAV audio signals and environmental sounds in multi-UAV scenarios, thus contributing to system-level decision-making and autonomy. The dataset in Ref. 40 employs advanced Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to generate large volumes of UAV audio data, providing a new solution to the challenge of data scarcity. The dataset mentioned in Ref. 41 offers a multimodal data collection that integrates both video and audio, enhancing detection reliability in complex environments. Reference 42 introduces a public dataset for drone sound source localization, filling the gap in existing datasets in terms of standardized sound source localization.
