In addition, some databases encompass sound signatures from different drone categories, such as multi-rotor and fixed-wing aircraft, combined with simulated environmental noise and disturbance signals in authentic settings, offering comprehensive data resources for scientific investigations.
D. Data augmentation
Current drone acoustic detection research faces challenges such as limited annotated data, uneven distribution, and scenario homogeneity. These constrained datasets hinder the deployment of learning algorithms and advanced classifiers that rely on large training datasets. Data augmentation offers a practical strategy to overcome this limitation by generating, modifying, synthesizing, or transforming existing data to increase dataset volume and heterogeneity, thereby providing data support to enhance the generalization capability and robustness of machine learning systems. For instance, Kümmritz45 significantly expanded the quantity and quality of original data by introducing techniques such as harmonic distortion, pitch shifting, temporal delay, and background noise mixing.
Building upon traditional data augmentation methods, deep learning-based generative models offer more powerful tools to address drone acoustic data scarcity. Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), Variational Autoencoder (VAE), and their variants are among these tools, while diffusion models—which have gained prominence in recent years—also play a key role. Collectively, these models fundamentally resolve data scarcity by learning the intrinsic distribution of raw data, enabling them to synthesize samples that are in feature space and indistinguishable from real data. GANs continuously refine the generator's capabilities through adversarial mechanisms, while VAEs, based on probabilistic graphical models, ensure sample diversity by learning the latent space of data and performing interpolation or sampling. Diffusion models, meanwhile, generate samples through an incremental denoising process and currently rank among the highest fidelity models in audio and image generation. As a result, researchers have already applied these generative models to augment drone audio data. For instance, Al-Emadi et al.46 employed GANs to generate realistic drone audio datasets, thereby enhancing the diversity of original datasets and significantly improving model classification performance.
III. ACOUSTIC SIGNAL PROCESSING AND FEATURE EXTRACTION FOR UAVS
The raw acoustic signal data captured from UAVs usually exhibit high complexity, often containing distortions and aliasing phenomena caused by data collection processes, which markedly elevate the challenges in subsequent drone categorization. Therefore, substantial research efforts in acoustic-based UAV detection have concentrated on signal processing and feature extraction strategies, targeting the accurate identification of critical information related to drone detection within complex acoustic patterns, consequently fostering the emergence of various signal processing techniques and their field applications. This section explores how to transform complex raw acoustic signals into efficient, learnable features through three key aspects: preprocessing, blind source separation, and feature extraction. It distills the rationale and steps of preprocessing to enhance the audio signal-to-noise ratio and separability. Subsequently, it introduces BSS modeling and representative algorithms to recover target sound sources from complex reverberation and multi-source interference. Finally, it compares the discriminative power, noise robustness, and computational overhead of commonly used time-domain and frequency-domain features, providing a robust signal processing framework and feature engineering foundation for subsequent drone acoustic classifier design.
A. Preprocessing
Within the realm of UAV acoustic detection, the preprocessing of signals represents a fundamental step, principally dedicated to extracting relevant features from unprocessed audio inputs and reducing noise interference to augment the accuracy of subsequent identification and classification tasks. The typical preprocessing workflow involves pre-emphasis, noise suppression, framing and windowing, filtering, normalization, and time–frequency domain representation,47 intended to eradicate environmental noise and intensify target signal attributes, thus converting the signals into more appropriate formats for analytical purposes. By implementing preprocessing, the differentiation capability of drone acoustic patterns is markedly enhanced, yielding high-quality input for ensuing signal transformation, feature extraction, and machine learning model development, thereby improving detection capabilities and system robustness.
1. Pre-emphasis
This technique enhances high-frequency components within acoustic signals, generally achieved through a first-order high-pass filtering mechanism. For UAV acoustic monitoring, the originally acquired signals exhibit energy concentration primarily in low-frequency bands, with limited high-frequency representation. Pre-emphasis application48 partially mitigates high-frequency signal loss, improving the quality of acoustic characteristic parameters.
2. Denoising
Target sound events in acoustic environments are often masked by ambient noise, resulting in compromised drone detection capabilities, a critical issue that must be addressed. Denoising plays an important role in drone acoustic detection, as it ensures that purer, more distinguishable features are extracted, thereby improving the performance of subsequent classification or recognition models. For example, for purer UAV acoustic signal acquisition, multiple studies49 have implemented BSS methods to segregate mixed acoustic inputs, successfully eliminating extraneous noise. Furthermore, a study50 has proposed a methodology based on the linear shrinkage-subspace projection-power spectral density filter (LSP) that employs covariance matrices and enhanced Wiener filtering, achieving effective noise suppression within a 135-m radius.
3. Framing and windowing
In real-world scenarios, acoustic signals often exhibit non-stationary behavior due to the dynamic changes in their statistical properties over time. As a result, the signal is typically divided into small, short-time frames to ensure approximate stationarity within each frame and facilitate better feature extraction. The boundary effects caused by segmentation, also known as truncation effects, are mitigated by applying window functions to the frames. This helps to smooth the signal edges, reduce spectral leakage, and improve the accuracy of frequency-domain analysis. Frequently used window functions include the rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, and Blackman windows.51
4. Filtering and normalization
Filtering and normalization are crucial preprocessing steps in UAV acoustic detection, aimed at improving the quality of audio features from the perspectives of frequency-domain denoising and time-domain amplitude normalization. The recorded UAV noise signals often contain substantial environmental noise and interference, which can disrupt feature extraction and recognition, thus reducing detection accuracy. On one hand, filtering techniques can design low-pass, high-pass, and bandpass filters based on different frequency response ranges, selectively preserving or suppressing signals from specific frequency bands to eliminate noise interference. To effectively cope with noise interference and non-stationary characteristics in complex signal environments, nonlinear system modeling is used, dynamically adjusting parameters for adaptive filtering.52 UAV noise signals also change significantly with varying distances from the UAV, with changes in signal strength causing noticeable feature variations. This leads to issues such as signal attenuation and feature distortion, significantly increasing the training data requirements. On the other hand, normalization focuses on adjusting the amplitude or energy distribution of signals to eliminate discrepancies in the numerical range of audio information. In UAV detection, this can effectively mitigate the impact of distance and enhance feature stability,53 including methods such as peak normalization, feature-level standardization, and dynamic response normalization.
B. Blind source separation (BSS)
Robust signal feature recognition in UAV acoustic detection faces substantial obstacles in field deployments because of intricate background noise interference patterns. Against this backdrop, the blind source separation (BSS) technique emerges as an innovative solution for enhancing the performance of acoustic-based UAV detection. This technique aims to recover constituent source signals from observed mixtures while not requiring prior knowledge of the mixing process.54 Such a framework consequently permits autonomous separation of UAV-specific acoustic patterns from complex noise backgrounds without environmental priors, substantially boosting the detection system’s signal feature identification reliability. It demonstrates sufficient resolution to discriminate between different drone sound profiles, facilitating simultaneous monitoring of multiple UAVs.
1. BSS fundamental principle
The fundamental objective of BSS is to recover the original source signals from observed mixtures without requiring prior knowledge of either the mixing process or the source characteristics.55 BSS research commenced with Cherry’s seminal 1953 cocktail party problem,56 illustrating the human auditory system’s remarkable capacity to segregate concurrent speech streams amid acoustic clutter. The architectural framework for applying BSS to acoustic-based UAV detection is visually summarized in Fig. 3.
The term “blind” in BSS implies no prior knowledge of source signals and mixing matrix, with the core objective being to estimate these elements solely from observed sensor signals.57 BSS models can be categorized into three distinct classes based on the relationship between observed signals (n) and sources (m): overdetermined (n > m), exactly determined (n = m), and underdetermined (n < m) scenarios. The aforementioned derivations are established under exactly determined conditions. For overdetermined scenarios, dimensionality reduction techniques such as whitening based on principal component analysis (PCA) or non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) are typically employed to achieve the required exactly determined configuration. For underdetermined scenarios, conventional approaches employ regularization techniques, sparse representation methods, and composite signal construction (observable signal synthesis) to address the inherent mathematical challenges.
2. Independent component analysis (ICA)
As one of the key technologies for blind source separation, independent component analysis (ICA) has found widespread applications in fields such as audio signal processing, medical signal processing, and image processing. Recently, it has shown promising results in drone acoustic detection, providing effective source separation and noise suppression, significantly enhancing the interference resistance of detection systems. Its application in this area is gaining increasing attention. The separation mechanism of ICA presupposes complete statistical independence among source signals,58 excluding all forms of cross correlation. Through appropriate mixing matrix formulation and objective function maximization, the algorithm achieves blind recovery of constituent signals through unsupervised optimization. Statistical independence serves as ICA’s fundamental postulate. Since independent signals exhibit zero statistical correlation, the central limit theorem dictates that their linear combinations typically demonstrate more excellent Gaussian characteristics than any individual source signal. Consequently, extracting signals with more substantial non-Gaussian characteristics facilitates the identification of independent source components.59 Common non-Gaussianity metrics include entropy, kurtosis, and skewness for quantitative evaluation.
ICA provides an effective speech signal separation approach, leading to its growing importance in acoustic-based UAV detection. Ghani and Khan60 modeled mixing non-speech, non-Gaussian, and independent sources. Building on ICA, they adapted the projection pursuit algorithm to the acoustic detection domain, revealing significant advantages in drone acoustic features and successfully recovering drone signals from mixed audio. This provides new strategies for drone monitoring in challenging acoustic environments. In another study, Uddin et al.61 proposed a time-varying UAV detection technique based on ICA. By combining ICA with a channel tracking mechanism, they effectively solved the signal separation problem in time-varying scenarios, achieved the detection of multiple UAVs, and improved the detection accuracy and adaptability of the system. Although ICA can separate simple noise mixtures, when assuming Gaussian-distributed background noise, the actual detection rate may drop significantly, positioning ICA as a more theoretical or simple-scenario solution.
3. Fast-ICA
Compared with ICA, Fast-ICA has a more streamlined algorithmic structure, eliminating the need for a learning rate, with the update step size being automatically determined. This overcomes the computational complexity and slow convergence rate found in ICA. Therefore, Fast-ICA is more adaptable to high-dimensional data or more complex models, making it particularly effective for UAV detection in complex acoustic environments.
Fast-ICA, known for its efficient computation, has become a widely adopted technique for separating various signal components in signal detection. Uddin et al.62 proposed an efficient machine learning method that combines Fast-ICA with a support vector machine (SVM) for spatial-domain acoustic-based UAV detection. After unmixing the audio signals, this method can detect various environmental sound signals and is also effective in detecting multiple UAVs, highlighting the potential of combining blind source separation with machine learning in UAV detection. Wang et al.29 introduced a UAV acoustic detection method based on a blind source separation framework, with Fast-ICA at its core, and combined it with principal component analysis (PCA) and variational mode decomposition (VMD) techniques. This method addresses the challenges of underdetermined, determined, and overdetermined situations in acoustic-based UAV detection, offering a more comprehensive solution.
4. Independent vector analysis (IVA)
IVA represents a relatively novel and promising approach to BSS, although notable research achievements in its application to acoustic-based UAV detection remain scarce. However, some progress in this area is still noteworthy. For example, Uddin et al.63 introduced an IVA-based method for acoustic-based UAV detection, designed to unmix signals recorded from multiple microphones. It enables simultaneous detection of several UAVs under heavy interference and has shown better performance than ICA in experimental evaluations. The main limitation, however, lies in its high computational cost. To tackle the bottlenecks of environmental robustness and processing speed, Kang et al.64 proposed a unified framework where IVA functions as the spatial model and deep neural networks serve as the source model, achieving real-time performance in mixed speech separation tasks. Scheibler65 developed an enhanced auxiliary function-based IVA (AuxIVA) approach, incorporating a calibrated iterative projection algorithm (IPA), to accelerate convergence in blind speech separation under reverberant conditions. To improve the efficiency of speech signal separation, Brendel and Kellermann66 proposed an accelerated IVA method that utilizes the majorize–minimize (MM) framework combined with eigenvalue decomposition. IVA has proven to be highly effective in signal separation tasks. As its performance continues to advance, it is likely to become a foundational technology for complex multi-UAV detection scenarios in future airspace monitoring systems.
C. Feature extraction
Feature extraction refers to the process of extracting features from acoustic signals, aiming to integrate prior knowledge of acoustics, sound perception, or specific attributes into the audio scene. This process results in a compact data representation that enables efficient sound scene analysis. Broadly speaking, as depicted in Fig. 4, UAV acoustic features can be classified into two categories: time-domain features and frequency-domain features.
1. Time-domain features
Time–frequency features are those extracted from the amplitude variations of an acoustic signal as it changes over time. These features capture both the instantaneous fluctuations in the signal and its statistical characteristics over short time intervals. In addition, these features are straightforward to compute and analyze and are extensively used in UAV audio analysis. Some of the most common time-domain features include
a. Short-time energy (STE).
Energy represents a quantitative measure of the time-varying amplitude characteristics of a speech signal. It quantifies the overall strength and intensity of the signal, offering insights into amplitude fluctuations and contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the audio signal and its features. Short-time energy67 is applied to short-time frames of the signal. It is beneficial for analyzing the dynamic changes of acoustic signals over time, especially for non-stationary signals such as UAV sounds. It is calculated by summing the squared values of the signal within a defined short-time window.
b. Root mean square (RMS).
RMS serves as a fundamental indicator for assessing the average power of an audio signal, capturing the energy distribution across the time domain.68 It is efficient in computing and effective in reducing the impact of non-periodic noise via squaring. However, it lacks the ability to differentiate harmonic signatures from UAV propellers and must be complemented with other features, such as spectral centroid, to enhance the classifier’s generalization.
c. Zero-crossing rate (ZCR).
ZCR measures the frequency at which a speech signal crosses the zero axis within a defined time period.69 It is determined by counting the number of polarity changes in the signal within a particular window. This feature is useful for distinguishing between different phonemes and is simple to compute, making it effective for UAV audio recognition tasks. However, its performance can be negatively affected by noise, resulting in relatively poor robustness.
d. Peak.
The peak reflects the maximum amplitude of an audio signal, representing the highest value observed within an audio segment.70 It provides insight into the signal's intensity and dynamic range and is crucial in audio processing to prevent distortion resulting from overly high signal levels.
2. Frequency-domain features
Frequency-domain features are derived from signals represented in the frequency domain, specifically from their spectral characteristics. Spectral representations, being two-dimensional visual displays, illustrate the strength distribution of various frequency components in signals, offering direct insight into the principal frequency features of acoustic waveforms. Frequency-domain features provide comprehensive spectral information that accurately reflects the acoustic characteristics of UAVs, leading to their widespread application. Frequently observed features consist of the following:
a. Short-time Fourier transform (STFT) spectrum.
b. Mel-spectrum.
c. Mel-frequency cepstral coefficient (MFCC).
As a prevalent audio feature representation, Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) consist of 12 frequency-related cepstral coefficients,73 offering an optimal balance between feature discriminability and representation compactness through adequate spectral channel information. MFCCs are obtained by applying a linear cosine transform (DCT) to the logarithmically scaled power spectrum on a nonlinear Mel-frequency scale, with the resulting coefficients collectively forming the Mel-frequency cepstrum. Key steps in MFCC feature extraction include analog-to-digital conversion, pre-emphasis processing, frame windowing, Fourier transformation, Mel-scale filtering, and discrete cosine transformation. By executing these procedures, MFCC features effectively capture essential spectral characteristics of speech signals while preserving relevant acoustic properties and eliminating redundancy.
d. Power spectral density (PSD).
As a fundamental metric in signal analysis, power spectral density (PSD) quantifies the frequency-domain power allocation of signals,74 elucidating the energy distribution pattern across spectral components. Serving as a pivotal characteristic in UAV acoustic detection, PSD proves highly effective in capturing low-frequency range propeller noise and associated harmonic features. This feature enables the identification of dominant frequency components in UAV acoustic signatures, facilitating both drone detection and classification.
e. Alternative features.
Existing research investigations have utilized linear predictive cepstral coefficients (LPCCs),75 gammatone frequency cepstral coefficients (GFCCs),76 spectral envelope features,42 and wavelet transform coefficients77 for UAV acoustic feature representation, exhibiting considerable efficacy in multiple operational scenarios.
In the context of UAV acoustic detection, frequency-based features demonstrate distinct advantages over time-domain features in identifying the signal’s periodic patterns and harmonic components, accentuating dominant frequencies and providing greater efficacy for UAV sound analysis. Yet, the computational demands of frequency-domain processing often exceed those of time–frequency techniques, compromising the representation of instantaneous signal changes and temporal localization. The synergistic integration of both feature domains is anticipated to offer optimized solutions for acoustic-based UAV detection applications.
IV. UAV’S ACOUSTIC SIGNAL FEATURE RECOGNITION
The unique acoustic signal produced by the rotating blades of a UAV exhibits a broad frequency spectrum and abundant harmonics,78 offering a distinct level of recognizability. Utilizing either traditional machine learning (ML) models or deep learning (DL) models to analyze features extracted from acoustic signals allows for the effective differentiation of UAV sounds from background noise or interference, leading to accurate UAV recognition. Below are some of the representative methodology frameworks.
A. Traditional machine learning-based methods
UAV recognition using traditional ML models for acoustic signal analysis has been demonstrated to be both efficient and accurate. Recently, conventional audio classification models such as support vector machine (SVM), K-nearest neighbor (KNN), multilayer perceptron (MLP), and hidden Markov model (HMM) have been increasingly used for UAV acoustic recognition, showing outstanding performance in various tasks.79
1. Support vector machine (SVM)
SVM is a supervised learning algorithm designed for classification and regression tasks.80 The core idea is to find an optimal hyperplane that maximizes the margin between different classes in the feature space. For linearly separable data, this involves solving a convex optimization problem to identify the hyperplane with the largest possible separation from the nearest data points of each class, called support vectors. In cases where data are not linearly separable, SVM employs the kernel trick (e.g., polynomial or radial basis function kernels) to implicitly map inputs into a higher-dimensional space where separation becomes feasible. SVM is robust to overfitting, especially in high-dimensional spaces, and excels in tasks such as text classification and image recognition by focusing on the most informative training examples (support vectors) while disregarding outliers. It has been extensively applied in pattern recognition problems, such as acoustic-based UAV detection. For instance, Yang et al.81 designed a multi-node acoustic UAV detection system that uses SVM to recognize STFT features of acoustic signals, accomplishing complete drone detection coverage with no blind spots within a 75-m range. Shi et al.82 designed an acoustics-based UAV monitoring system, applying SVM to classify acoustic signals from a single microphone. The system achieved a maximum detection error of 6.45% and real-time UAV localization within 100 m, demonstrating the advantages of SVM's rapid classification. However, SVM is highly sensitive to feature selection and kernel function parameters, and the computational cost increases significantly when processing large-scale datasets, which limits its application in scenarios with high automation requirements.
2. K-nearest neighbor (KNN)
KNN is a simple yet powerful instance-based learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks.83 The core principle is that similar data points tend to have similar outcomes. Given a new input, KNN identifies the K closest training examples (neighbors) in the feature space, typically using distance metrics such as Euclidean or Manhattan distance. For classification, the output is determined by majority voting among the K neighbors, while for regression, it is the average of their target values. KNN makes no assumptions about data distribution and is lazy learning (no explicit training phase), but its performance heavily depends on the choice of K, distance metric, and feature scaling. It excels in low-dimensional data but suffers from the curse of dimensionality as the feature space grows. It boasts advantages such as simplicity of principle, strong real-time update capability, and high scalability, demonstrating broad application prospects in acoustic-based UAV detection. For example, Akbal et al.84 propose an acoustics-based UAV classification model, utilizing Iterative Domain Component Analysis (INCA) to select features with higher informational content, followed by using KNN to achieve a classification accuracy of 99.72%. In addition, Uddin et al.62 introduce an efficient detection method based on ICA, which demixes the recorded audio to extract pure UAV sounds and employs KNN as a classifier, achieving a 99.1% accuracy in multi-UAV detection. Since KNN needs to traverse all training samples during each prediction, the computational overhead during the prediction phase is high, and an increase in feature dimensions severely affects the efficiency of distance calculation, known as the “dimension disaster,” which significantly affects its detection accuracy.
3. Multilayer perceptron (MLP)
MLP is a class of feedforward artificial neural networks composed of multiple layers of interconnected neurons.85 Its core principle involves hierarchical feature transformation: input data are processed through one or more hidden layers, where each neuron applies a weighted sum of its inputs, followed by a nonlinear activation function (e.g., ReLU or sigmoid) to introduce complexity and enable the network to learn nonlinear relationships. The final output layer produces predictions, typically using a softmax (for classification) or linear activation (for regression). Training relies on backpropagation, which adjusts weights via gradient descent to minimize a loss function by propagating errors backward through the network. MLPs serve as universal function approximators, capable of modeling intricate patterns in tasks such as image recognition, regression, and time-series forecasting. Despite challenges in depth and efficiency, its flexibility and generality still make it of significant value in acoustics-based UAV recognition tasks. For example, Tejera-Berengue et al.86 studied the impact of distance and environment on acoustics-based UAV detection, analyzing the recognition capabilities of five machine learning methods, including MLP, linear discriminant analysis, radial basis function network, SVM, and random forest, on UAV acoustic signals. The study confirmed the feasibility of using MLP for UAV recognition within a range of 200 m. Ahmed et al.87 proposed a low-cost, highly reliable UAV detection system based on machine learning and, in a dataset containing seven different UAV sounds and environmental signals, demonstrated that MLP can effectively recognize the complex nonlinear acoustic features of UAVs. However, the training process of MLP is prone to getting stuck in local optima. It is susceptible to the selection of hyperparameters such as initial weights and learning rates, requiring extensive tuning work.
4. Hidden Markov model (HMM)
HMM is a probabilistic graphical model used for modeling sequential data.88 It assumes that the system consists of a sequence of unobservable hidden states and associated observable emissions. The core idea is that the hidden states follow the Markov property (the current state depends only on the previous state), with transition probabilities defined by a state transition matrix, while each hidden state generates an observation according to an emission probability matrix. A key feature of HMMs is that the states are not directly observable and must be inferred from the observed sequence. HMMs are widely used in tasks such as speech recognition, natural language processing, and biological sequence analysis, with classic algorithms including the forward–backward algorithm, Viterbi decoding, and Baum–Welch parameter learning. HMM, as a powerful tool for executing various audio recognition tasks, offers unique advantages in acoustic-based UAV detection. For instance, Guo et al.89 proposed an acoustic-based system for detecting, classifying, and tracking illegal drones using a circular microphone array and beamforming techniques. Their system employs switched beamforming for initial scanning, an HMM for sound classification, and adaptive recursive least squares (RLS) beamforming for tracking. Simulations demonstrate high accuracy in both ideal and noisy environments, with effective performance even in the presence of interference. In response to the potential threat posed by small uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) in security-sensitive areas, Shi et al.90 designed a UAV sound recognition method based on MFCC feature extraction and HMM classification, which can capture the temporal characteristics of UAV sounds even in noisy environments. The limitation of HMM lies in its strict Markov assumption (the current state only depends on the previous state), which may not fully capture the complex distance dependencies in drone acoustic signals and may be affected when dealing with complex background noise and multi-source reverberation.
B. Deep learning-based methods
Deep learning has garnered significant attention in the recognition of UAV acoustic signals in recent years. Due to its sophisticated learning mechanisms and robust inference capabilities, it is capable of uncovering more detailed feature information from large datasets of acoustic signals, often yielding superior performance compared to traditional methods, particularly in multi-source acoustic scenarios. The deep learning approaches currently emerging in drone acoustic detection tasks mainly revolve around two core technologies: CNNs and RNNs.
1. Convolutional neural network (CNN)
CNNs are a specialized type of deep neural network known for their ability to extract local features, especially effective in handling data with grid-like structures such as time series or image data. Hence, they are highly suitable for processing acoustic signals and analyzing spectral features. Leveraging CNNs to understand the intricate patterns of UAV acoustic signals facilitates high-precision detection and classification of UAVs.91 This has significantly advanced the field of acoustic-based UAV detection, where typical CNNs and their variants, such as Residual Network (ResNet)92 and Visual Geometry Group (VGG),93 have demonstrated successful implementations.
a. Fundamental principles of CNN.
CNNs enable end-to-end processing, extracting features directly from the time–frequency spectrum of raw acoustic signals, and are exceptionally proficient at uncovering local frequency domain features. In addition, by integrating multi-scale features through deep networks, noise resilience is enhanced and can be expanded further through the integration of transfer learning. Currently, this method represents the prevailing deep learning approach for acoustic-based UAV detection, resulting in numerous successful applications. For example, Jiao et al.95 proposed a lightweight CNN named ADS-CNN for recognizing the flight attitude of quadrotor UAVs based on audio features. By fusing MFCC and STFT features extracted from a self-recorded UAV audio dataset and integrating an attention mechanism and depthwise separable convolutions, the method achieves high recognition accuracy with reduced model complexity. Experimental results show that ADS-CNN outperforms traditional models in both accuracy and efficiency. Agarwal et al.96 developed an acoustic-based drone identification system using a CNN model trained on spectrograms of drone audio. The model achieved 95.83% accuracy in distinguishing drone sounds from environmental noise, demonstrating effectiveness in detecting drones via CNN for their acoustic signatures.
These studies have also revealed some drawbacks of CNNs, including high computational demands, extensive data annotation requirements, limited interpretability, and weak generalization capabilities.
b. Residual network (ResNet).
ResNet allows gradients to be directly backpropagated through residual connections, thereby increasing network depth and strengthening deep feature extraction capabilities and noise resistance. The convergence speed and resistance to overfitting have also improved. It has attracted attention in some drone acoustic detection studies. For example, Lei et al.98 proposed a two-stage UAV noise removal method based on a deep ResNet to improve speech intelligibility in UAV noise environments. They used a ResNet with four residual blocks to estimate the spectral gain function and applied it to noisy speech, effectively removing drone noise and improving speech quality. Liu et al.99 designed an improved lightweight ResNet deep learning model to address the recognition challenges caused by the spectral overlap of drone sounds and background noise in environments with low SNR. This model reduces the number of layers and integrates channel and spatial attention mechanisms, significantly improving feature extraction performance under low SNR conditions while reducing the model’s parameters.
However, the ResNet series models discussed in these studies still have limitations, including a large number of parameters, high computational costs, and insufficient sensitivity to dynamic temporal features.
c. Visual geometry group (VGG).
VGG is also a variant of CNN, which adopts a modular design by combining convolutional layers, activation functions, and pooling layers into standardized VGG blocks and stacking them repeatedly.100 This allows for flexible adjustments in network depth and width. VGG increases the network depth while maintaining the receptive field, making it highly suitable for progressively extracting high-frequency harmonics and temporal continuity from time–frequency data, thereby capturing more complex data patterns. VGG exclusively uses small convolutional kernels, making it simple to extend and replicate. It is frequently pre-trained to establish weights, which are then transferred to a wide range of tasks to serve as a feature extractor. Nonetheless, due to the large size of its network and high computational costs, it necessitates substantial data and computational resources to complete the training process. VGG has significant potential in acoustic feature extraction and transfer learning for UAV detection, particularly suited for fine feature mining and multi-scale analysis in medium- to small-scale datasets, resulting in several representative application outcomes. For instance, Anidjar et al.101 proposed a real-time acoustic anomaly detection framework for UAVs, combining a Transformer-based model with a compressed VGG-inspired CNN to analyze drone-emitted sounds. Their method addressed reliability challenges in UAVs by offering a low-cost, microphone-based solution for real-time fault detection in noisy environments. El-Latif et al.102 presented a novel deep learning-based approach that integrates Mel-spectrogram analysis and the VGGish transfer learning model to accurately detect drone payload weights from acoustic signals, achieving an impressive accuracy of 99.98%.
Despite its potential, VGG is constrained by computational cost and network depth, which may cause it to underperform compared to ResNet in real-time or highly detailed classification tasks. In real-world applications, a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency must be considered, along with the characteristics of the data.
2. Recurrent neural network (RNN)
RNNs are effective for processing sequential acoustic data in UAV detection due to their ability to model temporal dependencies. RNN-based systems can operate in real-time, making them suitable for dynamic environments.46 By analyzing acoustic patterns over time, RNNs can distinguish drone noise from background interference. Unlike traditional methods, RNNs automatically learn features from raw audio signals, improving detection accuracy. RNN variants, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)103 and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU),104 address the issue of vanishing gradients, enabling robust modeling of long-range dependencies. Applications include surveillance, unauthorized drone identification, and collision avoidance.
a. Fundamental principles of RNN.
RNNs are effective for acoustic-based UAV detection due to their ability to model temporal dependencies in sound sequences, enabling robust noise filtering and real-time processing. For example, to address the threat of malicious use of commercial amateur drones, Jeon et al.106 proposed a detection method based on RNN and verified its applicability in real-time detection systems using a real-world dataset. Kim et al.107 designed a distributed drone detection system and compared the detection performance of three deep learning models—RNN, CNN, and Dense—on MFCC and Mel spectrogram features. RNN was proven to be the optimal network, achieving an accuracy rate as high as 96%. However, RNNs face challenges such as high computational costs, vanishing gradients in long sequences, and sensitivity to environmental variability in training data.
b. Long short-term memory network (LSTM).
With its powerful sequence modeling capabilities, LSTM can capture complex temporal patterns and contextual information, thereby improving model performance and enabling it to perform well in a variety of drone detection tasks. For example, Utebayeva et al.109 first attempted to use a stacked bidirectional LSTM network with two hidden layers for drone acoustic detection, aiming to achieve a multi-label classification detection system. The experimental results show that the model can classify various drone sounds with a high accuracy of 94.02%. Li et al.110 addressed drone intrusion events by combining LSTM, CNN, and self-attention mechanisms in a deep learning framework, effectively distinguishing drone sounds from wind events with 100% accuracy. Even in complex multi-source threat environments, the system maintained a high recognition accuracy rate of 96.25%. However, LSTM also has some inherent challenges. It cannot eliminate gradient issues, especially when dealing with extremely long sequences, and its complex internal structure significantly increases training costs.
c. Gated recurrent unit (GRU).
The GRU reset gate can help the model weaken unimportant or potentially noisy information, enabling it to focus more on key features in the signal. This is particularly important for drone acoustic detection in complex acoustic environments, making it an efficient and popular choice. For example, Utebayeva et al.27 conducted an in-depth study of various drone sound recognition systems based on recurrent neural network architectures, where GRU achieved the best performance. Notably, they also focused explicitly on drone payload conditions, achieving an accuracy rate of up to 98% for payload status classification across multiple categories, including loading, unloading, and background noise. Another study43 successfully validated the feasibility of determining the discrete distance range of UAVs using sound signals. Leveraging GRU's robust temporal modeling capabilities, the system achieved classification accuracy rates of 94% and 98% for UAV sounds classified at intervals of 10 and 15 m, respectively, within a range of 5–50 m. However, GRU is more sensitive to parameter tuning, such as learning rate, due to its compact structure, and it still faces challenges, such as limited context scope constrained by the depth of the recurrent layers and relatively poor interpretability.
3. Other deep learning methods
In addition to pure CNNs and RNNs, various innovative deep learning methods have emerged in the field of drone acoustic detection. These methods enhance detection performance by integrating the advantages of different architectures or introducing new mechanisms, greatly enriching the technical means of drone acoustic detection and promoting the development of this field toward greater efficiency and robustness.
a. Convolutional recurrent neural network (CRNN).
CRNN112 is a hybrid deep learning framework that cleverly combines the advantages of CNN in spatial feature extraction and the capabilities of RNN in sequence modeling. This collaborative approach enables CRNN to demonstrate unique performance in processing time-series audio data with local features, achieving outstanding results in drone acoustic detection tasks. For example, Kashyap et al.113 proposed an innovative deep learning architecture based on CRNN, achieving a detection accuracy of up to 99.30% for small drones within a 70-m airspace control range, highlighting the significant potential of CRNN in this field. Yang et al.114 utilized a CRNN network to address the dynamic evolution of drone acoustic features (fundamental frequency and harmonic structure). This approach inherits the strong ability of CNNs to extract local features in image-like spectra while effectively capturing the dynamic patterns of drone audio using RNNs, thereby achieving a comprehensive understanding of the spatiotemporal dynamics of drone acoustic signals. However, the high demand for data volume and limitations in handling ultra-long sequences and interpretability are challenges that CRNN must address in practical applications.
b. Concurrent neural network (CoNN).
CoNN115 is a parallel neural network architecture that has demonstrated unique potential in drone acoustic detection. Its core idea is to enhance the network's feature learning capabilities and information integration efficiency through parallelization and multipath processing. This means that multiple independent computing units within the network simultaneously process different parts or aspects of the input data. This design philosophy endows it with robust flexibility and scalability, enabling it to handle complex, multi-dimensional audio data rich in contextual information with distinct advantages. It has been successfully applied in drone acoustic detection tasks. For example, Dumitrescu et al.51 developed a drone acoustic detection system based on the CoNN architecture, combining three different types of competitive networks: a multilayer perceptron (MLP), a time-delay neural network (TDNN), and a self-organizing map (SOM) mapping. Independent training is used to solve different subproblems. From the experimental results, CoNN exhibits better generalization ability and faster convergence, resulting in higher accuracy. The theoretical and experimental results prove their feasibility and provide new ideas for solving complex audio signals. It is worth noting that the parallel capability of CoNN makes it very suitable for detecting and tracking multiple drone targets. However, the high computational overhead and design complexity remain challenges and are also the key to its development. In particular, CoNN subnetwork parallelization will significantly increase inference memory on the one hand and complicate architecture threshold and weight tuning on the other.
c. Transformers.
Through the self-attention mechanism, Transformers can capture complex time–frequency dependencies in audio signals, which is advantageous for drone detection in time-varying and complex acoustic environments. For example, Anidjar et al.101 introduced the Transformer architecture into drone acoustic anomaly detection to handle audio recognition in more challenging environments. By leveraging Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) pre-training, they significantly reduced the requirement for drone acoustic data, demonstrating the feasibility of Transformers for small-sample, edge-side drone acoustic detection. Berg et al.117 compared the classification performance of a fine-tuned Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) architecture and a custom CNN in a small-sample scenario of 4500 s and nine drone classes. The results showed that although AST had slightly lower overall accuracy than CNN, its accuracy increased with the scale of the data, demonstrating scalability with the potential to outperform CNN as the data grow. Transformers have demonstrated their effectiveness in multiple detection tasks. However, they still face challenges such as high computational resources and data requirements, high computational overhead for long sequences, and a lack of local inductive bias.
C. Comparison of various learning methods in acoustic-based UAV detection
1. General detection performance evaluation metrics
In addition to the above classification performance indicators, drone acoustic detection may also involve important indicators such as detection distance, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and processing time to evaluate the practicality and efficiency of the system comprehensively.
2. Comparison of traditional machine learning methods
Traditional machine learning methods are mostly simple in principle and played an important role in early drone acoustic detection research, demonstrating different characteristics. SVM is a typical binary classification model that introduces kernel functions to map the original feature space to a high-dimensional space, enabling the handling of nonlinear classification problems in complex drone audio. However, SVM is highly sensitive to parameter settings and kernel function selection, requiring careful tuning. KNN is a non-parametric supervised learning algorithm with an intuitive algorithm concept and natural support for multi-classification problems, without the need for additional combination strategies such as SVM. However, it is a “lazy learning” algorithm that requires traversing all training samples for each prediction, resulting in high computational overhead during the prediction phase. MLP is the most basic feedforward neural network, which can capture complex patterns in drone sounds through multiple layers of nonlinear transformations and has a structure that is easy to integrate into existing deep learning frameworks. However, in practical applications, it still faces challenges such as overfitting, long training times, and poor interpretability. HMM is a statistical model particularly suited for processing time-series data such as drone audio, identifying the most likely sequence of hidden states that generated the sequence to capture dynamic characteristics and temporal dependencies. This is crucial for detecting time-varying drone sounds. However, its heavy reliance on independence assumptions limits its discriminative ability during detection.
In conclusion, traditional machine learning methods are generally simple in principle and have strong interpretability. Although they perform less effectively than deep learning models in processing complex and dynamic drone acoustic signals, they are not entirely without value. In particular, in scenarios with resource constraints, limited data, or high requirements for model interpretability, they can still leverage their unique advantages and collaborate with deep learning methods to enhance the performance and practicality of detection systems.
3. Comparison of deep learning methods
In recent years, with the rapid development of deep learning, it has become the core driving force in the field of drone acoustic detection. Among these, CNN-based structures excel at capturing local spatiotemporal features, making them suitable for drone sound recognition based on acoustic spectrum maps, particularly in feature extraction and classification tasks. Its variant, ResNet, effectively mitigates the gradient vanishing problem in deep networks by introducing residual connections. VGG, with its stacked structure, enhances the network's nonlinear expression capabilities while maintaining feature extraction depth. However, these architectures have certain limitations in capturing time-dependent features, computational resource requirements, and reliance on large-scale datasets. Given the inherent temporal characteristics of acoustic signals, RNNs and their variants (such as LSTM and GRU) have played a crucial role in drone acoustic detection, particularly in processing audio temporal information and long-range dependencies, making them highly suitable for scenarios requiring the capture of dynamic changes in drone sounds. However, the inherent sequence processing mechanisms of such models limit their parallel computing capabilities, resulting in relatively low training efficiency, especially when handling ultra-long sequences. To fuse the advantages of different networks and overcome their limitations, hybrid neural networks have emerged. For example, CRNN combines the advantages of CNN and RNN to fully capture the spatial and temporal dependency information of drone sounds, demonstrating excellent performance in various acoustic detection tasks. CoNN aims to improve model efficiency and performance through parallel processing and information fusion. Although its research in the field of drone acoustic detection is relatively limited, its potential for multi-scale feature processing and multi-sensor fusion deserves further exploration.
In addition, the introduction of the emerging Transformer architecture has brought breakthroughs to drone acoustic detection. Its parallel computing capabilities and exceptional ability to capture long-range dependencies make it highly promising for processing large-scale acoustic data and complex scenarios. However, the large number of parameters and computational requirements limit its direct deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Overall, various deep learning models have their strengths in drone acoustic detection, and the choice of an appropriate model depends on the specific application scenario, data characteristics, and requirements for computational resources and real-time performance. In the future, with the development of drone edge computing and embedded devices, developing lightweight, high-performance deep learning models will become an important trend. Given the lack of drone sound datasets, researching meta-learning, contrastive learning, and generative adversarial networks to utilize limited datasets efficiently is expected to play a key role in this field. In addition, efforts to enhance model transparency and interpretability and to understand the decision-making processes of deep networks will help optimize models and improve their robustness in complex real-world environments.
4. Benchmark-based comparison of detection performance for various learning methods
To evaluate various methods in drone acoustics, we conducted drone acoustic detection experiments on a public dataset,40 which contains two categories of audio samples: drone and non-drone, with a total duration of 1332 s for each category. All audio files are in WAV format, with a sampling rate of 16 kHz and a bitrate of 16 kbps. UAV audio was obtained by recording flight and hovering sounds and overlaying real background noise. In contrast, non-UAV audio includes various noise types such as urban noise, helicopters, airplanes, birds, thunderstorms, storms, and rain, with a signal-to-noise ratio ranging from −32 to −3 dB. Each sample is 1 s long, and the dataset consists of 2664 samples, which are divided into a training set (2131 samples) and a test set (533 samples) in an 8:2 ratio. The performance metrics considered include accuracy (Acc), F1 score (F1), precision (P), recall (R), and test time. The experimental setup utilized an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4090 Ti graphics card, a 3 GHz Intel CPU, and 256 GB of memory. The network parameters were configured as follows: epoch = 30, batch size = 16, and learning rate = 0.000 01.
Table III presents the detection results for various networks. Among the ML methods, MLP achieved the best performance using nonlinear mapping (Acc 97.87% and F1 90.20%), followed by SVM and KNN. HMM performed significantly worse than other methods (Acc 78.96 and F1 78.50), which may be due to its limitation under the Gaussian assumption, making it less capable of capturing the complex harmonic and time-varying characteristics of drone acoustic signals. It is worth noting that these algorithms were tested within less than 1 s, with extremely low computational overhead, making them suitable for resource-constrained early warning or rapid prototyping scenarios. Among DL models, CNN achieved a significant leap in performance with its robust feature learning capabilities, improving accuracy to 94.56%. Its variants, ResNet and VGGish, further improved feature learning efficiency and effectiveness through deeper network structures and specific designs. Compared to other DL methods, the baseline RNN performs poorly with an accuracy rate of only 88.76%. This may be due to its unstable training and susceptibility to gradient vanishing, resulting in insufficient expressive power and noise robustness. LSTM and GRU effectively address the long-range dependency issue of RNN through gating mechanisms, achieving significant performance improvements (LSTM Acc 95.23% and GRU Acc 94.89%). CRNN and CNN-LSTM further integrate convolutional and recurrent structures, achieving the best performance, with CNN-LSTM reaching the highest performance in this experiment (Acc 96.87% and F1 96.88%). Transformer models leverage their self-attention mechanism to focus on key information in drone sounds, achieving performance on par with fusion-based models and demonstrating strong potential for handling global information and long-range dependencies. However, their testing time is the longest among all models, reaching 6.84 s.

However, while improving detection performance, they also increase computational complexity, resulting in significantly longer testing times compared to ML-based methods. To achieve broader and more reliable practical applications, future research should go beyond the pursuit of high accuracy and instead focus on model lightweighting and efficient deployment, robustness enhancement in complex environments, model interpretability, multimodal fusion, and collaborative perception.
V. ACOUSTIC-BASED UAV LOCALIZATION
The fundamental principle of UAV acoustic localization involves estimating the direction-of-arrival (DOA)119 through advanced signal processing techniques that analyze sound wave propagation characteristics across sensor arrays, subsequently determining the spatial coordinates via geometric triangulation. Acoustic source localization techniques can be systematically categorized into three principal methodologies based on their computational foundations: time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA) estimation,120 beamforming-based approaches,121 and high-resolution spectral analysis techniques.122 Table IV summarizes the existing studies on UAV acoustic source localization.

A. Array signal model
As illustrated in Figs. 8(a) and 8(b), for short-range sound sources, the near-field model assumes spherical wavefronts impinging on the sensor array. It considers both the amplitude and phase differences of the sound waves, thereby offering a more accurate approximation of real acoustic behavior. Conversely, the far-field model assumes that the sound source is far from the array, leading to minimal differences in the angles of incidence. As a result, signal phase information is typically ignored, and wave propagation is modeled using plane waves. The variation in incident angles at the array elements between near-field and far-field conditions makes selecting a suitable model crucial for accurate localization. Typically, UAV monitoring environments meet the criteria for the far-field model.
B. Time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA) localization technology
TDOA acoustic source localization relies on the precise synchronization of multiple sensors to locate UAVs, and a variety of significant applications have emerged in recent years. For instance, Shi et al.82 developed a TDOA algorithm based on a tetrahedral microphone array, utilizing a Bayesian filter and a Gaussian mixture model to effectively address multipath interference, achieving a detection range greater than 100 m in real-world settings. Fang et al.34 investigated a UAV detection system based on optical fiber acoustic sensors, integrating distributed acoustic sensing (DAS) technology with the TDOA localization method. By exploiting the time differences across various optical fiber sensing points, the system enables real-time 3D positioning and precise 3D tracking of the UAV. Accurate time delay estimation algorithms are key to achieving high-precision positioning. Liu et al.128 focus on improving time delay estimation accuracy in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) environments. They utilize a novel weighting function that combines the characteristics of the smoothing coherence transform (SCOT) and phase-hopping transform (PHAT), enabling the algorithm to achieve more accurate time delay estimates under low SNR conditions, with drone sound source localization errors not exceeding 22.1 cm. Lim et al.127 propose a drone sound source localization method using a distributed dual microphone array and combine it with GCC-PHAT technology to improve peak localization by 2.13 m in environments with a signal-to-noise ratio higher than 0 dB.
At present, TDOA technology reveals great application potential in UAV acoustic detection, and its high accuracy and real-time efficiency strengthen its status as the primary technology for UAV sound source localization. For long-range detection, integrating noise filters or adaptive signal processing technologies holds the potential to further improve localization accuracy in challenging environments. With advancements in sensor networks and edge computing, TDOA plays a key role in enabling efficient real-time acoustic monitoring of large UAV swarms, while also optimizing computational efficiency and minimizing power consumption.
C. Super-resolution localization technology
1. Multiple signal classification (MUSIC) algorithm
The multiple signal classification (MUSIC)135 algorithm, as depicted in Fig. 9(a), is a super-resolution technique that uses eigenvalue decomposition, typically employed to estimate the DOA or time delay differences in signals. In UAV sound source localization, it can pinpoint the direction of sound sources by estimating the DOA from multiple sources. The fundamental principle of MUSIC is to decompose the received signal’s covariance matrix into signal and noise subspaces, utilizing the orthogonality of these subspaces to infer the DOA of the signal. Thus, it can be regarded as a subspace algorithm specifically for narrowband signals.
The spectral function shows peaks in the signal’s direction. By finding these peaks, the DOA angle of the signal can be determined.
Recently, several improvements and extensions have been proposed to enhance the performance of the MUSIC algorithm, such as simplifying the calculation of the spectral function to lower complexity and developing algorithm variants to handle wideband signals. Satish and Medda124 designed a spherical harmonic function-based MUSIC algorithm, decomposing the received acoustic signals into spherical harmonic functions and then processing the mutual spectrum matrix of the signals using the MUSIC algorithm, successfully achieving simultaneous tracking of the azimuth angles of two drones. This improvement enhances the applicability of the MUSIC algorithm for processing broadband acoustic data from drones. Wu et al.31 proposed a MUSIC algorithm based on pseudo-spectrum normalized mean processing for low signal-to-noise ratio environments, eliminating the influence of energy differences among sub-bands on direction of arrival (DOA) estimation. This approach achieves more robust and more precise spatial spectral peaks under low signal-to-noise ratio conditions while effectively suppressing pseudo-peak interference.
To sum up, the MUSIC algorithm can break through the usual resolution limits, making it possible to tell apart multiple signal sources that are very close to each other. More noteworthy is that the MUSIC algorithm is capable of concurrently estimating the angles of arrival for multiple signals, rendering it suitable for the acoustic source localization of multiple UAVs. Furthermore, it exhibits considerable robustness against Gaussian noise. However, due to the involvement of eigenvalue decomposition of the covariance matrix, the MUSIC algorithm entails a substantial computational load, rendering real-time processing challenging. With algorithmic optimizations and hardware acceleration, MUSIC is expected to continue as a key choice for high-precision UAV localization tasks in the future.
2. Estimation of signal parameters via rotational invariance technique (ESPRIT)
ESPRIT is an efficient algorithm for estimating the DOA of signals.136 Its basic principle is based on the translation-invariant property of subarrays within the sensor array, which induces rotational invariance in the signal subspace. Through matrix decomposition and signal subspace methods, it extracts the spatial information of the signal from the received array signals. Compared to the MUSIC algorithm, ESPRIT has the advantage of not requiring spatial search through power spectra, resulting in relatively lower computational complexity and higher efficiency.
The ESPRIT algorithm has high application value in the field of drone acoustic positioning due to its advantages of fast calculation speed and high resolution. Hu et al.125 proposed an improved ESPRIT algorithm based on Toeplitz matrix reconstruction, which fully utilizes array reception data to provide a full-rank equivalent source covariance matrix. In an environment with a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of −7.5 dB, the resolution probability exceeds 95%. Notably, the authors successfully applied this algorithm to a self-developed UAV autonomous interception defense system, demonstrating real-time, precise tracking and interception capabilities for UAV targets in various practical scenarios.
Overall, in DOA estimation, if the application scenario with sufficient computational resources demands a higher resolution, the MUSIC algorithm is a powerful choice. For scenarios requiring real-time processing of large-scale arrays or involving high complexity in angle search, the ESPRIT algorithm is more efficient and suitable due to its spectrum search-free nature. However, it is worth noting that ESPRIT has a strong dependence on the array's geometric structure. If the structure is poorly designed, it may lead to a decrease in noise resistance.
D. Beamforming localization technology
Beamforming is a standard array signal processing technique.137 The fundamental concept is to weight and sum the signals from each array element, orienting the array's beam toward the spatial direction with the highest signal energy at a specific time. According to this principle, the steering vector that maximizes the desired signal’s output power can be determined, indicating the signal’s DOA. By adjusting the weights and phases, the beamforming algorithm is able to dynamically tune its sensitivity and track sound sources from various directions.
In UAV sound source localization, beamforming algorithms are used to determine the direction of each UAV’s sound source based on the signals received by the sensor array. By integrating triangulation methods, the precise positioning of multiple UAVs can be achieved, making it highly applicable for complex airspace control and UAV monitoring missions. Existing research results showcase the benefits of beamforming technology in UAV acoustic localization. As an example, Salvati et al.138 developed a spectral distance response algorithm based on beamforming, enabling efficient localization and enhancement of source signals, effectively identifying different types of sound sources. By incorporating the beamforming algorithm into an anti-UAV system, Cheng et al.126 enabled UAV sound source localization, overcoming the challenges of visual tracking in adverse environments. Itare et al.129 proposed a method for locating drones by tracking their acoustic harmonic characteristics in the time–frequency domain. Its core advantage lies in the use of beamforming technology to automatically ignore most of the irrelevant background noise when calculating the direction of arrival (DOA), thereby independently locating the drone on the energy map and significantly improving the signal-to-noise ratio and robustness of the location.
Beamforming sound source localization offers clear benefits in acoustic-based UAV detection, such as boosting signal strength, resisting interference, and enabling the tracking of multiple UAVs, making it ideal for surveillance and safety operations in complex environments. As such, beamforming localization is of significant importance for UAV flight monitoring and intrusion detection systems. Nevertheless, it encounters issues such as multipath interference, high computational demands, and equipment cost. As algorithms continue to improve and hardware performance advances, it is expected to become even more crucial in UAV security tasks.
VI. FUTURE AND CHALLENGES
Acoustic-based UAV detection remains in the nascent phase, with numerous technologies still underdeveloped. Furthermore, several open challenges have arisen during its evolution that hinder its operational efficiency, such as susceptibility to strong noise interference, insufficient large-scale datasets, difficulties in detecting and localizing multiple UAVs, and the limited applicability of existing detection systems. To tackle the existing open issues, we have conceptualized a few technological development strategies, which are presented as follows.
A. Multimodal detection models
Given the constraints of individual detection technologies in handling complex real-world environments, acoustic data from various sensor types is incorporated, integrating multiple techniques (e.g., acoustic detection, radar detection, visual detection, and RF detection) into a unified model for enhanced detection capability. As presented in Table V, there are ongoing studies dedicated to this area, with the goal of delivering more comprehensive and efficient solutions for drone security. Future studies in this field will place greater emphasis on data synchronization and integration, coordination between various sensors, improving the capacity to handle large datasets, and the development of more intelligent fusion algorithms. The development of large-scale multimodal models is expected to greatly enhance the advancement of UAV detection.

B. Large-scale proprietary datasets
The primary challenge in developing acoustic-based UAV detection technology lies in the lack of public acoustic datasets, which significantly limits models' training and testing performance. The primary challenge in developing acoustic-based UAV detection technology lies in the lack of public acoustic datasets, which significantly limits models' training and testing performance. In recent years, several representative public datasets have emerged, summarized in Table II. All datasets were collected independently by researchers, ensuring data authenticity and traceability—a foundation for advancing the field. However, most focus on common consumer-grade and small multi-rotor drones, failing to adequately account for drones in complex real-world environments. This results in insufficient practicality and representativeness of the data, leaving gaps compared to operational requirements. Examples include inadequate coverage of environmental diversity and the absence of unified industry standards for recording equipment, environmental control, and annotation specifications. These issues make it difficult to conduct fair performance comparisons and generalization evaluations between models trained on different datasets. Existing datasets typically capture acoustic signals within tens of meters, lacking data from longer ranges. This limits models trained on such datasets to handle detection requirements of hundreds or even thousands of meters in real anti-UAV applications. Furthermore, drone swarms represent a significant future threat, yet current datasets cannot support detection research targeting swarm-based threats. Finally, systematically covering drone flight modes (such as hovering, acceleration, cruising, and diving) helps comprehensively capture acoustic feature differences under various operational conditions, enabling refined behavior recognition and situational awareness. Therefore, establishing a large-scale, highly diverse acoustic dataset covering ultra-long-range and swarm scenarios represents a critical bottleneck for advancing algorithms from laboratory research to practical application in this field.
C. Resistance to strong background noise interference
Substantial background noise interference is one of the most prominent challenges in acoustic-based UAV detection. In real-world scenarios, random interference sources are ubiquitous, affecting recorded audio quality to varying degrees. Due to the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of UAV radiation noise received by acoustic arrays, the UAV’s rotor noise is often drowned out by background noise, severely limiting detection accuracy. Developing systems that can effectively handle substantial noise interference is crucial for adapting to complex urban environments, making it highly significant. Advances in directional detection, blind source separation techniques, and advanced filtering methods have made significant strides in addressing this issue, and they represent a promising and challenging research direction.
D. Multiple target detection of UAVs
Most current research focuses on detecting one or a small number of UAVs, and representative achievements are still relatively scarce. As UAVs become increasingly cost-effective, detecting UAV swarms will become an unavoidable challenge. In this situation, distinguishing and identifying signals from different UAVs while avoiding signal overlap and false detections presents an important avenue for future research.
E. Precise localization of UAVs
Most research in acoustic-based UAV detection at present is centered on identifying the presence of UAVs or differentiating between categories, with little emphasis on using acoustics for precise UAV localization. By integrating sound source localization technology, there is potential to create more accurate detection algorithms that can fulfill the growing needs of future detection systems, which is an auspicious research direction.
F. Application of deep reinforcement learning technology
Deep reinforcement learning (DRL),147 a novel tool, has been widely adopted in fields such as computer vision, healthcare, robotics, and audio signal processing. By integrating reinforcement learning with deep learning, DRL addresses the challenges posed by high computational requirements and the dependency on large datasets for deep learning model training. It has gained substantial attention in the field of audio processing, particularly in solving localization problems. With the growing interest in future audio processing technologies, DRL presents a promising research direction for advancing acoustic-based UAV detection.
VII. CONCLUSION
The acoustic-based UAV detection paradigm has proven to be an economical, passive, and environmentally adaptable solution with fundamental value in addressing the growing security concerns posed by unauthorized drone activities. We systematically reviewed the technical framework, including signal acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, identification algorithms, and sound source localization, to clarify the significant achievements and ongoing challenges in this rapidly developing field. A detailed review of the technical workflow reveals critical interdependencies, where high-fidelity signal collection, refined preprocessing to eliminate interference, and subsequent extraction of significant features from both the time and frequency domains are crucial for accurate drone detection. With the continuous maturation and refinement of deep learning technologies, they have demonstrated robust representation learning and generalization capabilities in drone acoustic detection and recognition tasks. Sound source localization represents a key advancement, extending acoustic-based detection from simple presence identification to comprehensive spatial perception. By leveraging array processing and complex sound source localization techniques based on time, angle, and phase differences, precise tracking and threat assessment can be achieved. Although existing technologies have made significant progress, some fundamental challenges remain in terms of noise robustness, long-range detection, and standard datasets. In future research, multimodal data fusion, efficient detection and recognition frameworks, and advanced signal processing techniques are expected to continue advancing detection accuracy, range, multi-target detection, and large-scale deployment. Developing intelligent adaptive systems capable of automatically learning and adapting to environmental changes represents an auspicious direction.
