results.title: How to Reduce Noise Interference in Sensor Readings
results.snippet: Aug 30, 2018 ... MEMS sensors have the most vulnerability to noise interference. A wider mechanical bandwidth causes a sensor to be more likely to detect ...
results.full_text:
Precision inertial sensors are so sensitive that even noise waves can be detected as vibration. For many applications, noise is inevitable, but noise interference in the sensor’s readings is unacceptable. This post explains what causes noise disruption and how it can be prevented.
What Causes Noise Interference for Sensors
Noise comes in two forms, electrical and seismic. The power supply typically transmits electrical noise through the connection to the sensor. Seismic radiates from an outside source such as an airplane engine or a siren. When these acoustic waves are present, the sensor will detect this as vibration.
Sensors are often placed in noisy environments such as factories, trains, and military equipment. Engineers can’t afford to have noise impact their readings, but there are solutions to minimizing this interference.
Eliminating Seismic Noise with a Low Pass Filter
If engineers are certain there will be noise interference in their application, they can request a low pass filter. Upon request, the sensor manufacturer can implement a low pass filter into the sensor’s design. This component will filter out seismic noise that the sensor experiences from external sources. In order to determine if a low pass filter is necessary, the engineers need to inform the manufacturer of the level of noise and its source.
Filtering Out Electrical Noise within the Sensor Connection
Voltage output can be vulnerable to noisy environments, but a shielded connector and cable can reduce this risk. Additionally, instead of using a parallel wire configuration within the cable, a twisted wire can minimize noise.
Long cable lengths typically require 4-20mA output. This is conducive to reducing noise interference since current output is naturally resistant to outside noise interference.
Sensors that Are Vulnerable to Noise Interference
MEMS sensors have the most vulnerability to noise interference. A wider mechanical bandwidth causes a sensor to be more likely to detect acoustic frequencies. Since they have bandwidth to 400 Hz, MEMS accelerometers can be most affected, but all models can come with a low pass filter.
Learn more about Jewell’s inertial sensors with the electronic product catalog. If noise is a factor in your application, talk to us.

results.title: 'Walnut' Attack Uses Sound To Trick Sensors In Cars, Phones, And Other ...
results.snippet: University of Michigan students revealed that MEMS accelerometers used in smartphones, cars, and other devices are vulnerable to sonic attacks.
results.full_text:
'Walnut' Attack Uses Sound To Trick Sensors In Cars, Phones, And Other Devices
Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.
You are now subscribed
Your newsletter sign-up was successful
University of Michigan students revealed that MEMS accelerometers used in smartphones, cars, and other devices are vulnerable to sonic attacks. Attackers could exploit this vulnerability to take over systems controlled via accelerometer data--but you don't have to soundproof your phone just yet.
The students demonstrated their findings by modifying a music video with additional tones meant to interfere with a nearby phone's accelerometer. They managed to trick the sensor into outputting data that spelled "WALNUT"--so, naturally, they decided to call their experiment the Walnut Attack. The students also showed how an RC vehicle controlled by a Samsung phone's accelerometer could have its steering disrupted via sonic-based attacks.
The attack is relatively basic. "Capacitive MEMS accelerometers use the deflection of a mass as a proxy for measuring acceleration," the students explained in their write-up. "Acoustic pressure waves exhibit forces on physical objects in their path," they said, which means "subjecting the sensing mass–spring structure to acoustic interference at its resonant frequency can displace the sensing mass enough to spoof false acceleration signals."
Article continues belowIn other words, sounds can trick accelerometers into thinking they're accelerating. Got it. Here's why that's a problem:
Attackers that have capabilities to deliver high intensity acoustic interference in close proximity to the target MEMS sensor can spoof the sensor to output arbitrary, attacker–chosen, signals. Our experiments demonstrate the spelling of the word 'WALNUT' over the output signal of a MEMS accelerometer. With proper knowledge of the algorithms that are utilizing the polluted sensor data, adversaries may be able to control the behavior of a system that relies on the sensor data to make automated decisions.
Using a music video in their demonstration also let the students illustrate another point--the sounds don't have to be isolated to affect targeted accelerometers. They noted that the "interference remains effective even when combined with videos and music that could be automatically played from websites, email attachments, Twitter links tapped on a smartphone, etc." That makes it harder for you to defend against these sonic-based attacks.
The good news is that none of this is life-threatening, so even though many different types of device use these vulnerable accelerometers, you shouldn't have to worry too much about trying to protect your phone or car from all outside noises. The students have a few suggestions for manufacturers, such as surrounding accelerometers with acoustic dampening foam and rejecting abnormal signals, but they also said they "have no reason to believe that acoustic sensor spoofing attacks are currently being mounted to disable autonomous cyber-physical systems" or used for other attacks.
Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.
Still, much like the revelation that malware could turn headphones into microphones, the Walnut Attack is another sign that cyber attacks don't all come in the same flavor. Sure, many of them use malware to affect a device's software or firmware, but attacks on other components, whether they're headphones or accelerometers, can also make a device vulnerable to attack--even if it's currently limited to spelling "WALNUT" or crashing RC vehicles.
Nathaniel Mott is a freelance news and features writer for Tom's Hardware US, covering breaking news, security, and the silliest aspects of the tech industry.

results.title: MEMS Technology: The Backbone of Modern-Day Sensors - Inertial Labs
results.snippet: Temperature Variations: MEMS sensors are sensitive to temperature changes, leading to variations in output characteristics such as bias, scale factor, and noise ...
results.full_text:
A term that has been ever-evolving in the tech landscape is the term “MEMS: Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems.” MEMS sensors have revolutionized various industries by enabling precise measurement of physical phenomena compactly and cost-effectively. This article explores the fundamental principles behind MEMS sensors, their mechanisms of operation, diverse applications across industries, and their integration into advanced technology systems within the Aerospace & Defense industry worldwide. Additionally, it discusses ongoing innovations in MEMS technology and their implications for future technological advancements.
MEMS sensors represent a fusion of electronics and mechanics on a microscale, facilitating precise measurements and driving innovations across industries. The components within these devices operate through transduction, converting physical parameters into electrical signals through the dynamic interaction of microscopic mechanical structures and electronic components. All Inertial Labs IMUs (Inertial Measurement Units) consist of robust MEMS-based accelerometers and gyroscopes.
Fundamental Design of MEMS Accelerometers and Gyroscopes
Fundamentally, all MEMS accelerometer sensors commonly measure the displacement of a mass with a position-measuring interface circuit. That measurement is then converted into a digital electrical signal through an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) for digital processing. The moving mass (suspended via a spring within a medium of air) is known to generate a change in electrical capacitance, which is digitized and then quantified as a known linear acceleration value. This sensing technique is known for its high accuracy, stability, low power dissipation, and simple structure to build. MEMS gyroscope sensors are similar in nature, with the only difference being that they measure both the displacement of the resonating mass and its frame because of the Coriolis acceleration, which allows them to sense angular displacement.
A series of fixed electrodes connected to the frame in parallel detects the change in capacitance generated by the moving mass. Once the signals are digitized, various signal processing steps, such as conditioning, demodulation, and low-pass filtering, are conducted before routing to a serial peripheral interface (SPI) or I2C serial protocol for the measurements to be provided to a host system.
TAG-304 – Three Axis Gyroscope
Challenges Faced by MEMS Accelerometers and Gyroscopes
While precise engineering developments have led to significant advancements in the size, weight, and power reduction of MEMS sensors, natural challenges may, over time, affect the sensor’s accuracy performance.
- Temperature Variations: MEMS sensors are sensitive to temperature changes, leading to variations in output characteristics such as bias, scale factor, and noise levels.
- Aging Effects: Long-term usage can cause drifts in sensor parameters, affecting measurement accuracy over time.
- Environmental Factors: Exposure to humidity, pressure variations, and mechanical shocks can impact sensor performance and reliability.
Calibration Techniques for MEMS Sensors
To account for the challenges stated above, manufacturing processes at Inertial Labs for MEMS-based IMUs go through a series of calibration and IMU procedures that result in high quality and continuous provision of sensor data streams over different environmental operating conditions:
Temperature Calibration: Employing temperature chambers or thermal control methods during calibration to assess sensor behavior across temperature ranges and compensate for temperature-induced errors.
Aging Compensation: Periodic recalibration, using algorithms to model sensor drift over time and applying correction factors to maintain accuracy.
Environmental Testing: Subjecting sensors to environmental chambers or simulated conditions to evaluate performance under varying humidity, pressure, and mechanical stresses.
Dynamic Calibration: Performing dynamic tests to assess sensor response under different acceleration or angular velocity inputs to verify linearity and dynamic range.
Advanced Calibration Methods
Sensor Fusion: Integrating data from multiple sensors (e.g., accelerometers, gyroscopes, magnetometers) and applying sensor fusion algorithms (e.g., Kalman filters) to improve accuracy, reduce errors, and enhance robustness.
Dual axis rate table
Applications of MEMS Sensors:
- Automotive: They play crucial roles in airbag deployment, stability control, and tire pressure monitoring for improved safety and performance.
- Healthcare: MEMS pressure sensors contribute to the functionality of medical devices, wearables for health monitoring, and minimally invasive surgical procedures.
- Environmental Monitoring: MEMS devices aid in weather forecasting, air quality assessment, and seismic activity detection, enhancing disaster preparedness.
- Industrial Sector: MEMS sensors optimize machinery operations and offer real-time equipment condition insights, improving productivity.
- Aerospace & Defense: They stabilize aircraft, manage navigation systems, and ensure precise airbag deployment during space missions, advancing aerospace technologies.
MEMS sensors continue to impact technology profoundly, offering compactness, intelligence, and interconnectedness to devices across industries. As we stand poised at the threshold of a technological revolution, one certainty stands tall – MEMS sensors will continue to pioneer how we perceive, engage, and navigate the world around us.
Inertial Labs is making significant advances in using MEMS sensors across various applications, emphasizing their innovation in this technology. One of their standout innovations includes the development of high-precision three-axis accelerometers, which are pivotal in enhancing performance and reliability in applications ranging from autonomous vehicles to marine and subsea systems. These accelerometers are designed to deliver critical data for navigation and control, ensuring high precision in challenging environments.
Moreover, Inertial Labs has been actively collaborating with major technology firms like Sony to integrate advanced LiDAR systems into drones, aiming to improve data accuracy and workflow efficiency in surveying, mapping, and cinematography. This collaboration highlights their commitment to leveraging MEMS technology in cutting-edge applications.

results.title: MEMS Microphone Sensitivity: Understanding Its Importance for ...
results.snippet: May 21, 2025 ... Sensitivity is a crucial aspect of MEMS microphone performance because it directly impacts the microphone's ability to pick up subtle sounds.
results.full_text:
MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) microphones have revolutionized the audio technology landscape, offering compact, high-performance solutions for a wide variety of applications. From smartphones and tablets to wearables and smart home devices, MEMS microphones have become the standard for modern audio capture. A key factor that determines the performance of these microphones is their sensitivity, which plays a critical role in achieving clear and accurate sound reproduction.
What is MEMS Microphone Sensitivity?
MEMS microphone sensitivity refers to the microphone’s ability to detect sound signals. It measures how effectively a microphone converts sound pressure into an electrical signal. The higher the sensitivity of a MEMS microphone, the better it can capture softer sounds, making it an essential feature for applications that demand high precision and clarity, such as hearing aids, automotive electronics, and voice assistants.
Why Sensitivity Matters in MEMS Microphones
Sensitivity is a crucial aspect of MEMS microphone performance because it directly impacts the microphone’s ability to pick up subtle sounds. The ideal sensitivity level ensures that the microphone can capture a wide range of sound pressures without distorting or losing detail. In consumer electronics, this means improved voice clarity for smart devices, while in industrial applications, it leads to more accurate sound detection for machines and equipment.
Factors Influencing MEMS Microphone Sensitivity
Several factors affect the sensitivity of MEMS microphones, including:
- Microphone Design: The physical structure and size of the microphone’s diaphragm influence how it responds to sound waves.
- Material Used: The choice of materials in the construction of the microphone, such as silicon, can affect how efficiently it picks up sound vibrations.
- Frequency Response: A microphone’s sensitivity is often paired with its frequency response, determining how well it can capture both high and low frequencies.
- Noise Reduction Technology: Many advanced MEMS microphones incorporate noise-canceling features, which can enhance their sensitivity by filtering out unwanted background noise.
Applications of High-Sensitivity MEMS Microphones
High-sensitivity MEMS microphones are used in a variety of applications that demand precise and accurate sound detection, including:
- Smartphones & Tablets: For clear voice commands and high-quality audio recording.
- Wearable Devices: Such as smartwatches and fitness trackers, where small microphones are needed for voice recognition and audio input.
- Smart Home Devices: Ensuring clear interaction with voice assistants and home automation systems.
- Automotive Electronics: Enhancing hands-free communication systems by providing clear audio in noisy environments.
- Hearing Aids: Offering users the ability to hear a broader range of sounds with enhanced clarity.
The Benefits of High Sensitivity in MEMS Microphones
- Enhanced Audio Quality: With improved sensitivity, MEMS microphones capture a fuller range of sounds, leading to more accurate and richer audio recordings.
- Better Voice Recognition: High sensitivity ensures that even soft-spoken commands are detected, making voice assistants more responsive.
- Noise Reduction: A sensitive microphone can better distinguish between desirable sounds and background noise, resulting in clearer audio in noisy environments.
Choosing the Right MEMS Microphone for Your Needs
When selecting a MEMS microphone for a specific application, it is essential to consider not only sensitivity but also other features like size, frequency range, and power consumption. Companies like Wuxi Silicon Source Technology Co., Ltd. offer a wide range of MEMS microphones that are optimized for different needs, from high-sensitivity models for precise audio capture to robust models designed for industrial and automotive applications.
MEMS Microphone Innovations
As technology evolves, MEMS microphones are becoming more advanced. New innovations in microphone technology are continuously improving sensitivity and noise reduction capabilities. For more details on the latest developments, check out this article on advances in MEMS microphone technology.
Conclusion
MEMS microphone sensitivity is a fundamental factor that influences the performance of audio systems in various devices. Whether you’re developing a voice-activated product, an advanced hearing aid, or a smartphone, choosing the right MEMS microphone with optimal sensitivity ensures superior sound quality and performance. At Wuxi Silicon Source Technology Co., Ltd., we provide state-of-the-art MEMS microphones that deliver exceptional sensitivity, making them the ideal choice for your next innovative product.

results.title: A Comprehensive guide to MEMS sensors - ES Systems
results.snippet: Jul 14, 2023 ... How does a MEMS pressure sensor work? ... Simply, MEMS sensors detect changes in resistance or capacitance when exposed to force on their surface.
results.full_text:
Table of Contents
State-of-the-art miniature devices, MEMS sensors have the power to transform industries by measuring physical quantities with incredible precision.
MEMS technology has enabled the development of highly integrated systems that monitor process performance and offer essential feedback for automated control systems in manufacturing plants, not to mention that it ensures safety by precisely measuring pressure within tanks and pipelines, mitigating potential hazards.
Let’s delve deeper into the fascinating world of MEMS sensors, improved productivity, and cost reduction, shedding light on their working principles, applications, and their vital role in the industrial field.
What is a MEMS sensor?
MEMS sensors are miniature devices used to measure physical quantities such as pressure, temperature, acceleration and magnetic fields.
Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) sensors are widely utilized across a wide variety of applications, from medical to automotive systems and with a ruggedized mechanical envelopes they are built to withstand harsh environmental conditions.
The ESCP-MIS2 sensor incorporates ES Systems capacitive MEMS sensor that is built using ES Systems exclusive SOI micro-fabrication TM30 process for absolute capacitive pressure sensors, withstanding temperatures ranging from -20oC to +85oC.
MEMS sensors are made up of both electronic and mechanical components. Microelectronic components handle sensing and processing of data while mechanical parts provide physical feedback – this combination makes MEMS sensors small, reliable, and cost-effective.
What Do MEMS Sensors Do?
MEMS devices and especially MEMS pressure sensors have become indispensable industrial tools, providing measurements in closed systems or monitoring changes in atmospheric conditions.
What Is MEMS Technology?
MEMS technology has transformed manufacturing and design practices significantly over recent years.
Microfabrication and nanotechnology come together to form an approach that creates small devices with both mechanical and electrical components that can be utilized across industries ranging from medical applications to consumer electronics, allowing us to make more efficient products by exploiting their small size and high precision.
MEMS fabrication uses tools such as lithography, etching, deposition and bonding to fabricate structures on the micron scale for use across various industries. Engineers now have access to MEMS fabrication technology which offers endless opportunities for innovation and advancement across several fields of endeavor.
What are some popular examples of MEMS sensors?
Check out some of the different types of MEMS sensors that are currently available and how they can be utilized in various situations.
- MEMS Pressure Sensors: MEMS pressure sensors are commonly utilized in industrial automation systems, automotive systems and medical devices for measuring and monitoring pressure in closed systems such as tanks or pipelines.
- MEMS Accelerometers: These accelerometers are used in aerospace, automotive and consumer electronics to measure acceleration, tilt, vibration and movement to provide motion detection and stability control capabilities.
- MEMS Gyroscopes: Used extensively in navigation systems, drones and virtual reality devices to measure angular velocity and orientation with great precision.
- MEMS Temperature Sensors: These temperature sensors are utilized in HVAC systems, industrial processes and medical equipment in order to monitor and regulate temperatures for optimal performance and safety.
- MEMS Microphones: MEMS microphones can be found in smartphones, wearables and audio systems to capture and convert sound waves into electrical signals for clear audio reproduction.
- MEMS Gas/Flow Sensors: These sensors are widely utilized for environmental monitoring, medical device development, automotive emissions control systems and industrial safety systems to detect and measure various gases or fluid flow rates to ensure quality and safety standards are upheld.
How does a MEMS pressure sensor work?
Simply, MEMS sensors detect changes in resistance or capacitance when exposed to force on their surface.
More specifically, it features a suspended mass between two capacitive plates that when tilted create a difference in electric potential resulting in a change of capacitance.
Capacitance changes can then be translated into electrical signals which can be recognized and understood by devices connected to them.
Let’s walk through how MEMS sensors operate:
- Sensing Element: At the heart of every MEMS pressure sensor is its sensing element – composed of a diaphragm and fixed electrode. The diaphragm acts like a flexible thin membrane that deforms in response to pressure applications.
- Capacitance Variation: As pressure is applied to a diaphragm, its deflection causes distance changes between it and a fixed electrode; these changes alter capacitance between components of a circuit.
- Converting Capacitance Change into Electrical Signal: Once converted, changes in capacitance are converted into an electrical signal by way of a sensor designed in such a way as to correspond with applied pressure.
- Signal Interpretation: The electrical signal generated by a MEMS pressure sensor is sent to an electronic circuit for interpretation and processing, which may either output analog or digital output depending on its design requirements.
- Output Representation: Once interpreted, the signal is further processed to accurately represent pressure measurements. It may then be displayed on a digital screen or recorded into a data logger for future use in control and automation applications for various industrial uses.
MEMS pressure sensors have quickly become an indispensable “tool”, providing accurate readings even under extreme conditions.
MEMS pressure sensors use capacitance changes resulting from diaphragm deflection to provide precise pressure measurements, with their small size, high sensitivity, and ability to operate in harsh environments making them invaluable devices across industries like automotive, aerospace, medical and industrial automation.
What are the main MEMS pressure sensor applications in industry today?
MEMS technology has become an ever-more significant component of industrial processes.
MEMS technology has enabled the creation of highly integrated systems to measure, control, and monitor a range of processes and operations – providing accurate measurements of physical parameters like pressure, temperature, acceleration and flow rate which are all vital in an industrial setting. Utilised to measure these factors are MEMS sensors, for instance these applications could include:
- Monitoring process performance in manufacturing plants
- Offering feedback for automated control systems
- Measuring tank and pipe pressure for safety purposes
- Establishing smart factories using advanced automation techniques that enhance productivity while decreasing costs, and more
Final Thoughts on MEMS Sensors
As engineering and technology continue to advance, MEMS sensors have proven themselves as transformative tools, offering industries precision and efficiency.
MEMS devices have revolutionized industries with their compact size and extraordinary accuracy, leading to further innovations, advancements, breakthroughs, and breakthroughs that drive highly integrated systems that monitor, control, optimize industrial processes and enable real-time insights and informed decision-making.
Accept the challenge of pushing boundaries, exploring uncharted waters, and using MEMS sensors to build smarter, more efficient systems with the assistance of ES Systems. Our wide range of products will allow industries to reach greater heights together.

results.title: Why MEMS Sensors are the Next Big Thing? Small Wonder, Huge Impact
results.snippet: A MEMS sensor is a micro-electro-mechanical system that integrates mechanical and electrical components on a miniature scale, typically using semiconductor fabrication techniques. These sensors are capable of detecting and measuring physical phenomena such as pressure, temperature, acceleration, vibration, and more, converting them into electrical signals that can be processed and interpreted.
results.full_text:
In the rapidly evolving landscape of technology, MEMS sensors stand out as a pivotal innovation, transforming how devices interact with their environment. These miniature marvels integrate mechanical and electrical components at a microscale, leveraging the precision of semiconductor fabrication techniques.
From the inception of MEMS technology by pioneers like Harvey C. Nathanson to its widespread application across industries, MEMS sensors have revolutionized fields such as consumer electronics, automotive, healthcare, and aerospace. Their ability to measure a vast array of physical phenomena with high accuracy and reliability has opened up new frontiers in data collection and analysis.
This article delves into the origins, workings, types, and applications of MEMS sensors, highlighting their advantages and future trends, and provides insights into integrating these sensors into various projects. Through this exploration, we uncover the significance of MEMS sensors in driving innovation and enhancing the capabilities of modern technology.
What does MEMS mean
What does MEMS stand for?
MEMS stands for Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems. This technology integrates mechanical and electrical components at a microscale, typically ranging from a few micrometers to a millimeter in size.
Who invented MEMS technology?
The invention of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) technology is credited to Harvey C. Nathanson, who, along with his team at Westinghouse Research Laboratories, developed the first MEMS device in 1967.
This device, known as the resonant gate transistor, marked a significant milestone in the history of MEMS technology, demonstrating the potential for combining mechanical elements with electronic components on a microscopic scale.
Introduction to MEMS Sensors
What is a MEMS sensor?
A MEMS sensor is a micro-electro-mechanical system that integrates mechanical and electrical components on a miniature scale, typically using semiconductor fabrication techniques.
These sensors are capable of detecting and measuring physical phenomena such as pressure, temperature, acceleration, vibration, and more, converting them into electrical signals that can be processed and interpreted.
Who makes MEMS sensors?
Several leading companies and manufacturers specialize in the production of MEMS sensors, catering to a wide range of industries including automotive, consumer electronics, healthcare, and industrial applications. Some of the prominent players in the MEMS sensors market include:
- Bosch
- STMicroelectronics
- TE Connectivity
- Infineon Technologies
- TDK Invensense
- CFSensor
Why MEMS are used as sensors?
MEMS technology is used for a multitude of reasons, stemming from its unique ability to integrate mechanical and electrical components at a microscale. This integration offers several advantages that make MEMS an attractive solution for various applications across different industries.
What are the different types of MEMS sensors?
Check out some of the different types of Mems sensors list and their applications:
Pressure Sensors
MEMS pressure sensors measure pressure in an environment and convert it into an electrical signal. They are widely used in automotive applications for tire pressure monitoring systems (TPMS), in medical devices for blood pressure monitoring, and in industrial settings for process control.
Accelerometers
MEMS accelerometer measure acceleration forces, whether static, like gravity, or dynamic, to sense movement or vibrations. They are crucial in vehicle airbag deployment systems, smartphones for orientation detection, and in wearable fitness devices to track physical activity.
Gyroscopes
MEMS gyroscopes measure angular rate or rotational movement around a particular axis. They are essential for the stabilization features in drones and cameras, for navigation in smartphones and vehicles, and in gaming controllers for motion sensing.
Magnetometers
Magnetometers measure magnetic fields, often used to determine orientation relative to the Earth’s magnetic poles. They are commonly found in smartphones and navigation systems as compasses, aiding in map rotation and accurate positioning.
Microphones
MEMS microphones convert sound waves into electrical signals, offering advantages such as small size, high sensitivity, and low power consumption. They are used in smartphones, hearing aids, smart speakers, and other consumer electronics for voice input and noise cancellation.
Temperature Sensors
These sensors measure temperature with high accuracy and are used in a variety of applications, including environmental monitoring, automotive systems for engine temperature control, and in smart home devices for HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) management.
Humidity Sensors
MEMS humidity sensors detect and measure the moisture content in the air. They are important for environmental monitoring, in smart HVAC systems to regulate air quality, and in agriculture for monitoring soil moisture levels.
Optical Sensors
MEMS optical sensors include devices like digital mirror devices (DMDs) used in projectors and optical switches for telecommunications. They can manipulate light for imaging, display, or fiber optic communication.
Chemical and Gas Sensors
Mems gas sensors detect and measure the concentration of various chemicals and gases in the environment. They are used for air quality monitoring, in industrial safety systems to detect hazardous gases, and in healthcare for breath analysis.
Biosensors
MEMS biosensors are designed to detect biological elements (e.g., enzymes, antibodies, microorganisms). They have applications in medical diagnostics, such as glucose monitoring for diabetes management, and in environmental monitoring for detecting contaminants.
Understanding how do MEMS sensors work
MEMS sensors operate based on the integration of microscale mechanical components with electronic circuits on a single chip, utilizing microfabrication technology. Let’s walk through how MEMS based sensors operate:
1. Physical Stimulus Detection
The core of a MEMS sensor is a microscale mechanical structure designed to respond to a specific physical stimulus from the environment, such as pressure, acceleration, temperature, or chemical presence.
This mechanical structure can be a movable mass, a flexible membrane, a beam, or any other form that can change its position or properties in response to external stimuli.
2. Mechanical to Electrical Transduction
The key to MEMS sensors’ functionality is their ability to convert the mechanical response of the structure into an electrical signal. This conversion is achieved through various transduction mechanisms, which can include:
- Capacitive Sensing: The movement or deformation of the mechanical structure alters the capacitance between it and another fixed element. This change in capacitance is measurable and correlates directly to the magnitude of the physical stimulus.
- Piezoresistive Sensing: The mechanical deformation leads to a change in the resistance of a material. By measuring this change in resistance, the sensor can quantify the applied stimulus.
- Piezoelectric Sensing: Certain materials generate an electrical charge in response to mechanical stress. This effect can be used to measure dynamic changes, such as vibrations or accelerations.
- Thermal Sensing: Changes in temperature can affect the resistance of a material (thermoresistive effect) or generate a voltage difference (thermoelectric effect), which can be measured to determine temperature changes.
3. Signal Processing
The electrical signal produced by the transduction mechanism is then processed by integrated electronic circuits. This processing may involve amplification, filtering, and analog-to-digital conversion, making the signal suitable for interpretation and analysis.
4. Output and Interpretation
The processed signal is outputted to an external system (such as a microcontroller or computer), where it can be further analyzed, displayed, or used to make decisions.
Where are MEMS sensors used?
MEMS sensors have a wide array of applications across various industries due to their small size, high reliability, and versatility. These sensors can measure physical phenomena such as pressure, acceleration, temperature, and more, making them indispensable in today’s technology-driven world. Let’s take a look at some of the main MEMS sensors applications:
Consumer Electronics
- Smartphones and Tablets: MEMS sensors like accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers enable features such as screen orientation changes, motion detection for gaming, and navigation.
- Wearable Devices: Fitness trackers and smartwatches use MEMS sensors to monitor physical activity, such as steps taken, heart rate, and sleep quality.
Automotive
- Safety Systems: MEMS pressure sensors for automotive and accelerometers are crucial for airbag deployment systems, detecting collisions and triggering airbags to inflate.
- Vehicle Stability and Navigation: Gyroscopes and accelerometers help with vehicle stability control systems and in navigation systems for accurate positioning and direction.
Healthcare
- Medical Devices: MEMS pressure sensor and accelerometers are used in devices like digital blood pressure monitors and wearable health monitors. MEMS microphones are also used in hearing aids.
- Lab-on-a-Chip: MEMS technology enables miniaturized devices for diagnostics and drug delivery, allowing for rapid testing and monitoring of health conditions.
Industrial and Environmental Monitoring
- Process Control: Pressure and temperature sensors are used in industrial settings to monitor and control processes, ensuring safety and efficiency.
- Environmental Sensing: MEMS sensors can detect and measure environmental parameters such as air quality, humidity, and temperature, useful in weather stations and pollution monitoring.
Consumer Goods
Home Appliances and Systems: MEMS sensors are used in smart home devices, such as thermostats and security systems, for environmental monitoring and control.
Entertainment and Gaming
Motion Control: In gaming consoles and virtual reality systems, MEMS sensors detect player movements, enhancing the interactive experience.
What are the advantages of MEMS sensor?
MEMS sensors unique benefits stem from the integration of mechanical and electrical components at a microscale, leveraging microfabrication technology. This leads to a number of other advantages:
Miniaturization
MEMS sensors are incredibly small, allowing them to be integrated into a wide range of devices without significantly impacting size or weight. This miniaturization is particularly beneficial for portable and wearable technology, medical implants, and other applications where space is limited.
Low Power Consumption
They are designed to operate with very low power consumption, which is crucial for battery-powered devices. This extends the operational life of products like smartphones, wearable devices, and remote sensing systems.
High Precision and Sensitivity
MEMS sensors can achieve high levels of precision and sensitivity, making them capable of detecting minute changes in physical phenomena such as pressure, acceleration, temperature, and more. This precision is essential for applications requiring accurate measurements, from automotive safety systems to medical diagnostics.
Cost-Effectiveness
The manufacturing process for MEMS sensors uses established semiconductor fabrication techniques, allowing for mass production at relatively low costs.
Integration Capability
MEMS technology enables the integration of multiple functions (sensing, actuation, and signal processing) onto a single chip. This reduces the complexity and size of systems, leading to improvements in reliability and performance.
Versatility
MEMS sensors are incredibly versatile, with the ability to measure a wide variety of physical and chemical parameters.
Robustness and Reliability
They are designed to be robust and reliable, capable of operating in harsh environments and under extreme conditions. This makes MEMS sensors suitable for critical applications in automotive, aerospace, and industrial settings where durability is essential.
Improved User Experience
In consumer electronics, MEMS sensors contribute to enhanced user experiences by enabling features such as gesture recognition, image stabilization, and context-aware services. These improvements make devices more intuitive and enjoyable to use.
Rapid Response Times
MEMS sensors can respond quickly to changes in the environment, which is critical for applications requiring real-time monitoring and control, such as active vehicle safety systems and dynamic industrial processes.
Scalability
The technology is scalable, allowing for the development of sensors that can meet specific application requirements, from very small sensors for medical applications to larger, more robust sensors for industrial use.
The combination of these advantages makes MEMS sensors a cornerstone of modern sensor technology, driving innovation and enabling the development of smarter, more efficient, and more responsive systems across a broad spectrum of applications.
How to Integrate MEMS Sensors into Your Projects
Integrating MEMS sensors into your projects can significantly enhance their capabilities, enabling them to interact with the physical world in precise and meaningful ways.
Whether you’re developing a consumer electronics device, a medical instrument, or an industrial monitoring system, the integration process involves several key steps to ensure successful implementation and optimal performance.
Here’s a guide:
1. Define Your Requirements
- Identify the Physical Quantities to Measure: Determine what you need to measure (e.g., pressure, temperature, acceleration) to achieve your project’s objectives.
- Specify Performance Criteria: Define the required accuracy, range, resolution, and response time for the measurements.
- Consider Environmental Conditions: Assess the environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, vibrations) the sensor will be exposed to and ensure the chosen sensor can operate effectively within those parameters.
2. Select the Appropriate MEMS Sensor
- Research Available Sensors: Look for MEMS sensors that meet your project’s requirements, considering factors like size, power consumption, and cost.
- Evaluate Sensor Interfaces: Choose sensors with interfaces (analog or digital) that are compatible with your system’s microcontroller or processor.
- Check for Additional Features: Some MEMS sensors come with built-in signal conditioning, temperature compensation, or data processing capabilities, which can simplify integration.
3. Design the System Architecture
- Plan the Sensor Integration: Determine how the MEMS sensor will physically fit into your system and how it will connect to other components.
- Design the Electronic Circuit: Develop the circuitry needed to power the sensor and to read and process its signals. This may include amplifiers, ADCs (Analog-to-Digital Converters), and communication interfaces.
- Consider Power Management: If your project is battery-powered, devise strategies to minimize power consumption by the sensor and associated electronics.
4. Develop the Firmware and Software
- Write Sensor Drivers: Develop software to initialize the sensor, configure its settings, and read data from it. Utilize the sensor manufacturer’s datasheet and application notes for guidance.
- Implement Data Processing: Depending on your application, you may need to filter, average, or otherwise process the sensor data to extract useful information.
- Integrate with Application Logic: Ensure the sensor data is appropriately used within your system’s overall application logic, triggering actions or decisions as needed.
5. Prototype and Test
- Build a Prototype: Assemble a prototype of your system incorporating the MEMS sensor.
- Conduct Testing: Test the prototype under various conditions to ensure the sensor performs as expected. Pay attention to accuracy, responsiveness, and any potential interference from other components.
- Iterate as Necessary: Based on testing results, make any necessary adjustments to the sensor integration, circuit design, or software.
6. Calibration and Validation
- Calibrate the Sensor: Perform calibration, if necessary, to ensure the sensor’s output accurately reflects the measured quantities. This may involve comparing the sensor’s readings with a known reference.
- Validate System Performance: Validate the overall system performance, ensuring it meets the project’s objectives and requirements.
7. Prepare for Production
- Optimize the Design for Manufacturing: Make any final adjustments to simplify manufacturing and assembly, reduce costs, and ensure reliability.
- Document the Integration Process: Document the sensor integration process, including design choices, calibration procedures, and testing results, to support future development and troubleshooting.
Integrating MEMS sensors into your projects requires careful planning and attention to detail, but the rewards include enhanced functionality, improved accuracy, and the ability to interact with the physical world in innovative ways.
Let CFSensor Team Help You Explore All Of The Possibilities To Build The Perfect Sensor!
Future Trends in MEMS Sensor Technology
The field of MEMS sensor technology is rapidly evolving, driven by continuous innovation and growing demand across various sectors. As these sensors become increasingly integral to our daily lives and industries, several future trends are emerging that promise to expand their capabilities, applications, and impact. The key trends in MEMS sensor technology to watch:
- Future of MEMS sensors in IoT devices
- Applications of MEMS sensors in healthcare
- Wearable Technology and E-Textiles
- Mems sensors in automotive applications
- MEMS technology in industrial automation
- AI and Machine Learning Integration
As MEMS sensor technology continues to advance, these trends highlight the potential for even greater impact across industries, improving efficiency, safety, and quality of life. The future of MEMS sensors lies in their ability to become more integrated, intelligent, and versatile, driving innovation in countless applications.
Final Thoughts on MEMS Sensors
In the future, the integration of MEMS sensors with emerging trends such as IoT, AI, and energy harvesting signals a future where technology is even more seamlessly woven into the fabric of our lives.
For innovators and engineers, understanding how to integrate these sensors into projects is critical to realizing their full potential. It is believed that with the help of CFSensor, boundaries will be pushed, smarter and more efficient systems will be built using MEMS sensors. CFSensor’s wide range of products will enable industries to reach greater heights together.
As MEMS technology continues to advance, it promises to lead to more innovative solutions, further blurring the lines between the digital and physical worlds and shaping the future of technology.
FAQs About MEMS Sensors
Is MEMS a chip?
MEMS can indeed be described as a type of chip, but it’s a special kind that integrates both mechanical and electrical components at a microscale.
Unlike traditional semiconductor chips that are purely electronic, MEMS chips incorporate tiny mechanical elements such as sensors, actuators, and structures alongside electronic circuits.
These mechanical components can move or interact with their environment, enabling the MEMS chip to perform a wide range of functions beyond what electronic chips can do.
Is MEMS a semiconductor?
MEMS technology incorporates aspects of semiconductor manufacturing processes but extends beyond traditional semiconductors by integrating mechanical elements with electronic circuits.
While MEMS devices are fabricated using semiconductor fabrication techniques, they are not semiconductors in the strict sense of the term.
How accurate are MEMS sensors?
Generally, MEMS sensors are known for their high precision and reliability, making them suitable for a wide range of applications, from consumer electronics to automotive and aerospace industries.
What is an example of a MEMS sensor?
An example of a MEMS sensor is the MEMS pressure sensor. These sensors are widely used across various industries for their ability to accurately measure pressure levels in environments or systems.
MEMS pressure sensors integrate mechanical and electrical components on a microscale, utilizing the principles of MEMS technology to convert pressure changes into electrical signals that can be easily measured and analyzed.

results.title: Protecting motion sensors from acoustic injection attack
results.snippet: Cyber-physical systems depend on sensors to make automated decisions. Resonant acoustic injection attacks are already known to cause malfunctions by disabling MEMS-based gyroscopes. However, an open question remains on how to move beyond denial of service attacks to achieve full adversarial control of sensor outputs. This work investigates how analog acoustic injection attacks can damage the ...

Definitions
- the present disclosure relates to methods and systems for protecting motion sensors from intentional acoustic interference.
- MEMS accelerometers have been widely used in cyber-physical systems, such as implantable medical devices, automobiles, avionics, and even critical industrial systems.
- cyber-physical systems such as implantable medical devices, automobiles, avionics, and even critical industrial systems.
- These systems deploy layers of software that abstract away hardware details to collect and analyze data provided by sensors, and then autonomously react to sensor data in real time.
- the software assumes that the underlying hardware is behaving according to specification, and the common practice is to inherently trust the output from sensors.
- developers are becoming more diligent in hardening software to security vulnerabilities, but fewer methodologies exist in the sensor hardware domain.
- MEMS accelerometers have a sensing mass, connected to springs, that is displaced when the sensor is accelerated. Acoustic waves propagate through the air, and exhibit forces on physical objects in their path. If the acoustic frequency is tuned correctly, it can vibrate the accelerometer's sensing mass, altering the sensor's output in a predictable way.
- the impact of acoustic interference on the sensor's entire architecture is modeled, including both the sensing mass and signal conditioning components.
- Two problematic components are identified in the signal conditioning path of typical MEMS accelerometers (i.e., insecure low-pass filters and insecure amplifiers) that lead to two types of adulterated outputs: fluctuating measurements and constant measurements.
- a method for determining an output from a motion sensor includes: receiving an output signal from the motion sensor, where the output signal exhibits a known resonant frequency; sampling the output signal at a sampling frequency, where the sampling frequency is less than or equal to the known resonant frequency and sample time for each sample is chosen randomly; and determining an output for the motion sensor by averaging the samples from the output signal.
- the sampling time is set by adding a random delay to the sampling period.
- the random delay is preferably uniformly distributed across the resonant period and may be generated using a true random number generator. In the event that the motion sensor exhibits multiple resonant frequencies, the random delay can be uniformly distributed in a period equal to the least common multiple of all resonant frequencies exhibited by the motion sensor.
- a system for mitigating effects of acoustic interference with output of a motion sensor.
- the system includes: a motion sensor configured to detect motion of an object; a random number generator configured to generate a random number; and a signal processor in data communication with the motion sensor and the random number generator.
- the signal processor samples the signal output by the motion sensor at a sampling frequency and generates an output by averaging the samples from the signal, where the sampling frequency is less than or equal to the known resonant frequency and sample time for each sample is chosen randomly.
- a different method for determining an output from a motion sensor. The method includes: receiving an output signal from the motion sensor, where the output signal exhibits a known resonant frequency; sampling the output signal at a frequency twice the known resonant frequency, where every other sample is taken at 180 degrees phase delay with respect to the known resonant frequency and forms a sample pair; and determining an output sample for each sample pair by averaging the samples forming a given sample pair.

- Capacitive MEMS accelerometers are traditionally implemented using a variable capacitive structure, and are manufactured using MEMS technology: a process by which micro-mechanical structures are machined into integrated circuit (IC) packages along with other electrical components. These sensors measure acceleration using the displacement of a mass connected to springs. This displacement is translated to a continuous voltage signal.

- accelerometers While specific reference is made to accelerometers, the concepts set forth in this disclosure are applicable to other types of sensors, including gyroscopes, pressure sensors, microphones and other sensors that can be spoofed at a specific resonant frequency.
- FIG. 2 illustrates a typical design of the signal conditioning path in a MEMS accelerometer.
- ADC Analog-to-Digital Converter

- the amplifier and ADC have limitations. Amplifiers have upper and lower bounds; when the input signal exceeds these bounds, signal clipping occurs, and abnormal acceleration readings are reported. Likewise, the ADC has requirements that must be met.
- Analog accelerometers output the analog signals from the amplifier directly, while digital sensors typically contain an LPF and ADC. Analog sensor are used to help understand how acoustic waves interact with the sensing mass-spring structure.
- the attacker is able to induce sound in the vicinity of the victim device, at frequencies in the human audible to ultrasonic range (2-30 kHz). This can be done by applying the sound externally, or by playing sounds from speaker in the vicinity of the target sensors. This might be done via means of remote software exploitation (e.g., remotely affecting the multimedia software in a phone or a car) or by a drive-by ditty where a user is tricked into playing malicious music either by email or a web page with autoplay audio enabled.
- the attacker is also able to synthesize any shape, i.e., varying amplitude and phase, of acoustic signal within the stated frequency range.

- forces from acoustic pressure waves can also displace the mass.

- a model is developed for how an electrical acceleration signal, generated by a capacitive MEMS accelerometer, is distorted by acoustic noise.
- the measured acceleration is modeled as a linear combination of the true acceleration and acoustic acceleration. Namely, for a true acceleration signal s(t), and the acoustic acceleration signal s a (t), the measured acceleration signal ⁇ a (t)

- the acoustic frequency must match the mechanical resonant frequency of the sensor. For the previous experiment, 2.9 kHz was the resonant frequency of the ADXL337.
- the two critical hardware components typically included in a MEMS accelerometer's signal conditioning path are: an amplifier and a low pass filter (LPF), components C and D in FIG. 2 respectively.
- LPF low pass filter
- the accelerometer's LPF is designed with a cut-off frequency that is either above, or too close to the resonant frequency of the sensor.
- the sinusoidal acoustic acceleration signal whose frequency matches the accelerometer's resonant frequency, is not completely attenuated by the LPF. It slips through to the ADC where it is usually under-sampled, as shown in FIG. 6 b.
- the acoustic acceleration signal's frequency is well above the cut-off frequency of the LPF and is completely attenuated.
- the input range of the amplifier is large enough to handle any signal the sensing mass can produce.
- the amplifier is typically chosen to cope with the maximum specified acceleration. This exposes an attack surface. Resonant acoustic interference can displace the sensing mass enough to create a high amplitude acceleration signal that exceeds the dynamic range of the amplifier. Thus, acoustic acceleration signals can potentially be distorted.
- the sensor may report three types of measurements: true measurements and two types of falsified measurements.
- the false sensor measurements are due to insecurities in hardware components, as shown in FIG. 6 .
- the accelerometer's amplifier tolerates the high amplitude acceleration signals generated under resonant acoustic interference, i.e., no signal clipping occurs.
- the accelerometer's resonant frequency is much greater than the LPF's cutoff frequency.
- the LPF attenuates high frequency acoustic acceleration signals.
- the LPF does not completely attenuate high frequency acoustic acceleration signals. Acoustic acceleration signals are under-sampled by the ADC.
- a sensor at rest should measure constant acceleration of 0 g along the X and Y axes and 1 g along the Z axis, accounting for gravity.
- At a given frequency if output measurements deviate from normal, i.e., they are fluctuating or constantly shifted, that frequency is considered a resonant frequency.
- Fluctuating measurements are observable by calculating the standard deviations of multiple samples at each frequency.
- Constant shifted measurements are observable by calculating the means of multiple samples taken at each frequency.
- the experimental setup is identical to the setup in FIG. 4 , absent the vibrating platform. All 40 MEMS accelerometers (both digital and analog) were attached to a table. The experiments were conducted in an acoustic isolation chamber to avoid external acoustic effects. Each sensor was oriented to experience 0 g along the X and Y axes and 1 g along the Z axis, due to gravity. Outside the chamber, a commodity audio amplifier amplified single frequency acoustic signals generated by a function generator. The amplifier drove an off-the-shelf tweeter speaker inside the acoustic chamber. The speaker was suspended 10 cm above the sensor to decouple the sensor from mechanical vibrations emanating from the speaker.
- the speaker was operated near its maximum amplitude, around 110 dB Sound Pressure Level (SPL). To ensure that the speaker produced all sounds at similar SPL, the speaker's frequency response was validated using a measurement microphone with a frequency response of 4 Hz-100 kHz. The speaker's frequency response was relatively flat (at 110 db SPL) across its entire range, from 1.8 kHz to 30 kHz.

- the output biasing attack utilizes sampling deficiencies at the ADC and gives an adversary control over the accelerometer's output for several seconds.
- This attack pertains to accelerometers that experience fluctuating false measurements at their resonant frequencies due to insecure LPFs ( FIG. 6B ).
- an adversary must accomplish two goals: (1) stabilize fluctuating false measurements into constant measurements by shifting the acoustic resonant frequency to induce a DC alias at the ADC; and (2) reshape the desired output signal by modulating it on top of the acoustic resonant frequency.
- the first step can be accomplished through signal aliasing.
- the second step can be realized with signal modulation.
- Aliasing is the misinterpretation of an analog signal caused by digitizing it with an inadequate sampling rate. According to the Nyquist sampling theorem, an analog signal with maximum frequency component F max must be sampled at a minimum rate of 2 ⁇ F max to avoid signal aliasing.
- FIG. 9A illustrates aliasing with a 5 kHz sinusoid and a sampling rate of 1.5 kHz. Reconstructing this signal from the digital samples results in a 500 Hz aliased signal. When the frequency of the analog signal is an integer multiple of the sampling frequency, a constant DC (direct encountered.
- FIG. 9B illustrates this phenomenon with a 4.5 kHz sinusoid sampled at 1.5 kHz.
- Signal modulation is used to transmit arbitrary information signals over another carrier signal.
- the focus is on amplitude and phase modulation, which utilize constant frequency carrier signals.
- Amplitude Modulation consists of varying the amplitude, A, of the carrier signal over time according to the amplitude of the information signal being transmitted.
- FIG. 10A illustrates amplitude modulating a square wave on top of a sinusoidal carrier frequency, f c .
- Phase Modulation consists of varying the phase, ⁇ , of the carrier signal over time according to the amplitude of the information signal being transmitted.
- FIG. 10B illustrates phase modulating a square wave on top of a sinusoidal carrier frequency, f c .
- the output biasing attack is demonstrated by spoofing a MEMS accelerometer to output a signal spelling “WALNUT”.
- converting the fluctuating false measurements into constant false measurements is accomplished by inducing a DC alias of the acceleration signal at the ADC ( FIG. 9B ).
- a DC alias of a periodic analog signal is observed if the analog signal's frequency is an integer multiple of the sampling frequency, F samp .

- the attacker employs either amplitude or phase modulation techniques to further shape the output signal of the accelerometer.
- PM allows an attacker to use the full amplitude of the carrier frequency to modulate the desired signal, where AM utilizes only the upper or lower half of the carrier signal ( FIG. 10 ).
- An attacker must use PM to stimulate an acceleration signal that has both negative and positive components.
- FIG. 9B illustrates that the maximum bias amplitude is reached when samples are taken at the peaks of the acoustically stimulated acceleration signal. The less ⁇ samp drifts over time, the more stable the attack. With some sensors, it is possible to tweak F a so that the DC-aliased output is maintained for up to 30 seconds.

- FIGS. 12A and 12B illustrates the output biasing attack on two analog accelerometers interfaced with accurate ADCs, the ADXL337 and LIS344ALH.
- These analog accelerometers were interfaced with accurate ADCs that took samples at precise time intervals. This made it easier to maintain a consistent DC-aliased output signal for several tens of seconds.
- PM was used to attack both sensors, simply to yield the highest peak-to-peak amplitude possible. Note how the spoofed acceleration signals on sensors with accurate ADCs compares to the spoofed signals on sensors with inaccurate ADCs ( FIG. 12 vs. 11 ).
- the output control attack gives an adversary indefinite full control of an accelerometer's output.
- This attack is applicable to accelerometers that exhibit constant shifted false measurements at their resonant frequencies due to insecure amplifiers ( FIG. 6C ). No signal aliasing at the ADC is needed, since the false output measurements are already stable and constant. This allows an adversary to control the acceleration output indefinitely.
- an adversary need accomplish one goal: reshape the desired sensor output signal by modulating it over the resonant frequency.
- the output control attack was evaluated on all sensors that demonstrated constant false output measurements (mean spikes in FIG. 7 ).
- the same experimental setup shown in FIG. 4 was used, absent the vibrating platform.
- a signal spelling “WALNUT” was amplitude modulated over each sensor's acoustic resonant frequency.
- FIGS. 13A and 13B illustrates the chosen output attack on two accelerometers tested, the MIS2DSH and MPU6500.

- the acoustic resonant frequency of an accelerometer mostly stands apart from its packaging, though the amplitude of acoustic acceleration can be attenuated by packaging.
- the Samsung Galaxy S5 phone contains an MPU6500 accelerometer, a sensor that is vulnerable to the output control attack.
- the phone ran three Android applications from the Google Play store: 1) RC car controlling application (i-Spy Toys), 2) accelerometer monitoring application (Wireless IMU), and 3) an application that played audio files (WavePad Audio Editor).
- the car controlling application polled the orientation state of the accelerometer and sent digital commands to the car over a TCP connection.
- the accelerometer monitoring application sent UDP packets with accelerometer measurements to the computer in real time.
- the audio application played a malicious WAV file that had been pre-loaded on the phone.
- the RC car application monitors and reacts to X-axis acceleration.
- the application When the user tilts the phone flat or upright, i.e. the X-axis acceleration is 0 g or 1 g respectively, the application sends forward or backwards commands to the car.
- the X-axis acceleration When the phone is approximately at a 30° angle, the X-axis acceleration is 0.3 g and the application sends stop commands to the car.
- the phone was placed in an upright position (X-axis aligned with gravity).
- the malicious WAV file contained an AM acoustic interference signal designed to drive the car forward and backward, shown in FIG. 14A .
- the acoustic interference was played over the phone's speaker.
- FIG. 14B shows the X-axis acceleration spoofed by the malicious audio file, and how the RC car reacted.
- a Higi.com account is opened and a Fitbit One device is tethered to the account.
- acoustic interference at the resonant frequency of the Fitbit's accelerometer was played for approximately 40 minutes. No signal aliasing or modulation was needed as simply spoofing fluctuating false measurements was sufficient to register thousands of false steps. In this way, 2,100 steps were registered in that time and 21 rewards points earned on Higi.com without walking a single step.
- Output biasing attacks leverage signal aliasing at the ADC to control the accelerometer's output, a capability that should be suppressed by low pass filtering the analog acceleration signal prior to digitization.
- Low pass filters are designed to pass low frequency signals while blocking high frequency signals. They have three important frequency ranges: 1) pass band, 2) transition band, and 3) stop band. The pass band does not block any frequencies in its range. Frequencies in the transition band are increasingly attenuated, and frequencies in the stop band are completely blocked. The frequency that marks the transition point between the pass band and transition band is known as the cutoff frequency, F cutoff .
- a properly designed analog LPF should have a cut-off frequency of less than half of the ADC sampling rate, i.e.,
- scenario 1 (though this scenario is unlikely) is straightforward: add an LPF.
- scenario 2 is discussed in the following section.
- scenario 3 is the most difficult to address. Designing an LPF that has a transition band that does not overlap the accelerometer's resonant frequency can be accomplished in three ways: 1) lower the cutoff frequency, 2) narrow the transition band, or 3) design the mass-spring sensing structure to exhibit a higher resonant frequency. All three have different limitations. The first lowers the frequency limit of vibrations an accelerometer can measure. The second requires adding many extra components, eventually for little to no added benefit. Finally, the last is possible but requires stiffening the spring and losing sensitivity.
- Output control attacks leverage signal clipping at the amplifier to introduce a DC component into the acceleration signal which slips through any subsequent LPF. This is prevented in two ways: (1) more tolerant amplifier: design an amplifier that can accept the large amplitude inputs that are generated under acoustic interference; and (2) pre-filter amplifier inputs: filter acoustic resonant frequencies prior to the amplifier with another LPF or band-stop filter.
- the first solution is potentially limited by size, power, and cost.
- the second solution, which some designs do employ, is limited by the cost of adding more components, but may not increase power consumption.
- Attenuating acoustic waves before they penetrate sensor packaging can prevent acoustic acceleration signals from being generated at all.
- Surrounding accelerometer ICs with acoustic dampening materials, such as synthetic foam can shield it from acoustic noise.
- the limitation here is size: acoustic dampening foam takes up space, a scarce resource in most embedded systems.
- Redesigning hardware to tolerate acoustic interference is not an option for accelerometers already deployed in the field.
- two different defense mechanisms can be implemented in software and deployed as firmware updates: randomized sampling and 180° out-of-phase sampling.
- These solutions are capable of preventing output biasing attacks, where acoustic acceleration signals have not been distorted by amplifier clipping. They work by eliminating an attacker's ability to achieve a DC signal alias at the ADC.
- Each defense mechanism takes advantage of the requirement that only acoustic resonant frequencies can displace the sensing mass, and that these frequencies are known at design time. For that reason, only sensors that exhibit false fluctuating measurements under resonant acoustic interference are considered.
- Both solutions assume the device has control over the sampling regimes of its sensors, i.e., they employ analog sensors and software controlled ADCs (several microcontrollers allow software to trigger the ADC to take a sample).
- Randomized sampling eliminates the predictability of an ADC's sampling regime. Instead of setting an ADC to sample at a fixed interval, randomized sampling adds a random amount of delay to the beginning of each sampling period. This prevents an attacker from tuning the resonant frequency to induce a DC alias, i.e., step 1 (stabilize) of the output biasing attack. Randomized sampling intentionally amplifies the effect of having an inaccurate ADC. Computing a moving average over several samples then smooths the fluctuating measurements.

Description
- This application claims the benefit of U.S. Provisional Application No. 62/339,292, filed May 20, 2016. The entire disclosure of the above application is incorporated herein by reference.
- The present disclosure relates to methods and systems for protecting motion sensors from intentional acoustic interference.
- With the proliferation of motion-driven applications and microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) technologies, MEMS accelerometers have been widely used in cyber-physical systems, such as implantable medical devices, automobiles, avionics, and even critical industrial systems. These systems deploy layers of software that abstract away hardware details to collect and analyze data provided by sensors, and then autonomously react to sensor data in real time. The software assumes that the underlying hardware is behaving according to specification, and the common practice is to inherently trust the output from sensors. After years of effort towards encouraging better security practices in software, developers are becoming more diligent in hardening software to security vulnerabilities, but fewer methodologies exist in the sensor hardware domain.
- It is already known that acoustic interference can cause denial of service (DoS) attacks against MEMS gyroscopes. Building upon this previous knowledge, this disclosure questions current assumptions about the integrity of sensory data, and specifically explores the data integrity of MEMS accelerometers with a focus of answering the following questions: (1) How can an adversary achieve fine grained control over a sensor's output? (2) How well will system software cope with untrustworthy measurement of motion? (3) How could sensors be designed differently to eliminate the integrity issues? (4) What can be done to protect legacy sensors? Answering these questions is challenging yet critical to securing cyber-physical systems, and the learned insights can guide future design choices and methodologies to mitigate security risks introduced by deploying MEMS sensors in cyber-physical systems.
- MEMS accelerometers have a sensing mass, connected to springs, that is displaced when the sensor is accelerated. Acoustic waves propagate through the air, and exhibit forces on physical objects in their path. If the acoustic frequency is tuned correctly, it can vibrate the accelerometer's sensing mass, altering the sensor's output in a predictable way. To systematically analyze the vulnerabilities of MEMS accelerometers, the impact of acoustic interference on the sensor's entire architecture is modeled, including both the sensing mass and signal conditioning components. Two problematic components are identified in the signal conditioning path of typical MEMS accelerometers (i.e., insecure low-pass filters and insecure amplifiers) that lead to two types of adulterated outputs: fluctuating measurements and constant measurements. These two components not only explain the root cause of DoS attacks but also enable one to design two additional attack classes: sensor output biasing and output control that permit increasing levels of adversarial control over the output of MEMS accelerometers. Of the 20 models of accelerometers tested, experiments show that 75% are vulnerable to output biasing attacks (i.e., insecure low pass filters enable false fluctuating output measurements under acoustic interference), and 65% are vulnerable to output control attacks (i.e., insecure amplifiers enable false constant output measurements under acoustic interference). At the software system level, experiments demonstrate the ease of injecting acoustic interference into an Android smartphone's accelerometer to take control of an app that drives a remote controlled car. The results confirm concerns that system software does not adequately validate the integrity of sensory data—blindly trusting the output of sensors by default.
- Defending against malicious acoustic interference by applying acoustic dampening materials to sensors was previously investigated. Other defense mechanisms exist to thwart sensor-spoofing attacks in scenarios where the actuator and sensor operate in tandem. Other common approaches to deal with signal interference include averaging or filtering. All of these techniques are either impractical (increases packaging size), not applicable (the sensor must operate with an actuator in a closed loop system), or insufficient (cannot filter out all interference) in defending against all proposed acoustic injection attacks. Therefore, two types of defenses are proposed: (1) hardware solutions, whereby the acoustic injection attacks can be eliminated if the MEMS sensors are designed with security in mind, i.e., each component on the signal conditioning path is chosen with larger operation parameters, and (2) software solutions for retroactively protecting vulnerable MEMS accelerometers already deployed in various devices and systems.
- This section provides background information related to the present disclosure which is not necessarily prior art.
- This section provides a general summary of the disclosure, and is not a comprehensive disclosure of its full scope or all of its features.
- A method is provided for determining an output from a motion sensor. The method includes: receiving an output signal from the motion sensor, where the output signal exhibits a known resonant frequency; sampling the output signal at a sampling frequency, where the sampling frequency is less than or equal to the known resonant frequency and sample time for each sample is chosen randomly; and determining an output for the motion sensor by averaging the samples from the output signal. The sampling time is set by adding a random delay to the sampling period. The random delay is preferably uniformly distributed across the resonant period and may be generated using a true random number generator. In the event that the motion sensor exhibits multiple resonant frequencies, the random delay can be uniformly distributed in a period equal to the least common multiple of all resonant frequencies exhibited by the motion sensor.
- A system is also provided for mitigating effects of acoustic interference with output of a motion sensor. The system includes: a motion sensor configured to detect motion of an object; a random number generator configured to generate a random number; and a signal processor in data communication with the motion sensor and the random number generator. The signal processor samples the signal output by the motion sensor at a sampling frequency and generates an output by averaging the samples from the signal, where the sampling frequency is less than or equal to the known resonant frequency and sample time for each sample is chosen randomly.
- In another aspect of this disclosure, a different method is presented for determining an output from a motion sensor. The method includes: receiving an output signal from the motion sensor, where the output signal exhibits a known resonant frequency; sampling the output signal at a frequency twice the known resonant frequency, where every other sample is taken at 180 degrees phase delay with respect to the known resonant frequency and forms a sample pair; and determining an output sample for each sample pair by averaging the samples forming a given sample pair.
- These methods and systems are suitable for different types of sensors including accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors and other types of sensors that can be spoofed at a specific resonant frequency.
