results.full_text:
Abstract
Renewable energy systems are subject to uncertainty arising both from modeling simplifications/imperfections and a stochastic environment. To maximize energy production under this uncertainty, real-time plant and controller adaptability can be introduced. While the inclusion of plant and controller adaptability has been demonstrated to enhance the energetic performance of both wind and marine energy systems, this comes at an economic cost, due to the need for additional actuators and adaptive control software development. In this work, a co-design framework is presented to identify the system design, model refinement technique, and degree of plant and controller adaptability that minimize the expected levelized cost of energy (LCOE) of a renewable energy system. This framework is then applied to a detailed case study involving an installation of marine hydrokinetic (MHK) kites, using a detailed cost model and a computationally efficient surrogate model to estimate energetic performance. The study resulted in identified LCOE-optimal levels of plant and controller adaptability, in addition to an optimal level of model refinement following the plant freeze date (recognizing that this model refinement comes at a development cost). To fully realize the range of adaptation built into the design of the optimized kite system, a real-time extremum-seeking-based adaptation strategy is presented. Through dynamic simulation results, we show that the energetic performance of the kite converges to the predictions generated by the surrogate model, thus validating the use of the surrogate model in the co-design optimization.
1 Introduction
To mitigate the effects of the ongoing global climate crisis, there is an urgent need for energy solutions that are both environmentally sustainable and economically viable.1 Within the next three decades, projections indicate that renewable energy production in the U.S. will double, constituting 42% of the nation's total power generation [1]. To meet the growing demand for renewable energy technology without incurring substantial economic losses, the development of economically feasible renewable energy systems, cost-competitive with fossil fuel alternatives, is necessary.
While both the demand for and prevalence of large-scale renewable energy systems have increased drastically over the last decade, economic optimization of these systems is challenging as a result of strong plant–environment–controller coupling (pointed out in Ref. [2]). This coupling leads the optimal plant and controller parameters to depend strongly on both the environmental energy resource and each other. To consider the plant–controller coupling present in energy-harvesting systems, a large body of literature has focused on co-design of these systems. Within the broader field of co-design, several frameworks have been developed that are tailored to specific classes of energy-harvesting systems. These frameworks can be divided into sequential frameworks (see Ref. [3]), iterative frameworks (see Ref. [4]), nested frameworks (see Refs. [2], [5], and [6]), and simultaneous frameworks (see Ref. [7]), which describe the specific manner by which the plant and control decision variables are considered. Sequential co-design strategies represent “one-shot” optimizations where a controller optimization follows a plant optimization that often accounts for some proxy for how the controller is expected to behave. Iterative frameworks involve an adjustment of the plant design, followed by an adjustment in the control design, followed by a repetition of this cycle. This approach has been used in Ref. [4] to optimize the aerodynamic, structural, and control design of a large-scale wind turbine. Within a nested formulation, a complete controller optimization is performed for every adjustment (iteration) of the plant. This formulation has been applied to the co-design of floating offshore wind turbines in Ref. [5] and to marine hydrokinetic (MHK) kites in Ref. [6]. Finally, a simultaneous framework considers all of the decision variables within a single integrated optimization. This has been applied to a horizontal-axis wind turbine in Ref. [7].
While the aforementioned works apply co-design methodologies to the optimization of energy-harvesting systems, none of the aforementioned studies accounts for uncertainty. Nonetheless, within the co-design community at large, there has been significant recent effort to address the issue of uncertainty. The general issue of uncertainty within a co-design problem has been formalized in Ref. [8]. This work also discusses relevant approaches to solving a narrow subset of uncertain co-design problems. In Ref. [9], robust stability in the co-design of nonlinear systems is addressed when faced with structured uncertainty. In this work, control is leveraged in the context of a nested co-design formulation to ensure satisfaction of stability criteria. Critically, however, prior work in the area of uncertain co-design focuses on robustness (i.e., optimizing a single design that works acceptably across all operating points) rather than adaptation (i.e., optimizing a design that can be adjusted on the fly in order to yield optimal performance over a variety of conditions). For example, Refs. [9] and [10] leverage robust control to maximize worst-case performance over a range of operating conditions. Alternatively, a formulation described in Ref. [8] aims to maximize the performance of a system while reducing the probability of violating constraints to below an allowable threshold, trading off performance for robustness. In all of these cases, a single system is designed. Given that this single system design must achieve acceptable performance over a range of operating conditions, the identified design will necessarily perform as well as (in the best case) or worse than a system designed in the absence of uncertainty for a single operating condition. This motivates the inclusion of adaptability into the design of a system. Integrating adaptive components into the design of a system can enable it to achieve near-optimal or optimal performance in spite of uncertainty, through the ability to make real-time adjustments to plant and controller parameters.
Mathematically, optimizing a plant and controller design for adaptability fundamentally differs from robust co-design in the sense that the decision variables are sets. Through real-time adaptation, the plant and control variables can take on different points within these sets during different points in the operating life of the device. While this bears some relevance to the field of set-based design [11,12], a key difference lies in the fact that set-based design methods have always assumed that the design would ultimately be reduced to a point by the end of the design cycle; with adaptability, the final design is in fact a set. The optimal sizes of the aforementioned sets depend on the level of modeling and environmental uncertainty at the time that the sets are frozen. After all, large amounts of uncertainty will typically motivate larger amounts of adaptability. In the event that model refinement is performed after the level of plant adaptability is frozen but before the controller design is frozen, there exists an opportunity for design refinement between these two dates. It stands to reason (and will in fact be shown in this work) that the level of model refinement committed to after the plant design freeze is in fact important in determining the optimal amount of adaptability. In the context of energy-harvesting systems, the inclusion of plant and controller adaptability has been demonstrated to enhance energetic performance (as applied to energy-harvesting kites in Refs. [13] and [14] and wind turbines in Refs. [15] and [16]). That said, adaptability comes at an economic cost, necessitating additional actuators and adaptive control software development. None of these costs were examined in the aforementioned prior work, in spite of the fact that the economic cost of both model refinement and adaptability are critical drivers in the optimization. Aside from our preliminary work in Ref. [16] (which does not account for the important role that model uncertainty reduction plays in driving the optimal level of adaptability), no co-design framework to-date has examined these considerations in tandem.
This work focuses on developing a co-design methodology that optimizes adaptability, applying this methodology to an MHK energy application, namely, underwater kites. While marine energy sources have immense potential, with an estimated 334 TWh per year of tidal energy [17] and 163 TWh per year of ocean current energy [18] available off the coast of the U.S. alone, this resource remains largely untapped. Challenges to widespread adoption stem from the considerable seabed depths at the most favorable locations (see Ref. [19]), along with the spatiotemporally varying nature of the resource [13]. MHK kites (an example of which is depicted in Fig. 1—left) are a promising technology with the potential to yield competitive energy costs even at locations with more favorable seabed depths but lower flow resources. Specifically, kites use a high lift-to-drag wing to execute periodic orbits (see Fig. 1—right), with flight speeds that are 5–10 times faster than the prevailing flow, thereby delivering significantly more power per unit mass than stationary turbines. Furthermore, kites can adjust their tether length or angle of the tether with respect to the seabed (termed the elevation angle) in order to target optimal resources. Finally, as demonstrated in Refs. [13] and [14], kites can benefit from real-time wing morphing in order to adapt to a changing environment. The case in this work study focuses on underwater kites for three key reasons. First, the performance of an underwater kite is significantly impacted by a variable environment and modeling uncertainty, thereby providing strong motivation for adaptation. Second, given the authors' extensive background in kite design and control, focusing on kites as an application ensures the case study to be well-grounded in the state of the art. Third, focusing on kites facilitates the use of a recently released techno-economic reference model [21] for system cost modeling.
In this work, a framework, which is applicable to any energy-harvesting system for which modeling uncertainty and variable environmental parameters play a significant role in driving performance, is developed to identify the economically optimal level of plant and controller adaptability, along with the correspondingly optimal level of model refinement over the design life cycle. The formulation considers the practical constraints of freeze dates (at which time the plant and control architectures are fixed, but these architectures allow for real-time variation over the course of operation, if they are selected to be adaptive). To demonstrate the efficacy of this framework, we present a case study on MHK kites deployed in the Gulf Stream. In summary, the contributions of this work are as follows:
We present a unique co-design formulation for renewable energy systems, which identifies the techno-economically optimal level of plant and controller adaptability and method of experimental model refinement while considering an uncertain environmental energy resource, an uncertain model parameter, and design freeze dates.
We introduce a computationally efficient surrogate model for characterizing the techno-economic performance of an MHK kite system.
We present a co-design case study applied to the design of a small farm of MHK kites using the proposed co-design framework and the computationally efficient surrogate model.
We present an extremum-seeking-based adaptation strategy to leverage the adaptability built into the optimized kite system design.
We present dynamic simulation results in a realistic operating environment wherein the energetic performance of the dynamic kite model is shown to converge to the performance predicted by the surrogate model.
The remainder of this work is structured as follows. In Sec. 2, the adaptive co-design framework is presented. Following this, the models used to characterize the energetic and economic performance of MHK kite systems are introduced in Sec. 3. In Sec. 4, a case study involving the design of an installation of MHK kites is presented. Following this, in Sec. 5, a real-time adaptation strategy specific to kite systems is presented. Subsequently, in Sec. 6, dynamic simulation results are presented and compared to performance predictions from the surrogate model. Finally, conclusions are discussed in Sec. 7.
2 Adaptive Co-Design Formulation
Here, is the estimated average power generated by the candidate system design, is the probability density function (PDF) of the environmental energy resource (evaluated between and ), f is the PDF of an uncertain model parameter (evaluated between and ), is the predicted power generation, and is the predicted maximal achievable power generation at a single environmental condition and value of the uncertain model parameter for any plant and controller design contained within the “adaptability sets” and ( and , where p and c are the number of plant and controller parameters, respectively). These sets, which are hypervolumes within the space of admissible plant and controller designs ( and ), define the amount of real-time adaptation in plant and control variables (respectively) that is built into the design. When certain plant and control parameters are fixed (nonadaptive), and degenerate into lower-dimensional sets. Thus, without loss of generality, the full design, regardless of the amount (or existence) of adaptability, can be specified based on and . Note that both the environmental variable and the uncertain model parameter described by can be either scalar or vector valued ( and , where n and m refer to the number of parameters needed to describe the environmental energy resource and the number of uncertain model parameters, respectively). If these variables are vectors, the corresponding probability density functions ( and f) become joint density functions (JDFs).
where and are plant and controller adaptability supersets ( and ), within which all admissible plant and controller adaptability sets must lie. Given that the decision variables of this optimization problem are sets, and are parameterized in terms of vector values that describe the limits of these set. Ultimately, the vectors that define these sets are the decision variables of the optimization. In the case study presented in this work, the vectors used to define the plant and controller adaptability sets ( and , respectively) define the upper and lower values of each parameter which can be achieved during real-time operation. As such, the adaptability sets considered in this work are hypercubes. That said, the presented framework is compatible with separate parameterizations of the adaptability sets.
Having introduced the foundational concepts behind the co-design formulation, Fig. 2 illustrates the co-design process that will be evaluated in this work. In this framework, the overall optimization problem described in Eq. (4) is broken down into multiple subproblems, which are evaluated over the course of a design cycle. In particular, the formulation focuses on optimizing two coupled features of the system and design process: the level of plant/controller adaptability and the amount of model refinement to be pursued following the plant freeze date. An assumption is made in this work that, consistent with standard practice, the plant design (comprising the corresponding adaptability set) must be frozen before the controller. It is important to note that this is not equivalent to assuming a so-called sequential co-design process (as described in Refs. [2] and [7]), where the plant and controller parameters are not considered simultaneously within the optimization; in fact, the two sets of parameters are indeed considered simultaneously in our framework, up to the plant freeze date (at which point the plant adaptability set is no longer a decision variable). The coupling between the optimal level of plant/controller adaptability and the amount of model refinement following the plant freeze date may not be immediately apparent, but is illustrated through a simple example in Sec. 2.2. In essence, model refinement that occurs before the controller freeze date allows the controller to be designed with maximal certainty, which often means that less plant adaptability is needed to compensate for the suboptimal controller. On the other hand, if model refinement does not occur before the controller freeze date, then a different (often larger) amount of plant adaptability is required in order to compensate for the fact that the controller design will be finalized under significant uncertainty.
2.1 Proposed Co-Design Framework.
This section details the proposed process for solving the optimization problem posed in Eq. (4) in the context of a realistic design cycle.
2.1.1 Stage 1: Plant Design and Refinement Technique Optimization.
Here, is an integer-valued variable describing the type of analysis that will be conducted between the plant and controller freeze dates (noting that the cost of conducting said analysis, , is added to when calculating LCOE), and is the set of all possible methods of model refinement. Meanwhile, is the controller adaptability set that minimizes the expectation of LCOE for a given plant adaptability set, environmental resource PDF, uncertain model parameter PDF, and degree of future model refinement. Furthermore, represents the minimum expectation of LCOE obtained over all candidate controller adaptability sets for a given plant adaptability set and degree of model refinement. While the value of depends on the environmental and uncertain model parameter PDFs as well as the decision variables at this stage of the co-design optimization ( and R), is expressed as a function of the decision variables alone in subsequent equations for conciseness.
Here, represents the single controller adaptability set that minimizes the expectation of LCOE across the initial distribution of possible parameter values.
Here, is the post-analysis probability density function of the uncertain parameter evaluated at mean with standard deviation . Crucially, without running a specific experimental campaign or analysis, the standard deviation () around the presently unknown observed value of the parameter () is known based on the expected measurement uncertainty associated with the analysis technique. Note that while the case study presented in Sec. 4 considers a single uncertain parameter, generally , where n refers to the number of uncertain parameters. The LCOE-optimal controller adaptability set for a given plant adaptability set, environmental resource PDF, and a specific value of the uncertain parameter is given by . By committing to run analysis in the future, the expected performance of a system can be calculated assuming that the economically optimal controller adaptability set will be selected for each possible value of the uncertain parameter. Notably, regardless of the degree of experimental characterization conducted between the plant and controller freeze dates, an initial estimate of the uncertain parameter PDF is needed at the onset of the co-design process.
2.1.2 Stage 2: Model Refinement.
After identifying the expected LCOE-optimal plant adaptability set and method of model refinement, the selected experiments or analyses are conducted, corresponding to the “stage 2” block in Fig. 2. The PDF of the uncertain parameter is then updated pending the results of the analysis. When running no analysis is expected to minimize LCOE (), this PDF remains unchanged.
2.1.3 Stage 3: Controller Design Optimization.
Following this optimization, the controller adaptability set is frozen.
2.1.4 Stage 4: Real-Time Adaptation Strategy.
After both adaptability sets are frozen, real-time adaptation strategies for both the adaptive plant and controller parameters ( and , respectively) are developed to fully exploit the system's adaptability sets during real-time operation (corresponding to the “stage 4” block in Fig. 2). These adaptation strategies modify the adaptive plant and controller parameters in operation in response to both changes in the operating environment and mismatch between the predicted and true value of the uncertain model parameter (which is not known until the time of deployment). After this step, the system is deployed.
While the co-design framework shown in Fig. 2 may resemble a conventional, sequential co-design framework at first glance, it is important to note that multiple optimizations occur throughout this process. Stage 1 and stage 3 in this framework both represent design optimization processes. Stage 1 comprises a nested co-design optimization which is used to select the plant adaptability set and desired method for model refinement, while stage 3 involves optimization of the controller adaptability set alone, since the plant adaptability set has been fixed at this point. Crucially, these optimizations occur at different points in time within the design cycle. Notably, in the stage 3 optimization, the plant adaptability set has been fixed, leaving only the controller adaptability set to be optimized. Thus, stage 3 does not represent a co-design process but rather represents a further revision/refinement of the controller adaptability set initially identified in stage 1 (prior to any post-plant-freeze model refinement). For clarity, the key variables relevant to this optimization framework, along with their respective domains, are listed in Table 1.

2.2 Illustrative Example of Coupling Between Optimal Adaptability and Degree of Future Model Refinement.
While the relationship between modeling uncertainty and the usefulness of adaptability is rather intuitive, it is perhaps less obvious why the intended future level of model refinement (after the plant adaptability set freeze) would influence the optimal degree of plant adaptability (and, hence, why both are considered within the first step of the co-design process). To understand the inherent coupling between the expected LCOE-optimal adaptability sets and the degree of model refinement, a simple example is presented with a fixed plant design. In this example, there is an uncertain parameter that can take on three possible values—either low, medium, or high—with a one-third chance of each value occurring. Furthermore, an analysis can be conducted at a known cost to identify the true value of the uncertain parameter (either low, medium, or high). After running the analysis in the future, the true parameter value will be known with certainty. In this example, there are three candidate controller adaptability sets corresponding to “architecture A,” “architecture B,” and “architecture C.” As shown in Fig. 3, each architecture performs differently at each potential value of the uncertain parameter. For example, at low uncertain parameter values, architecture A is predicted to generate the lowest expected LCOE of the three architectures. That said, at high uncertain parameter values, architecture A is shown to perform poorly, resulting in the highest expected LCOE of the three architectures. Without committing to run an analysis prior to the controller freeze date, the single control architecture that minimizes LCOE across the initial distribution of possible parameter values is selected. However, by committing to run analysis before the controller freeze date, the control architecture that minimizes LCOE for each possible uncertain parameter value described by f can be selected, resulting in a lower expected value of LCOE, even when considering a nominal cost of conducting experiments. The LCOE PDFs resulting from both cases (running and not running analysis before the controller freeze date) are shown in Fig. 4. In this figure, the expected value of LCOE when committing to run future analysis is shown to be substantially lower than the expected LCOE without running analysis in the future, despite the cost of running said analysis.
The LCOE PDFs shown in Fig. 4 also demonstrate the coupling between the optimal level of plant adaptability to incorporate into the design of a system and the optimal degree of future model refinement. As depicted by the solid curve in Fig. 4, with a single, nonadaptive plant design and without committing to conduct analysis prior to freezing the controller design, the variance in LCOE is high. This variance can be reduced (and the risk of designing a poorly optimized system can be mitigated) by either committing to conduct future analysis at the time of the plant design freeze or incorporating plant adaptability into the design of the system in the absence of future analysis. That said, the benefit of incorporating plant adaptability into the design of a system is diminished when committing to conduct future model refinement, given that the level of controller adaptability can be tailored to a more accurate estimate of the uncertain model parameter, necessitating a lesser degree of plant adaptation.
3 Modeling of Marine Hydrokinetic Kites
Kite systems extract energy from a flow resource via onboard turbines (termed fly-gen operation) or by cyclic spooling using a winch/generator system (termed ground-gen operation). In this work, fly-gen kite systems are considered. In this section, the models used to characterize the performance of a fly-gen MHK kite and the control architectures used to govern its flight are detailed.
3.1 Energetic Performance Modeling.
To characterize the energetic performance of a kite system, two models are considered: a computationally expensive but detailed dynamic model and a computationally cheap quasi-static surrogate model.
3.1.1 Detailed Dynamic Model.
In this dynamic model, a combination of rigid lifting bodies, each subject to forces and moments induced by lift, drag, buoyancy, and gravity, along with a lumped-mass tether, are used to describe the system. Methods from Ref. [22] were used to formulate the dynamics of the system while allowing for the inclusion of added mass terms. Ultimately, this model contains 12 state variables (three linear velocities, three angular velocities, three translational positions, and three Euler angles) for the six degree-of-freedom kite (comprising the vector ). Additionally, six state variables are added for each node in the tether (three positions and three velocities). The control inputs are the aileron, elevator, and rudder deflections (denoted by , , and , respectively).
and where , , and are the , , and direction velocities of the kite, and p, q, and r are the angular velocity components about the , , and axes. In Eq. (17), is the relative velocity vector of the kite, the vector is composed of the forces and moments acting on the kite, the vector characterizes Coriolis terms, and is an inertia matrix that accounts for added mass. The derivation of these terms can be found in Ref. [22]. Readers are referred to Refs. [23] and [24] for a detailed derivation of the forces and moments on the kite.
The tether is modeled as a chain of cylindrical links, which are characterized as noncompressive spring–damper elements, connected by nodes. Each node has three translational degrees-of-freedom and is subject to forces due to the tension, drag, and gravity. The mathematical derivation of these forces is available in Ref. [25].
3.1.2 Surrogate Energetic and Structural Performance Model.
where is an efficiency function used to characterize the ratio of tension generated by the dynamic model over the tension predicted by the quasi-static model, and is the distance from a wing's root to its center of lift. This function is generated similarly to , involving a sweep of offline simulations. Overall, the equations described in this subsection enable efficient computation of Eq. (1) as applied to a kite system.
3.2 Kite Control Architectures.
Control strategies for guiding the flight path of a kite system primarily focus on either tracking periodic roll and yaw setpoints (resulting in figure-eight flight [30,31]) or tracking a prescribed 3D path [32,33]. In this work, three candidate control architectures for guiding a kite system into figure-eight flight are considered. Specifically, the orientation-based, enhanced orientation-based, and path-following control architectures detailed in Ref. [27] are considered. A description of each control strategy, its respective energetic performance, and the associated hardware cost is provided in this subsection.
An orientation-based controller guides a kite to track periodic roll and yaw setpoint trajectories ( and , respectively), relying on an inertial measurement unit to measure the kite's roll and yaw Euler angles ( and , respectively). The orientation errors are converted to desired rolling and yawing moments ( and , respectively) through two parallel proportional–integral– derivative controllers. Using a measurement of the apparent flow speed experienced by the kite (), the control surface deflections needed to achieve the desired rolling and yawing moments are then calculated through a control allocation matrix that considers the effect of each control input on the two moments. A block diagram of this control architecture is shown in Fig. 6. This strategy is relatively cheap to implement, requiring only measurements of the kite's orientation and apparent flow speed. That said, this strategy is energetically low-performing as compared to the other two strategies discussed (as detailed in Ref. [27]). Most significantly, simple orientation-based control does not provide any direct feedback mechanism for driving the kite to a target elevation angle, even though it is well-known that the elevation angle has a significant effect on energetic performance (as can be seen in Eq. (19)). While the sinusoidal roll and yaw setpoint profiles can be tuned to drive the kite to a favorable elevation angle, significant experimentation or online adaptation is typically needed to dial in those setpoint profiles.
Similar to the orientation-based architecture, an enhanced orientation-based controller guides a kite to track periodic roll and yaw setpoint trajectories. Unlike the orientation-based controller, however, a measurement of depth from a relatively cheap and reliable pressure sensor is used to estimate the kite's elevation angle, with the estimate denoted by . Combining this elevation angle estimate with a time-based estimate of the kite's position along the figure-eight flight path, the periodic roll setpoint trajectory is modified to track a desired mean elevation angle setpoint (). The roll and yaw setpoint trajectories and the measured roll and yaw angles are then fed to the same common lower-level controller depicted in Fig. 6 to generate control surface deflections. A block diagram of this flight control strategy is shown in Fig. 7. This strategy is substantially more energetically effective than the orientation-based controller, with only a modest increase in cost associated with incorporating a depth sensor. Furthermore, this strategy facilitates the tracking of a variable elevation angle setpoint, allowing the kite to adjust its altitude above the seabed as the flow environment changes.
The last control strategy considered in this work is a path-following strategy (as depicted in Fig. 8), which uses a four-layer hierarchical controller to guide the kite into tracking a prescribed 3D path [34]. The basis parameters that define a figure-eight flight path are given by the vector and include the mean elevation angle setpoint (), path center azimuth angle (), elevation sweep (), azimuth sweep (), and tether length . The first layer selects a desired velocity angle (), which specifies the desired direction of the kite's velocity vector. The second layer selects a desired tangent roll angle (), which represents the kite's bank angle relative to the instantaneous sphere it is flying on (whose radius is dictated by the tether length), as needed to achieve the desired velocity angle. The third and fourth layers are comprised of the common lower-level controller shown in Fig. 6, taking in the tangent roll error and the hydrodynamic sideslip angle () in place of the roll and yaw errors, to compute the aileron and rudder deflections needed to track the path. While this strategy is highly energetically efficient and enables the tracking of a variable elevation angle setpoint, it is also by far the most costly to implement. To track a 3D path, a direct measurement of the kite's position is needed, which is typically obtained via an ultrashort baseline acoustic sensor. A comparison of the three candidate control strategies, including their associated sensor suite costs for a single kite system (as informed by Refs. [35] and [36]), assuming a sensor lifespan of 15 years, is shown in Table 2.
| Control strategy | Energetic performance | Variable elevation tracking | Sensor suite cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orientation-based | Low | No | $7400 |
| Enhanced orientation-based | Moderate | Yes | $8440 |
| Path-following | High | Yes | $70,440 |
| Control strategy | Energetic performance | Variable elevation tracking | Sensor suite cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orientation-based | Low | No | $7400 |
| Enhanced orientation-based | Moderate | Yes | $8440 |
| Path-following | High | Yes | $70,440 |
3.3 System Cost Model.
To model the total cost associated with developing, operating, and maintaining an MHK kite system, the open-source economic reference model for kite systems described in Ref. [21] was used. This model takes in the physical system design of a kite system, along with the intended control architecture (which informs the cost of the required sensor suite as shown in Table 2), flow speed probability density function, and the anticipated power and loading curves as a function of flow speed, outputting the total expected lifetime system cost (). This is done while considering both the capital costs of the kite system and operating expenses. Capital costs include the cost of structural mass (which depends on the geometric design of the kite), avionics (noting that additional actuators are required for morphing systems), sensors (noting that the required sensor suite depends on the intended control architecture), onboard turbines, and the tether (noting that tether cost varies with tether length) in addition to the costs associated with installing and commissioning a device. Operating costs comprise the cost of replacing sensors (which have a limited operating life) and structural components of the kite system. Given that the kite system is subject to cyclic loading, the calculation of operating expenses considers the cost of replacing the tether and structural components as detailed in Ref. [21]. The frequency at which these components must be replaced varies as a function of the loading they are subjected to as informed by the kite's loading curve. Note that the power and loading curves that this model takes in are representative of kite performance in the structurally constrained power-optimal configuration achievable within the adaptability sets. Crucially, these curves are calculated using the surrogate model described in Sec. 3.1.2, which considers both the physical system design and the selected control architecture.
While this model was originally designed to model the cost of airborne kite systems (the sister technology to MHK kite systems), we have modified the model to include the cost parameters for marine energy systems (e.g., siting and installation costs) detailed in Ref. [37]. Specifically, we consider the turbine, siting, installation, and annual land maintenance costs per unit of installed capacity while also considering the cost of structural mass (per unit mass) as detailed in Ref. [37]. We further modified the model to consider the cost of plant adaptability, detailed in Sec. 4. Combined with the surrogate energetic performance model, this cost model enables direct computation of the expected LCOE of a given system design. As a result, the expected LCOE of a kite system (which constantly operates at the power-optimal points within its adaptability sets) at a site with a characterized flow resource and an uncertain model parameter can be calculated as described in Eq. (3).
3.4 Environmental Modeling.
In this work, an installation site roughly 100 miles east of Fort Lauderdale, FL, is considered. This site was selected for its relatively consistent and high-speed ocean flow resource. The ocean current speed at this site was characterized in Ref. [38] using daily flow speed measurements captured by gliders and taken at a range of altitudes above the seabed (with a resolution of 10 m). Using a year's worth of data, we generated Weibull distributions to describe the PDF of flow speed at each altitude above the seabed. A PDF of flow speed can be generated for any altitude above the seabed by interpolating between the Weibull parameters associated with each discrete altitude interval. The PDFs of flow speed at several altitudes above the seabed are shown in Fig. 9, noting that the seabed depth at this site is 275 m.
3.5 Parametric Modeling Uncertainty.
As discussed in Refs. [13], [14], and [39], the power-optimal wing design for a kite system is sensitive to the level of drag contributed by the tether. As can be seen in Eq. (19), a kite's power generation is proportional to . At high values of the tether drag coefficient, when tether drag comprises the majority of , small changes in the drag contributed by the wings will have a minimal impact on the total drag of the system. As such, (and therefore power) is maximized by maximizing lift at the cost of added wing drag with a large, low aspect ratio wing. The opposite is true at low values of the tether drag coefficient, where drag from the wings dominates . Here, an efficient, high aspect ratio wing maximizes power generation. By incorporating plant adaptability into the design of a kite, enabling it to modify its wing geometry in operation, the system is made robust to uncertainty in the tether drag coefficient.
From experience conducting experimental campaigns involving underwater kites [14,26,27,30], the authors have observed that the effective drag coefficient of the tether varies substantially based on both the design of the kite system and the flow conditions experienced by the kite. This variation is induced by a fluid dynamic effect known as strumming, high-frequency vibrations of a loaded cable in cross flow [40]. As a result of strumming, the tether oscillates rapidly, resulting in significant variations in the experienced Reynolds number and effective drag coefficient of the tether. To reflect uncertainty in the tether drag coefficient, a Gaussian PDF of the expected tether drag coefficient prior to freezing the plant design was constructed (depicted in Fig. 10). The mean and standard deviation of this distribution are reflective of the values observed in past experimental campaigns. Note that this PDF represents the function f in Eq. (1), where represents .
After freezing the plant design of a system, experiments can be conducted to better characterize the drag coefficient of the tether at an economic cost. Specifically, the drag coefficient of the tether at a range of flow conditions under the loading that corresponds to the selected plant design can be identified experimentally by mounting the cable to the sea floor, as was done in Ref. [41]. A Gaussian distribution was fit to time-series estimates of tether drag coefficient presented in Ref. [41] (normalized by the mean measured tether drag coefficient) under a single loading condition. The standard deviation of this distribution () represents the measurement uncertainty around a nominal value of tether drag coefficient that this analysis will result in. Furthermore, given that the experimental setup and instrumentation used in Ref. [41] were specified, we estimated the cost of running this set of experiments (using equivalent instrumentation costs from Refs. [42] and [43]).
While the aforementioned analysis technique provides a high-fidelity estimate of the tether drag coefficient, it is quite costly to conduct the requisite experiments. A less expensive alternative is to estimate the drag coefficient via force and moment testing in a wind tunnel. While wind tunnel testing is significantly cheaper, it introduces another source of uncertainty to the estimate of the drag coefficient. While it is possible to achieve Reynolds similarity between a stationary tether experiencing cross flow in a wind tunnel as compared to a tether in a marine environment, this similarity cannot be maintained when the cable oscillates. Knowing that the drag coefficient of a taut tether varies with Reynolds number (detailed in Ref. [41]), an additional uncertain parameter was considered, . This term represents the standard deviation of as a function of Reynolds number as characterized in Ref. [41]. Assuming a Gaussian distribution in the Reynolds number of the tether experienced during wind tunnel testing and the same measurement uncertainty experienced when conducting ocean testing, we calculated the post-analysis uncertainty in tether drag coefficient associated with wind tunnel testing () as . The cost of conducting wind tunnel testing was calculated based on the guidelines provided in Ref. [44]. The overall cost and measurement standard deviation associated with each analysis technique are shown in Table 3.
4 Marine Hydrokinetic Kite Adaptive Co-Design Case Study
4.1 Co-Design Problem Formulation.
To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed co-design framework while leveraging models described in Sec. 3, we conducted a realistic case study involving the development of a small farm of MHK kites. This case study identifies the LCOE-optimal kite design to be deployed in a small farm of twenty 50 kW kites at a site near the eastern coast of FL. Here, a kite capable of modifying its chord length in operation (as detailed in Ref. [14]) is considered. The plant adaptability set is comprised of a fixed tether length (), wing span (S), root chord length (c), and a variable range of chord length extension (). Note that refers to the maximal additional fraction of the root chord length that can be achieved at full chord extension (e.g., with m and , the minimal achievable root chord length is 2 m and the maximal achievable root chord length is 3 m). When the allowable range of chord extension is nonzero, the plant adaptability set is a line with length equal to the range of chord extension. If the selected range of chord adjustment is set to zero, the plant adaptability set degenerates into a single point. To consider the cost of plant adaptation via chord morphing, the model described in Sec. 3.3 was modified. The cost of plant adaptability is calculated by adding the cost of the additional wing mass needed to extend the chord length and the cost of the actuators required to extend the wing's chord in real-time. This cost is then added to the system's total hardware cost. Crucially, any nonzero level of plant adaptability incurs the cost of additional actuators. Note that while chord length is assumed to be adjustable over the entire wing span, a constant taper ratio is maintained. A graphical depiction of a candidate system design is shown in Fig. 11.
The controller adaptability set is comprised of the flight control architecture (), which is selected from one of three candidate control strategies described in Sec. 3.2, and a range of elevation angle setpoints () for the enhanced orientation-based and path-following controllers. Here, C corresponds to an orientation-based controller, C corresponds to an enhanced orientation-based controller, and C corresponds to a path-following controller. While some of the candidate control strategies enable tracking of a variable elevation angle setpoint, others only allow a single, fixed elevation angle to be maintained. Like the plant adaptability set, the controller adaptability set is either a line with length equal to the range of achievable elevation angles associated with each control strategy or a point corresponding to operating at a fixed elevation angle. Fundamentally, although it has a profound impact on energetic performance, elevation angle is not directly adaptable through orientation-based control. Instead, the parameters that describe the orientation setpoint trajectories must be adapted. While such a process has seen some success in Ref. [30], a fundamental assumption of the co-design optimization is that an adaptation strategy can be developed to quickly drive the system to the configuration within its adaptability sets that maximizes power generation. This assumption cannot be satisfied when pairing an online adaptation strategy with an orientation-based flight controller. Finally, the tether drag coefficient () is an uncertain parameter in this case study. While the drag coefficient of each tether link can be specified in the dynamic model described in Sec. 3, the physical value of this parameter is unknown before the time of deployment. That said, after the plant freeze, experiments can be conducted at an economic cost to better characterize the true value of the tether drag coefficient before selecting the controller adaptability set. The fixed plant, control, and economic parameters are listed in Table 4, while the decision variables in this case study are listed in Table 5.

4.2 Adaptive Co-Design Case Study Results.
Using the energetic, cost, environmental, and tether drag models discussed in Sec. 3, the full co-design optimization depicted in Fig. 2 was conducted. The results of the first stage of the optimization (wherein the plant adaptability set is frozen along with the method of model refinement) are shown in Fig. 12. Committing to conduct wind tunnel testing following the plant freeze was shown to minimize the expected value of LCOE as compared to ocean testing and no testing at all. Interestingly, when committing to run analysis following the plant freeze, the expected optimal level of plant adaptability decreases. This is because running analysis in the future enables the size of the controller adaptability set to be tailored to a narrower distribution in tether drag coefficients, necessitating less adaptability in the plant design.
To demonstrate the expected economic benefit of introducing adaptability into the system, the same optimization was conducted in the absence of adaptability. Specifically, the LCOE-optimal system design was identified in the absence of plant adaptability (but including controller adaptability), in the absence of controller adaptability (but including plant adaptability), and in the absence of both plant and controller adaptability. As shown in Fig. 13, the expected LCOE in the absence of adaptability is considerably higher than it is when considering any form of real-time adaptability. Furthermore, including both plant and controller adaptability individually reduced the expected LCOE, though incorporating both simultaneously resulted in the lowest expected LCOE. Here, plant adaptability primarily serves to improve expected performance when considering an uncertain tether drag coefficient. At the time of deployment, if the tether drag coefficient is observed to be higher than initially expected, a large, high-lift wing is needed to maximize power generation, agnostic to the environmental conditions. The opposite is true if the tether drag coefficient is observed to be lower than predicted at the time of deployment, where a slender, efficient wing is desirable. At the same time, controller adaptability improves performance when considering an uncertain operating environment by enabling the system to continuously operate at the point in the flow column where the greatest amount of power can be extracted. When considering uncertainty within both the tether drag coefficient and the flow environment, incorporating both plant and controller adaptability minimizes the system's LCOE.
After identifying and freezing the plant design, it was necessary to conduct the expected LCOE-optimal model refinement technique, wind tunnel testing. To simulate the effects of running this characterization, a random value was sampled from the initial tether drag coefficient distribution shown in Fig. 10. This value represents the nominal tether drag coefficient identified during wind tunnel testing. As a result of measurement uncertainty, the exact value of remains unknown after conducting this analysis. Instead, the PDF f is updated to be centered at the nominally measured value of , , with a standard deviation of . In this instance, was identified, a value substantially higher than the initial mean value of 1.2.
Using the frozen plant adaptability set and the updated tether drag coefficient PDF, the LCOE-optimal controller adaptability set was identified as shown in Fig. 14. Here, the path-following controller was found to minimize the expectation of LCOE. Given that the mean tether drag coefficient was higher than initially expected, the LCOE expectations at this stage are higher than in the first stage of the optimization. With a high value of , a high-cost, high-performing control strategy was selected. Intuitively, with a high-drag tether, a kite system will generate less power and will thus seldom generate rated power when using a low-cost, low-performing control strategy like orientation-based control. Alternatively, with a low-drag tether, the kite's rated power can be generated for a large fraction of the time regardless of the performance of the control strategy. By running experimental characterization after freezing the plant design but before freezing the controller design, it was possible to tailor the control architecture to the specific tether drag coefficient observed experimentally.
5 Real-Time Adaptation Strategy
Here, is a running integer-count of the total number of figure-eight laps completed, is the amplitude of the altitude dither signal, is the number of laps per altitude dither period, is the altitude learning gain, and and are the start and end times of dither half-period n, respectively. In this adaptation scheme, the log of average power (as opposed to average power) is maximized, as was done in Ref. [46], to improve convergence times when operating over a wide range of flow speeds.
Here, is the amplitude of the chord length dither signal, is the number of laps per chord length dither period, is the chord learning gain, and and are the start and end times of dither half-period m, respectively.
This adaptation strategy updates in the iteration (figure-eight lap) domain, not the time domain, allowing the kite to complete a specified number of figure-eight orbits prior to each update. A block diagram of the overall adaptation strategy is shown in Fig. 15.
6 Dynamic Simulation
In the energetic surrogate model discussed in Sec. 3, it is assumed that the kite will always operate at the point within its adaptability sets that maximizes power generation for a given flow column. To validate that this assumption is approximately achievable in real-time operation (and to verify the accuracy of the surrogate model in predicting energetic performance and loading), dynamic simulations were conducted using the dynamic model introduced in Sec. 3 and the plant and controller adaptability sets identified in Sec. 4. The results of these simulations were then benchmarked with predictions from the surrogate model. In these simulations, flow data from the installation site were used as shown in Fig. 16. Given that the flow environment at this site changes on the time scale of weeks, simulations were conducted at two distinct dates corresponding to and days in Fig. 16. The adaptation controller parameters used in this simulation are described in Table 6.

| Altitude dither period | 20 | Laps | |

| Chord dither period | 4 | Laps | |
| Chord learning gain | 1 |
| Variable | Description | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|

Power generated by the kite in the dynamic simulations and power predictions from the surrogate model are shown in Fig. 17. Similarly, the wing root bending load experienced by the kite in the dynamic simulations and loading predictions from the surrogate model are shown in Fig. 18. The altitude and root chord length setpoints, along with the optimal parameter estimates, are shown in Fig. 19 for the simulation initialized at days and Fig. 20 for the simulation initialized at days. As shown in Figs. 19 and 20, the chord length setpoint quickly converged to the maximum allowable chord length. Given that the drag coefficient of the tether is higher than initially anticipated, it is energetically optimal to operate with a larger, less lift-efficient wing as described in Sec. 3.5, irrespective of the flow environment. While the same optimal chord length was identified in both simulation cases, the altitude setpoints converged to two distinct values. At days, the flow speed increases sharply with altitude near the surface. As such, power is maximized by operating at the highest allowable altitude, which the extremum-seeking controller converged to quickly. At days, the flow environment is vastly different. At this time, the peak flow speed occurs at an altitude above the seabed of roughly 225 m, decreasing gradually for altitudes above this point. As shown in Fig. 20, the altitude setpoint converged to this altitude. Even when initialized at the same root chord length and altitude setpoints, the extremum-seeking adaptation controller identified the distinct, power-optimal configuration in both cases.
After converging to the power-optimal root chord length and altitude setpoints, the average power and loading in dynamic simulations closely agreed with the predictions from the surrogate model, validating the assumptions made by the surrogate model and justifying its use in the co-design optimization. Notably, while the surrogate model accurately predicted the average power generated by a kite over a figure-eight lap, it did not account for the variations in power generation over a single figure-eight orbit, which are a result of the kite gaining speed on the straightaways of the figure-eight and slowing down on the curved edges of the path. To account for this when deploying a farm of devices, the phase of each kite system along its flight path can be shifted to create a consistent overall power output.
7 Conclusions
In this work, we presented a unique, adaptive co-design framework intended to minimize the expected LCOE of a renewable energy system. This co-design framework identifies the nominal system design, level of plant and controller adaptability, and degree of experimental model refinement that minimize the expected levelized cost of energy of a renewable energy system. To evaluate the efficacy of this framework, this framework was applied to a detailed case study involving an installation of marine hydrokinetic kites at a site off the coast of FL, using a detailed cost model and a computationally efficient surrogate model to estimate energetic performance. Through this process, the optimal wing geometry, tether length, range of real-time adjustable chord length, and flight control architecture were identified. Furthermore, the expected LCOE-optimal degree of experimental model refinement was identified to account for uncertainty in the tether drag coefficient, noting that this experimentation came at an economic cost. Through this case study, a sophisticated (but costly) path-following control architecture combined with a large range of real-time achievable chord lengths was found to minimize the system's expected LCOE for the selected installation site and initial degree of uncertainty in the tether drag coefficient. Furthermore, the expected benefit of experimentally refining the uncertain system model after freezing the plant design but prior to selecting the controller design was found to outweigh the cost of running such an analysis. To fully leverage the adaptability sets identified in the co-design optimization, we developed a real-time extremum-seeking-based adaptation strategy to identify the optimal elevation angle setpoint and chord length while operating in a spatiotemporally varying flow column. Using this strategy, dynamic simulations were conducted wherein the energetic performance of the kite was shown to converge to the predictions generated by the surrogate model, thus validating the use of the surrogate model in the co-design optimization. This case study serves to validate the proposed co-design framework. By examining the uncertain co-design problem through the lens of adaptation as opposed to robustness, this framework has the potential to decrease the LCOE of renewable energy systems, incentivizing widespread adoption of sustainable energy.

results.full_text:
HEMA: A Hands-on Exploration Platform for MEMS Sensor Attacks
Abstract
Automotive safety and security are paramount in the rapidly advancing landscape of vehicular technology. Building safe and secure vehicles demands a profound understanding of automotive systems, particularly in safety and security. Traditional learning approaches, such as reading materials or observing demonstrations, often fail to provide the practical, hands-on experience essential for developing this expertise. For novice users, gaining access to automotive-grade systems and mastering their associated hardware and software can be challenging and overwhelming. In this paper, we present a novel, affordable, and flexible exploration platform, HEMA, that enables users to gain practical, hands-on insights into the security compromises of micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) sensors, a critical component in modern ADAS systems. Furthermore, we discuss the unique challenges and design considerations involved in creating such a platform, emphasizing its role in enhancing the understanding of automotive safety and security. This framework serves as an invaluable resource for educators, researchers, and practitioners striving to build expertise in the field.
Index Terms:
Automotive Security, Exploration Platforms, MEMSI Introduction
The landscape of automotive systems has undergone a rapid and transformative evolution, primarily driven by advancements in autonomous vehicles and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). These cutting-edge technologies aim to improve safety, enhance driving efficiency, and redefine the overall user experience. At the heart of these innovations are sensors and actuators, which enable real-time data collection, processing, and execution of critical vehicle functions. Components like cameras, radar, LiDAR, and Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) work in tandem with actuators to provide vehicles with the capability to sense their environment and make informed decisions. However, this increasing reliance on digital systems has also introduced a significant vulnerability: the heightened risk of cyber-attacks. Modern vehicles, now functioning as interconnected systems with embedded communication and control networks, are susceptible to malicious exploitation. For instance, a compromised IMU sensor, which is critical for navigation and stability, can severely disrupt a vehicle’s functionality, potentially leading to unsafe driving conditions. This underscores the alarming susceptibility of automotive systems to cyber threats, where even a single compromised component can jeopardize the integrity of the entire system.
Prior research [1, 2, 3, 4] has shed light on various cyber-attack vectors targeting automotive components, particularly IMU MEMS sensors, which are central to the operation of autonomous and semi-autonomous vehicles. These investigations reveal the intricate methods used to disrupt the functionality of these sensors, from electromagnetic interference (EMI) to deliberate spoofing and signal injection attacks. Despite these significant contributions, much of the existing research has been conducted by cybersecurity experts and requires a deep understanding of advanced concepts, tools, and methodologies. Consequently, the broader community, including engineers, developers, and stakeholders who are not cybersecurity specialists, often finds it challenging to grasp and simulate these attacks effectively. For novice users and non-specialists, the steep learning curve associated with understanding and replicating sensor-based cyber-attacks creates a barrier to furthering research and awareness in this domain. Addressing this gap is critical to ensuring that the knowledge of automotive cybersecurity vulnerabilities and defenses is accessible, not only to experts but also to a wider audience, fostering a collaborative approach to building safer automotive systems.
MEMS sensors consist of a micromechanical comb-like structure that forms a capacitor. Changes in motion cause this structure to change capacity, which is measured to deduce acceleration. Exploitations on these sensors include causing the structure to artificially vibrate from the acoustics of a speaker, which leads to false readings. Several research works have been done on MEMS sensor attacks [5]. Trippel et al. [1] investigated analog acoustic injection attacks on capacitive MEMS accelerometers, revealing vulnerabilities that enable adversarial control of sensor outputs and proposing software defenses to mitigate these risks. Gao et al. [2] developed the KITE attack, demonstrating stable and practical acoustic transduction threats to inertial sensors in multi-degree-of-freedom systems like drones. Hong et al. [3] demonstrated the stealthy acoustic attacks on MEMS gyroscopes in vehicle ESP systems and proposed a neural network-based defense strategy validated through Carsim-Simulink co-simulation.
Similarly, there have been several exploration and educational platforms for automotive security. AutoHAL, designed for ultrasonic sensors [6] by Ravi et al. and [7] by Carter et al.; HAWA [8], which focuses on wheel speed sensor attacks; VeCAEP, developed for V2X systems by Prakash et al. [9]; and platforms for computer vision modules [10] by Chamarthi et al.. These platforms have demonstrated the versatility and scalability of the HELP architecture, addressing a wide range of cybersecurity and safety challenges. Kabir et al. [11] presented ViSE, a digital twin platform that facilitates the exploration of automotive functional safety and cybersecurity. However, there is no dedicated exploration and educational platform targeting MEMS sensors.
To bridge this gap, we propose an exploration platform HEMA (Hands-on Exploration Platform for MEMS Sensor Attacks) focused on MEMS sensor attacks. This platform is designed to equip users with limited expertise in automotive cybersecurity with the practical knowledge and hands-on experience needed to understand and address these challenges. The primary goal of this platform is to provide an interactive environment for users to intuitively explore vulnerabilities, enhancing their understanding of cybersecurity threats in modern vehicles. As part of the HELP series, it aims to advance research, foster innovation, and support education in automotive safety and security. The HEMA setup uniquely offers a hands-on approach to teaching cybersecurity compromises in MEMS sensors.
II HEMA Approach
MEMS-based wheel speed sensors provide real-time data to the vehicle’s ECU, enabling precise monitoring of each wheel’s rotation. In ABS, this data helps prevent wheel lock-up by modulating brake pressure for better traction and control. In the stability control systems, the sensors work with gyroscopes and accelerometers to detect skidding or instability, allowing selective braking and power adjustments to maintain vehicle control. HEMA allows users to observe behavior based on various parameters the user inputs. Fig. 1 shows the overview of HEMA’s architecture. It includes the following components:
II-A Victim Vehicle
The victim vehicle serves as the target for the attacks, allowing researchers to observe and analyze its behavior under compromised conditions. For example, the vehicle in a benign scenario would follow a trajectory defined by its parameters. The vehicle consists of a microcontroller that manages the vehicle’s longitudinal and lateral motions. A MEMS sensor located in the middle of the chassis measures movement and maintains the desired speed and heading. Any discrepancy in the functioning of the MEMS sensor leads to deviation from the desired behavior. There is also a wheel speed sensor that continuously corrects the drift error from the MEMS sensor and provides ground truth information about the kinematics.
II-B Attacker
It enables users to generate malicious audio signals specifically targeting onboard MEMS sensors. Research has demonstrated that MEMS sensors can be compromised through acoustic attacks, either via internal agents, such as a multimedia speaker within the system, or external agents, such as a speaker or tweeter mounted on an external entity near the vehicle. The tool provides users with the flexibility to select the type of attacker they wish to simulate, whether internal or external. Additionally, users can customize the attacker’s parameters to fine-tune the attack, allowing for precise control over the nature and intensity of the threat.
II-C Platform Controller
The central coordinator between the victim vehicle and the attacker is served by the platform controller. It allows users to modify vehicle parameters such as speed and heading, as well as attacker parameters like audio frequency and trigger rate. The controller includes a GUI (see Fig. 2) where users input these parameters and provide a feedback section displaying quantified data about the vehicle’s behavior, enabling evaluation of the attack’s outcome and effectiveness. Additionally, the controller captures malicious data from the MEMS sensor and compares it with ground truth data from the wheel speed sensor, offering initial insights into the attack’s impact. It also records the dynamic behavior of the vehicle, helping users understand the overall outcome. Advanced users have the option to delve deeper by modifying the software program, enabling them to craft and explore more complex attacks creatively.
The HEMA platform is designed to meet these requirements by providing users with effective feedback during failed attempts while encouraging continued exploration of the attack. It allows users to experiment with a wide range of parameter values, including those that may not lead to a successful security compromise or produce well-defined behavior in the target vehicle. Moreover, the platform offers extensive configurability and extensibility, making it adaptable for a variety of attacks and reusable for diverse cybersecurity scenarios. This flexibility ensures that the HEMA platform fosters an interactive and iterative learning environment, enabling users to gain deeper insights into automotive security challenges.
III Use Case Scenario
The HEMA platform demonstrates its utility through a variety of use case scenarios, focusing on both benign and attack conditions. These scenarios allow users to understand the dynamics of vehicle behavior under different operational states and evaluate the impact of malicious interference on system performance. Fig. 3 shows the physical implementation. Below, we outline a representative use case to illustrate the HEMA platform’s capabilities.
III-A Benign Scenario: Stable Vehicle Operation
In a benign scenario, the vehicle operates as intended, following a predefined trajectory and maintaining a constant velocity as set by the user in the GUI. The user specifies the desired speed, and the vehicle accelerates until it reaches this target velocity. A PID controller ensures that the vehicle maintains this velocity by using feedback from the onboard Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU).
III-A1 Operational Flow
The user inputs the desired speed (e.g., 50 km/h) via the GUI. The vehicle accelerates to the target speed and stabilizes using the feedback loop from the IMU. The IMU continuously measures the vehicle’s motion parameters, including velocity and heading, and sends this information to the control loop. If there are no external disturbances, the vehicle maintains its trajectory and velocity without deviations.
III-A2 Outcome
The vehicle performs smoothly, with stable longitudinal and lateral motion. The interaction between the MEMS sensor, the wheel speed sensor, and the PID controller ensures precise kinematic control. The wheel speed sensor serves as a secondary validation mechanism, ensuring ground truth data about the vehicle’s behavior matches the IMU feedback.
III-B Attack Scenario: Disrupted Vehicle Operation
In an attack scenario, a malicious actor generates acoustic interference to target the onboard MEMS sensor. The attacker’s parameters, such as audio frequency and intensity, are configured via the GUI. These attacks compromise the integrity of the IMU data, resulting in abnormal vehicle behavior.
III-B1 Operational Flow
The attacker launches a malicious acoustic signal, simulating interference from an external source (e.g., a speaker positioned near the vehicle) or an internal source (e.g., an embedded multimedia speaker). The IMU begins to produce false data due to the acoustic attack, causing incorrect velocity feedback to the control loop. The vehicle experiences erratic motion, such as jerks, non-uniform acceleration, or deviation from the desired trajectory.
III-B2 Impact
The vehicle’s motion becomes unstable, failing to maintain the user-defined velocity. Erroneous IMU data leads to inappropriate control decisions, affecting the overall driving performance. The wheel speed sensor detects discrepancies between its readings and the IMU data, but without effective countermeasures, the attack impacts the vehicle’s operational safety.
III-C Future Enhancements
The HEMA platform will incorporate sensor fusion to detect tire slip by comparing data from the IMU and wheel speed sensor.
III-C1 Benign Condition
Accurate detection triggers the ABS to prevent wheel lock-up, maintain traction, and ensure optimal stopping distance.
III-C2 Attack Condition
Acoustic interference on the IMU leads to false readings, degrading ABS performance and increasing stopping distance. To address limitations in simulating real-world tire slip, future upgrades will include a larger-scale vehicle with sufficient inertia, enabling realistic tests of ABS performance and skidding behavior.
IV Educational and Training Framework
The HEMA platform is designed not only as a tool for exploring MEMS sensor attacks but also as a valuable resource for education and professional training. Its versatility and hands-on approach make it an ideal candidate for integration into academic curricula, industry workshops, and certification programs focused on automotive security.
IV-A Academic Integration
HEMA can be integrated into undergraduate and graduate courses on automotive systems, embedded systems, and cybersecurity. It offers a hands-on complement to theory, allowing students to simulate real-world scenarios and study MEMS sensor vulnerabilities in critical automotive systems.
The platform can also support capstone or final-year projects, enabling students to explore attack strategies, mitigation techniques, or extend HEMA’s functionality to other subsystems like radar or LiDAR.
IV-B Industry Training
HEMA can be used to conduct targeted workshops aimed at upskilling industry professionals who may have expertise in automotive system design but lack experience in cybersecurity. By simulating MEMS sensor attacks, the platform bridges the gap between system functionality and security considerations.
Professionals can use HEMA to explore and mitigate attack scenarios relevant to their specific domain, such as ADAS or autonomous vehicles, thereby enhancing their understanding of how to secure modern automotive systems.
IV-C Collaboration with Institutions
Educational institutions and industry leaders can collaborate to deploy HEMA in training programs, tailoring it to address region-specific challenges or emerging trends in automotive security. HEMA can serve as a platform for collaboration among researchers, students, and professionals, encouraging the exchange of ideas and best practices in automotive safety and security.
V Conclusion
MEMS sensors in the automotive field play a pivotal role in providing critical data for safety, stability, and performance, serving as the backbone for systems like ABS, ESP, and ADAS. These sensors enable real-time monitoring and control by measuring parameters such as acceleration, angular velocity, and vibration, which are essential for maintaining vehicle functionality and ensuring passenger safety. However, their integration into increasingly connected automotive systems also exposes them to potential security vulnerabilities. In this paper, we present a platform, HEMA, designed to enable users to explore and understand attacks on MEMS sensors. HEMA serves as an educational and training tool, offering a hands-on approach to automotive security. It is particularly valuable in two scenarios: 1) teaching students who are new to automotive systems and security concepts, and 2) training industry professionals who are familiar with automotive system functionality but may lack expertise in cybersecurity. By allowing users to simulate various attack scenarios, HEMA provides an interactive learning environment that bridges the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application.
In future work, we plan to extend HEMA to facilitate the exploration and analysis of more sophisticated attack vectors, such as signal injection, acoustic interference, and electromagnetic attacks. Future iterations will incorporate simulations of the dynamic consequences of these attacks, providing insights into how they impact real-world vehicle behavior, such as stability control, braking, and navigation systems.

results.title: Protecting motion sensors from acoustic injection attack
results.snippet: This work investigates how analog acoustic injection attacks can damage the digital integrity of a popular type of sensor: the capacitive MEMS accelerometer.
